incident__event__summary: On May 2, 2024, during 5-year recertification load testing of the Forward Starboard knuckle boom crane (KBC #1) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a winch brake assembly failure occurred at approximately 70 metric tons while attempting to reach the full load test of 93.5 MT. The failure resulted in uncontrolled descent of a water bag test load, causing a Loss of Primary Containment (LOPC) due to hydraulic test port failure. The water bag contacted the take-on station corner, ripped open, and descended into the Gulf of Mexico. Property damage totaled $168,150. Root cause was determined to be crane overload due to improper sea lift area designation combined with lack of personnel training on Automatic Overload Protection System (AOPS) functionality and inadequate procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Reset sea lift area when performing 5-year load test over the side of the vessel; Provide proper training on AOPS functionality to personnel performing load tests; Revise job instructions to include safety system checks before load test, not after; Improve maintenance procedures for brake overhaul and installation to ensure Quality Control checks; Update work task references and load test procedures to specify sea lift area reset requirements

incident__event__top_event: Traveling block struck crown saver assembly
incident__event__summary: On July 8, 2024, at 1908 hours, while pulling out of the hole with drill pipe on the jack-up Enterprise 205 at South Timbalier Block 23, the traveling block struck the crown saver assembly with approximately 117,000 lbs overpull, resulting in $11,210 in damages to the crown saver and crown-o-matic. The driller lost situational awareness by focusing on pipe tally while pulling, and the crown-o-matic failed to trip when contacted by the drill line because the barrel was not set properly. The incident was not immediately reported to BSEE.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop rig-specific procedure for checking the crown-o-matic with step-by-step instructions; Ensure Job Safety Analysis identifies the hazard of traveling block striking crown block assembly; Implement requirement for crew member to monitor wire rope and count wraps during crown-o-matic check; Ensure proper setup and maintenance of crown-o-matic barrel; Improve communication of job instructions for pulling out of hole operations; Establish fleetwide procedure for checking crown-o-matic

incident__event__top_event: Head injury from winch assist handle strike
incident__event__summary: On July 11, 2024, at 0730 hours, a contract pipeline operator from Quality Process Services suffered a serious head injury when struck by a winch assist handle during a routine lifeboat capsule inspection at the High Island A-5 Manta Ray Gathering Facility. The handle remained in the socket when the powered winch was engaged, causing it to rotate and strike the operator in the head. The operator sustained a fractured skull requiring surgery and was transported via USCG to UTMB in Galveston. The incident was caused by failure to remove the winch assist handle before engaging the powered winch, compounded by a bypassed safety device and lack of a Job Safety Analysis.
incident__event__recommendations: Conduct onsite training for all operators on proper winch operation and safety protocols; Develop and implement a Job Safety and Health Analysis (JSHA) for routine tasks including winch operations; Review and update operational procedures and checklists to ensure all necessary safety measures are included; Ensure winch assist handle removal is a mandatory step before powered winch engagement

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Ship Shoal Block 189
incident__event__summary: On March 13, 2024, at approximately 20:10 hours, a 4x40' basket of plug and abandon tools weighing 13,000 pounds was being offloaded from the M/V GOL Cowboy to the upper cantilever of the jack-up Enterprise 264 using the port side crane. The stinger, rated for 9.80 tons in vertical pull, failed due to internal corrosion buildup from moisture in the socket. The failure to inspect the stinger prior to use was identified as a significant contributing factor.
incident__event__recommendations: Preventive Maintenance Daily checklist should clearly identify inspecting the stinger as a required step; Cold work permits and Job Safety Analysis documents should specify inspecting the stinger; Revision of sling and lifting gear replacement frequency

incident__context__region: Pacific OCS Region, Gulf of Mexico
incident__event__top_event: Amputation injury during conductor removal operations
incident__event__summary: On June 18, 2023 at 18:35 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered amputation of the middle and ring finger of his left hand while adjusting chains on a marine growth removal system (MGRS) during conductor removal operations at Platform Gail. The IP placed his left hand fingers under the chain during an assumed pause in conductor lifting. The conductor resumed rising, tightening the chain and trapping the IP's hand, resulting in amputation of finger tips between the fingernail and first knuckle. The incident occurred due to inadequate verbal communication regarding the 'all stop' command, lack of risk assessment for the MGRS methods employed, and deviation from the approved Application for Permit to Modify (APM) which specified use of diver methods with water jets.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop and implement comprehensive written management of change (MOC) procedures for switching between MGRS methods; Conduct formal risk and hazards analysis on all MGRS methods prior to use, in accordance with API RP 75; Improve radio communication protocols with explicit confirmation of 'all stop' commands by the Weatherford Lead; Implement enhanced guarding or engineering controls to prevent worker proximity to moving machinery and tensioned chains; Ensure compliance with approved APM and notify BSEE of any deviations or alternative methods; Provide additional training on communication procedures and hazard recognition for all personnel involved in conductor removal

incident__event__summary: On January 18, 2024, during drilling operations at GC 478, OCS-G35662, Well 004 on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a 22" casing connection failed at the rig floor. The S-90/MT pin connection of joint 16 was cross-threaded and made up only ¼ - 3/8 turn instead of the required ½ - ¾ turn. When the flush-mounted slips holding joint 17 were opened to install anti-rotation keys, the axial load transferred to the improperly made-up connection, causing the pin to separate from the box. Seventy-two joints of casing fell partially into the hole and onto the seafloor, breaking apart and impacting critical subsea infrastructure. No injuries or environmental impacts occurred, but damages exceeded $15 million.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure visual inspection of casing connections is completed as per approved running procedures to detect cross-threading and makeup discrepancies; Clarify in JSA and running procedures when anti-rotation keys should be installed relative to slip release; Implement supervisor verification of connection makeup before slips are released; Establish clear communication protocols between crew members regarding casing positioning and connection verification

incident__event__top_event: Hand caught between crane cable and sheave resulting in finger lacerations and partial amputation
incident__event__summary: On July 20, 2024, at 2200 hours, a rigger working on abandonment operations at Ship Shoal 224 D platform suffered lacerations to his ring finger and partial amputation of his pinky finger when his right hand was caught between the crane cable and crane block sheave while guiding a casing cutter tool through the deck. The incident occurred during a blind lift operation where the injured person used his hands instead of push poles to guide the suspended load, contrary to the operator's Material Handling policy. The JSAs performed that night did not identify the hazard of being caught between the crane cable and sheave, and Stop Work Authority was not invoked when the unsafe hand placement began.

incident__event__top_event: Dropped Objects - Traveling Block and Drill Line
incident__event__summary: On 20 July 2017 at 16:45 hours, Arena Offshore, LP experienced a dropped objects incident during drilling operations at Eugene Island Block 251C. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Walker Ridge Block 758
incident__event__summary: On March 21, 2023 at 02:30 hours, a 25-pound riser bolt insert dislodged from a riser flange and fell approximately 75 feet to the rig floor on the drillship Valaris DS-18. The object passed between two Floorhands and struck an aluminum stairway, damaging two steps. The riser retainer pin was either never installed or fell out during transport. The retention cable (sling) required by WIMS Step 6, Item 'd' was not installed. Personnel failed to follow proper procedures and did not stop the job when the incident occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop checklist with all pertinent safety steps for running riser attached to WIMS; Break down long WIMS into stages with designated second crew member to verify compliance; Develop Stop Work Authority Drill program by OIM to increase personnel comfort in stopping jobs when needed

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 389
incident__event__incident_type: Caught between C-plates during wireline tool disconnection
incident__event__summary: On April 22, 2024, at 0815 hours, an injured person (IP) assisting a wireline helper to disconnect wireline tools on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse had his right hand caught between upper and lower C-plates weighing 175 lbs. The IP placed his hand under the upper C-plate to stabilize the tool string while the helper released the tools, causing them to fall 4 inches and crush the IP's hand. The IP sustained lacerations to the right hand ring and middle fingertips, a broken ring finger tip, and required 14 stitches. The IP was evacuated and released to light duty on 4/22/24 and full duty on 5/22/24. Root causes included inadequate training on wireline tools, lack of deepwater experience by the helper, poor communication about hazards, and failure to identify pinch point hazards.

incident__event__top_event: Fall from height through open hole
incident__event__summary: On March 28, 2024, a Transocean Subsea Superintendent fell approximately nine feet through an open hole in a BOP work platform while performing maintenance on the Deepwater Atlas drillship. The hatch cover securing the hole had been damaged and removed during a pre-lift check without proper communication or barriers being installed. The IP was wearing a safety harness but was not tied off. He sustained a knee injury and abrasions and was evacuated for medical treatment. The incident was caused by failure to communicate the open hole, flawed hatch cover design, and lack of proper hazard analysis and barriers.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 468
incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of rope access technician
incident__event__summary: On May 31, 2023, at 09:00 hours, a Rope Access Technician (RAT #2) employed by Southey Contracting experienced an uncontrolled descent of approximately 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson (SWRC) on the Stampede tension leg platform. The technician's working rope slipped through an improperly threaded I'D descender device at the anchor point. During the fall, the technician's left leg struck a SWRC support clamp, causing lacerations and ligament damage. The fall was arrested approximately 8 feet above the waterline when the anti-error catch safety feature of the I'D engaged near the stopper knot. The injured person was rescued by an alternate rope access team and evacuated via helicopter for medical treatment.
incident__event__recommendations: Discontinue use of Shunt devices as personal backup devices and replace with alternative backup devices; Implement refresher training for all RATs on safety and supervisory procedures; Implement Advanced Rescue Training for supervisory personnel; Review and enhance Stop Work Authority procedures; Mandate risk assessment and equipment requirement evaluation for all work scope changes; Provide dedicated and suitable rescue kits with third set of ropes for emergency rescue; Require supervisors to maintain direct line of sight with RATs during rope positioning; Amend rope access procedures to include suitable knots (e.g., alpine butterfly) attached to structure to prevent slippage of descending/retrieval devices; Develop and implement robust rope access permitting checklist; Ensure buddy checks and function testing are completed by different team members; Implement equipment tracking and identification system for all rope access gear; Provide materials and procedures to mitigate wet work conditions from SWRC discharge leak; Install directional carabiners per manufacturer recommendations for all I'D descender installations; Secure/tie-off all anchor point devices with knots to prevent accidental lowering; Ensure stopper knots are installed directly behind anchor point I'D devices

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 52
incident__event__summary: On May 31, 2024 at 01:00 hrs, during offloading and backloading operations at GC 52 A Platform, a stinger attached to the crane's main block latched into a lace on the crane boom. Crane Operator 2 (CO-2) observed this and stopped operations, notifying the Person In Charge (PIC). The PIC instructed Crane Operator 1 (CO-1) to lower only the boom without lowering the main block, contrary to CO-2's recommendation to lower both simultaneously. As the boom was lowered, the stinger pulled tight and snapped a weld on the boom lace. The damaged crane continued in operation until June 6, 2024, when BSEE inspectors removed it from service. The boom tip was replaced at a cost of $70,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Remove stinger when not in use; Enhance pre-use crane inspection procedures to identify loose gear; Improve Job Safety Analysis to cover possibility of stinger hanging up on boom; Implement and enforce Stop Work Authority (SWA) protocols; Ensure supervisory personnel listen to and act on safety concerns raised by crew

incident__event__summary: On March 12, 2016, at 10:06 hours, a fire occurred in accommodation cabin 417 on the drill ship Maersk Valiant during drilling operations in Alaminos Canyon 475. The smoke alarm system was activated and personnel mustered. Fire Teams responded and extinguished the fire using fire hoses after handheld fire extinguishers proved ineffective. The fire was extinguished at 10:26 hours. Forensic analysis could not definitively identify the ignition source; the Samsung tablet could not be eliminated as a possible source but could not be proven. The source was classified as undetermined. Damage was estimated at $50,000 with no injuries reported. A similar fire had occurred in the B-deck linen closet on December 13, 2015.
incident__event__recommendations: All electrical equipment must be checked in when arriving and inspected by electrical department; No electrical equipment should be left plugged into rig power system unattended; Charging devices should not be stored on materials with high combustion potential; 24-hour fire watch in living quarters until well completion

incident__event__top_event: Lifting equipment failure resulting in dropped load
incident__event__summary: While lifting a 2.69 Ton discharge module from a 14-P-220 Mud Pump using a 3 Ton chain hoist trolley, the internal chain guides failed in a bent condition, causing the module to fall approximately 3 inches onto metal grating. Third party testing determined the failure resulted from overload conditions caused by unforeseen forces including friction from the load catching on bolts or lip edges, shock loading, and backup equipment pulling against the load. incident__event__incident_type: Equipment failure due to human error
incident__event__summary: On 1 April 2022, during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q-4000 semi-submersible rig in well SA001 (G-19409 Lease, Alaminos Canyon 815), four sections of 5-7/8 inch Heavy Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP) and the Bottom Hole Assembly (BHA) weighing 6000 pounds and 123 feet long disconnected from the drill string and dropped to the sea floor. The disconnection occurred on 31 March 2022 at 15:42 when a loss of 5000 pounds was recorded on the drill string weight indicator, but was not identified until 1 April 2022 at 08:20 via ROV monitoring. The root cause was determined to be an improperly torqued drill pipe connection. The lost equipment came to rest 92 meters from well center and 377 meters from the center of the safe operating zone. No subsea infrastructure damage was sustained. Recovery and inspection costs totaled $2,300,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Lessee needs to ensure all drill string connections are properly torqued to required specifications

incident__event__top_event: Personnel struck by flange released from bench vise
incident__event__summary: On 08-FEB-2016 at 1315 hours, an H&P Mechanic was struck in the face by a flange associated with a butterfly valve while tightening bolts on a flange connection secured in a bench vise. The flange rotated and released from the vise due to excessive force applied during torqueing of the second-to-last bolt. The mechanic sustained lacerations above the right eyebrow and down the bridge of the nose, requiring medical evacuation and stitches. Contributing factors included improper body positioning, excessive force applied to the tool, and excessive wear on the vice jaw.
incident__event__recommendations: Personnel should not apply excessive force to a tool while pulling toward their body; Personnel should not stand in the direct path of impact should equipment fail; Proper tool selection should be used for the job to be conducted; Proper body positioning must be maintained during tool use

incident__event__summary: During tripping in the hole with 22 inch casing on the landing string, a Floor Man monitoring the hydra racker drag chain noticed excessive derrick shaking and observed smoke from the main side crown cluster. The first reduction 78 inch sheave in the crown cluster was found to have a vertical crack and had shifted downward approximately 3 inches. The root cause was identified as weld defects near the root of the weld between the web plates and the hub as noted in NOV Product Bulletin No. 87819987.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Mud Pit Discharge
incident__event__summary: On 11 February 2014, an estimated 144 barrels of Synthetic Base Mud was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from the #3 Active Pit dump valve during a mud pit cleaning and transfer operation. The incident resulted from failure to follow the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), improper Lock Out/Tag Out procedures, and inadequate sampling prior to discharge. The #3 Dump Valve was left in the open position and not properly identified during the operation.
incident__event__recommendations: Installation of additional isolation valve in piping layout to provide barrier against accidental discharge; Enhanced procedures for opening and closing overboard discharge valves; Improved Lock Out/Tag Out execution and verification; Proper sampling from correct pit location before discharge authorization

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Alaminos Canyon Block 337
incident__event__top_event: Worker pinned by Pipe Racking System arm
incident__event__summary: On October 12, 2024, at 0540 hours, a Derrickman (Injured Person/IP) working on the upper fingerboards of the Pipe Racking System during tripping operations was caught and pinned between the upper PRS arm and a stand of pipe. The IP had repositioned himself from the designated safe observation area onto the upper fingerboards without a proper working-at-heights permit to manually secure a malfunctioning fingerboard latch in the open position. While attempting to tie the latch, the PRS initiated an automatic move to retrieve a stand of pipe in the same row, extending the upper arm and pinning the IP. The IP was freed by manual intervention and evacuated for medical treatment.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement written policies prohibiting personnel from working directly on fingerboards during pipe tripping operations; Incorporate derrick areas into Personnel Zone Management system go/no-go zones; Enforce mandatory working-at-heights permits for any work on fingerboards with moving equipment; Establish active monitoring protocols for CCTV cameras during all tripping operations; Conduct training on hazard recognition and safe-zone compliance for all rig personnel; Address fingerboard latch maintenance and repair procedures to prevent malfunctions

incident__event__summary: On 27 September 2024, a Deckhand on the M/V Harvey Freedom support vessel injured his left leg during equipment back-loading operations from the Noble Valiant drillship. The injured party was positioned in the aft setback area attempting to steady a 50-foot basket with a tagline when the support vessel rolled, causing the worker to lose footing. The worker was wearing required PPE and working from the safe setback area. Sea conditions were 2-3 feet with occasional swells of 5-7 feet and winds of approximately 14 knots. The deck was wet but clear of other trip and slip hazards. The worker's boots were in serviceable condition with sufficient tread. The injury required surgery and resulted in more than 3 days lost time.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Alaminos Canyon Block 735
incident__event__top_event: Lost Time Injury - Personnel Contact with Moving Top Drive System
incident__event__summary: On 8 December 2023 at approximately 08:30 am, a Floorhand sustained a lost time injury when struck by the Top Drive System (TDS) on the Noble Globetrotter II at Alaminos Canyon 735. The injured person was positioned on the port pipe racker upper access platform approximately 80 feet above the drill floor, attempting to troubleshoot a slack rope alarm. The injured person failed to notify the Driller of their return to the platform. When the Driller lowered the TDS, the lower port TDS roller assembly contacted the injured person, folding their upper torso around the handrail. The injured person sustained a punctured lung, fractured ribs, and fractured scapula. The incident resulted from inadequate communication, lack of hazard identification on the JSA, absence of engineering barriers, and no visible warnings identifying the moving equipment hazard.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement engineered barrier preventing access to port pipe racker upper access platform during operations or until safe clearance is granted; Identify TDS moving equipment hazard on all relevant JSAs; Post visible warnings and cautions identifying moving equipment hazards on port pipe racker upper access platform; Establish mandatory communication protocol requiring personnel to notify drill floor of position changes; Review and enhance hazard analysis procedures for all drilling operations

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Alaminos Canyon Block 857
incident__event__summary: On October 3, 2024, at approximately 9:00 am, an arc flash fire occurred in the SCR house on Shell's Perdido SPAR when a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) failed. During wireline logging operations and cement pumping for hydrate mitigation, the cement unit's electronic panel lost power. Personnel entering the SCR room heard a loud popping sound and observed smoke. Investigation identified arcing between DC buss bars, arcing on circuit breaker heat sinks, and a failed buss insulator in SCR cabinet #1. The fire was not detected by the rig fire and gas alarm system and was only identified when personnel entered the SCR house. Property damage was estimated at $25,000.

incident__event__top_event: Unauthorized release of Synthetic Based Mud
incident__event__summary: On 4 September 2023 at approximately 13:20, approximately 24 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from Alaminos Canyon (AC) 857, H&P 205 Drilling Rig located on the Shell Perdido Spar. The discharge resulted from obstructions in two valves (knife valve and butterfly valve) on the degasser tank discharge line that prevented proper sealing. The knife valve was obstructed by cuttings and the butterfly valve was blocked by a piece of rubber, both preventing full closure despite visual verification by crew members.
incident__event__recommendations: Crews will fill processing pits with sea water to check for any leaks through containment valves prior to future operations; Install skillet on processing pit master dump valve to prevent unintentional discharge, consistent with active pit master dump valve design

incident__event__top_event: Fire in Subsea Motor Control Center
incident__event__summary: On June 21, 2023, at 0930 hours, the Variable Frequency Device A (VFD-A) failed at the Perdido Spar platform in Alaminos Canyon Block 857, releasing smoke and activating smoke detectors in the Subsea Motor Control Center room. The failure was caused by the B-phase reactor on the VFD-A failing to ground, resulting in smoke generation. The automated facility shut-in system functioned as designed, triggering personnel muster. Cabinet A reached 350°F while other cabinets reached 210°F. No injuries or pollution were reported. The estimated replacement cost was $65,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Siemens to investigate why the reactor thermal switch did not trip the drive on over temperature

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Alaminos Canyon Block 25
incident__event__summary: On July 18, 2021, the Diana Hoover Spar facility experienced loss of mooring line #4 due to chain link failure. The 160 mm R4 grade chain link, in service for approximately 20 years, fractured at the crown and flash weld. Investigation by Stress Engineering Services determined the fractures were caused by hydrogen embrittlement resulting from improper heat treatment during manufacturing, which resulted in high hardness, low ductility, and low impact toughness. The facility remained stable with the remaining 11 mooring lines per 2010 Facility Model analysis. Estimated repair cost is $10-15 million with 6-9 months required for replacement chain manufacturing.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Alaminos Canyon Block 380
incident__event__summary: On 29 September 2020 at approximately 17:15 hours, a crane lifting a 15-ton riser spider/gimbal on the Noble Globetrotter II made contact with a handrail during the lift. This contact broke a weld holding a guard for hydraulic hoses. The guard (4 feet long, 12 lbs) fell 6 feet 10 inches to a walkway, bounced through a handrail, and fell an additional 9 feet 6 inches to the main deck. No personnel were injured as the area was clear by design of the lift plan.
incident__event__recommendations: Install guides inside the hole in the spider to guide it straight up to clear all hazards and achieve better control of the riser spider

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Alaminos Canyon Block 815
incident__event__summary: On July 17, 2021 at 10:30 hours, the Transocean Offshore Deepwater Pontus Drillship experienced a complete loss of power while conducting well completion operations on the SA 008 Well in Alaminos Canyon Block 815. The loss of power was caused by failure of an 11,000 volt to 110-volt transformer that sent 11,000 volts to ground, triggering the ground fault sensing device to trip low voltage breakers open. This caused all six power generating units to shut down, resulting in vessel drift. The crew successfully activated the Emergency Disconnect System to disconnect the LMRP from the subsea BOP within 35 seconds. The Chief Electrician manually reset the breakers to restore power after the vessel had drifted approximately 1,000 meters. The LMRP sustained damage from dragging on the seafloor for approximately 320 feet. No injuries or pollution occurred. Total property damage was estimated at $2,150,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Improve the power distributing system to monitor the open or close circuit status of the low voltage breakers

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Tree Valve Leak
incident__event__summary: On 18 March 2022 at 1800 hours, during abandonment operations on Shell's Alaminos Canyon 815 well SA001 from the Helix Q-4000, a pressure test of a five-barrel cement squeeze failed to hold 1000 psi. Investigation revealed the chemical injection tree valve 1 (CIT1) on the subsea tree was leaking approximately 188 barrels of a 50/50 mixture of Mono Ethylene Glycol and seawater into the Gulf of Mexico. The CIT1 valve had not been functioned to verify closure prior to the pressure test; operators relied on documented position from well handover procedures. Manual ROV intervention and hydraulic functioning of the valve eventually stopped the leak.
incident__event__recommendations: Shell to modify Shell Perdido SA001 Well Handover Procedure to include procedures for verifying status of all valves on the subsea tree

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, East Breaks Block 602
incident__event__summary: On 23 January 2022 at approximately 1545 hours, a Blake International floorhand on the Nansen SPAR platform was instructed to ascend the derrick to check a light plug connection. After completing the task, the injured party (IP) began ascending to retrieve a Self Retracting Line (SRL) at the top of the derrick. The Driller ordered the IP to descend immediately. Upon reaching the derrick access platform approximately 11 feet above the A-frame walkway, the IP disconnected both fall protection lanyards, removing his 100 percent tie-off. The IP then lost his balance, fell off the access platform, struck his head on the A-frame walkway edge, and sustained moderate internal injuries and head lacerations requiring multiple back surgeries and sutures.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure SRL is readily accessible with tag line to prevent use of loop-back lanyards; Install alternative ladder safety systems or safety cages to reduce fatigue; Reinforce documented safety procedures compliance (100% tie-off requirement); Ensure emergency rescue plans are explicitly discussed and marked on JSEA prior to work

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 82
incident__event__summary: On December 3, 2021, at approximately 13:00 hours, the riser elevation crane on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk drillship dropped one end of a riser joint into the riser holding area during drilling operations at MC 82. The incident resulted in damage to the aft stairway and handrail. Investigation revealed that the crane's hydraulic flow control valve had become worn and stuck in the fully engaged position, preventing proper indication of latch status to the crane operator. The evening crew flagger was new to the position and had not been trained on latch verification procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Identify a new way of visualizing riser capture devices are fully engaged to replace unreliable black indicator marks; Ensure Site-Specific Procedures (SSP) and Job Safety Analysis (JSA) encompass all steps required for riser movement operations; Implement mandatory latch verification training for personnel involved in riser handling operations

incident__event__top_event: Deckhand Injury from Unbalanced Crane Load
incident__event__summary: On 26 April 2017 at approximately 0830 hours, a Deckhand was injured while offloading a 550 gallon diesel tote tank from the M/V Miss Julie at platform BAA 105A. The Deckhand attached the crane's stringer to a pre-slung four-part wire rope sling, signaled to lift, but then moved toward the tote tank without stopping the lift when he noticed one sling leg was caught on a shackle. The tote tank lifted while unbalanced, swinging and pinning the Deckhand between the tank and starboard gunwale. The Deckhand sustained a fracture to his lower right leg and two fractured left ribs, requiring surgery.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 778
incident__event__incident_type: Equipment Failure - Rod Piston Seal Failure
incident__event__summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE to consider developing policy to address potential SCSSV rod piston seal failures to prevent reoccurrence; BSEE to perform detailed investigation into rod piston seal failures affecting BP's subsea wells; Follow-up meeting scheduled between BSEE and BP engineering and leadership to investigate similarities in multiple rod piston seal failures over the last 3 years

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Pipeline Leak
incident__event__summary: On 8 June 2022 at 1310 hours, production personnel at MC 778 Thunderhorse South reported an external subsea leak at the P2 Riser Touchdown Connector - Pipeline End Termination (PLET) No. 5. The leak was discovered during a ROV survey initiated following a platform blackout on 5 June 2022. An estimated 12.8 to 128.1 gallons of crude oil was released. The root cause was determined to be connector failure on the PLET, potentially due to design flaw, improper installation, or manufacturing defects. The leak was temporarily sealed using a Connector Actuation Tool pending permanent replacement.

incident__event__summary: On 6 October 2019 at 14:40, the Motor Vessel Ms. Emily struck the Breton Sound (BS) 54 #13 platform operated by Cantium, LLC. The vessel was traveling at 10 knots or less on a west northwest course when large ground swells began pushing it towards the platform. The Captain attempted to avoid collision by steering full right rudder and shifting engines to reverse, but all 4 engines died due to a safety feature that automatically shuts down engines when shifting from forward to reverse gear without allowing the required 6 second delay. The vessel's bow struck the platform, causing structural damage estimated at $150,000. There were no injuries, pollution, or loss of primary containment. No personnel were on the platform at the time of the allision.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Walker Ridge Block 634
incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to Crown Motion Compensator with falling object hazard
incident__event__summary: On September 21, 2020, at approximately 2100 hours, the Driller on the Transocean Deepwater Conqueror attempted to take drill string weight with the Crown Motion Compensator (CMC) while the CMC cylinders were at mid-stroke. The Driller opened the CMC valve without fully retracting the cylinders to the beams as required by procedure. The CMC rapidly dropped 13 feet, shearing all 7 bolts on the starboard equalizing bumper assembly bracket (48 pounds), which fell 203 feet to the drill floor. The port bumper assembly had 6 of 7 bolts sheared. A software logic error in the CMC auto-equalizing feature prevented pressure equalization before the Olmsted valve opened, causing the rapid retraction. No injuries were reported.
incident__event__recommendations: Transocean updated CMC-related procedures to ensure correct setup and sequences; Implemented rig-specific CMC training for Toolpushers, Drillers, Assistant Drillers, and Derrickmen; NOV updated software logic to prevent unintentional CMC valve opening if differential pressure exists

incident__event__summary: On 19-OCT-2022, during facility startup after a three-day shutdown, the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater failed to operate as designed. The incident was reported to the National Response Center.

incident__event__top_event: Fire at gas compressor engine starter
incident__event__summary: On 27 January 2022 at approximately 1625 hours, a fire occurred near the starter of a rental gas compressor on Main Pass 73-A production platform. The fire resulted from an improperly tightened JIC fitting on the engine's starter exhaust hose that backed off over time due to vibration, combined with a leaking Flow Safety Valve (FSV) that allowed natural gas to migrate to the hot exhaust area (>900 degrees). Four platform operators successfully extinguished the fire using handheld fire extinguishers within three minutes. No injuries or pollution occurred. Damage was limited to compressor wiring and FSV internal parts, estimated at $12,000.

incident__event__top_event: Personnel Fall from Basket During Transfer
incident__event__summary: On 19 June 2022 at 0930 hours at West Delta 73 C, during personnel transfer from Motor Vessel Sapphire to platform via 8-man personnel basket, the basket made contact with a scrap iron basket on the vessel deck. One of eight personnel fell 2-3 feet from the basket to the deck, sustaining a fractured right heel. The incident resulted from the crane operator lifting the basket prematurely without proper signaling while the vessel was drifting, combined with inadequate hazard identification in the Job Safety Analysis and a congested vessel deck.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure all personnel involved with personnel transfers are familiar with and discuss all potential hazards affecting safe outcome; Use JSA, JSEA, Hazard Analysis, and pre-job safety meetings to communicate hazards to everyone; Identify vessel underpowering and engine issues in shared JSA documentation; Implement Stop Work Authority procedures to halt unsafe operations

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge via Vent Boom
incident__event__summary: On 25 February 2022 at 0530 hours, a pollution event occurred at West Delta 73 C production platform. An estimated 660 gallons (later revised to approximately 111 gallons) of unrecoverable oil was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the platform's vent boom. The incident resulted from a combination of a Water Skimmer float device failure (pinhole defect) and failure of the Level Safety High (LSH) device on the Vent Scrubber to operate as designed. Contributing factor was operator fatigue, with OP2 working 21 hours (0600 on 24 Feb to 0300 on 25 Feb) and becoming extremely fatigued before the incident occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District has no recommendations for the Office of Incident Investigations at this time

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, West Delta Block 74
incident__event__top_event: Boom section collapse and crush injury
incident__event__summary: On 13 May 2022 at approximately 1125 hours, a lead crane mechanic employed by Seatrax was struck and pinned by a falling boom mid-section while removing connector pins from an Aero-G15 crane on platform WD 74 F. The mechanic had struck a connector pin with a sledgehammer to remove it prior to rigging the mid-section to the bridle, contrary to proper procedure. The pin ejected unexpectedly, causing the boom sections to split and fall, pinning the mechanic's right leg between the boom and sledgehammer handle. The mechanic sustained a compound fracture and was evacuated by helicopter to hospital. Root causes included failure to follow proper Job Safety Analysis procedures, inadequate communication between mechanics, and failure to use required Boom Connector Safety Pins that had been left at the hub facility.

incident__event__top_event: Tensioner ring disengagement from slip joint
incident__event__summary: On August 14, 2014, at 2020 hours on the Noble Globetrotter drilling rig, the tensioner ring disengaged from the slip joint while the crew was scoping out the slip joint in preparation for diverter installation. The riser became unsupported, causing the Hands Free Goose Neck (HFGN) to separate from the tensioner ring. This resulted in extensive damage to the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP), numerous riser joints, tensioner ring, coflex housing, service lines, mux reels, hot line, and top-drive components. The root cause was the Sub-sea Engineer's failure to ensure that the locking dogs and secondary lock were fully engaged on the tensioner ring.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Desoto Canyon Block 4
incident__event__summary: On June 25, 2019, a 162 lb packer setting tool detached from the wireline rope socket and free fell approximately 40 ft to the rig floor during well completion operations on the Deepwater Asgard Drillship. The incident was caused by a breakdown in communication between the winch operator and E-line operator during rigging down operations. The winch operator slacked off before the E-line operator, causing the lubricator weight to set down on the packer setting tool, which allowed the electric line to pull free from the rope socket. No injuries occurred; damage was limited to an indentation in the drill floor and a wooden plank.
incident__event__recommendations: Use a tool trap on the lubricator to trap the wireline tool string in the event of accidental wire stripping from the rope socket/cable head; Improve radio communication to be more descriptive, identifying who is speaking and to whom the command is directed; Consider issuing a safety alert

incident__event__summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.
incident__event__recommendations: Update operating procedures to ensure that umbilical lines are positively pressured; Provide operator training to ensure the line does not get bled to zero for any maintenance tasks; Add a check valve to the system topsides to ensure a positive pressure is maintained on the system; Add a low-pressure alarm on the chemical pump skid for any injection lines that are connected subsea with a hose

incident__event__summary: On 24 June 2022 at approximately 1147 hours, two Gulf Island Services (GIS) employees were performing construction work on Main Pass 260 Platform P, a right-of-way platform owned by Destin Pipeline Company located 55 miles offshore in 300 feet of water. While installing a skillet to isolate the flare scrubber and flare, workers unbolted a 10-inch flange and determined the skillet did not fit. While waiting for the correct size skillet, a handheld gas detector alarmed, pressure was heard escaping from the flange, and an explosion occurred with a large plume of smoke. Both workers reported burning sensations and smoke inhalation. Both were transported to an onshore medical facility and released the same day without restrictions. No pollution or facility damage occurred. BSEE investigation identified three plausible causes: pyrophoric iron (FeS) combustion when exposed to oxygen through the opened flange (most likely), galvanic cell corrosion from a chain hoist generating electric current, or hydrate release within the piping.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE recommends that the Office of Incident Investigations consider a safety alert warning operators about Pyrophoric Iron (FeS) and ways to mitigate risk of these reactions

incident__event__top_event: Crane Boom Failure and Load Drop
incident__event__summary: On September 21, 2016, at approximately 19:15 hours, a crane crew was repositioning Product Tanks on the deck of Motor Vessel Candy Barrel. The second P-Tank was lifted and repositioned alongside the first tank. The tank was initially placed at an angle. The Crane Operator picked the tank up and swung it over the water then back towards the boat. As the load swung starboard, the operator stopped it over the first tank. Vertical movement of the boat caused the bottom tank to strike the suspended tank multiple times, shock loading the crane. The wire rope parted, the suspended tank fell onto the tank below, the boom buckled and fell into the Gulf of Mexico. Estimated damage: $800,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Lake Jackson District has no recommendations at this time

incident__event__summary: On 5-Jan-2014 at 17:30 a portable diesel generator caught fire on Platform A in the Gulf of Mexico. The generator had undergone maintenance earlier that day (09:30-10:45) with no leaks detected. The generator shut down at 17:30 and fire was observed. Platform personnel extinguished the fire using dry chemical extinguishers and cooled the unit with water. Investigation revealed a pinhole-sized leak in the secondary spin-on fuel filter, likely caused by improper installation with a strap wrench. The generator shutdown was initiated by a melted Temperature Safety Element (TSE) within the enclosure.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - East Breaks Block 160
incident__event__top_event: Crane boom collapse due to shock loading
incident__event__summary: During a rigless Plug and Abandonment operation at East Breaks 160 A on 01 December 2015 at approximately 2230 hours, a work string became lodged in the well casing while being lifted by the platform crane. After initial attempts to free the string using the crane failed, the crew attached a hydraulic jack system while leaving the crane still connected to the work string. When the hydraulic jack applied 275,000 pounds of pulling force, the work string suddenly released from the downhole bind, rebounded upward 4-6 feet, and then fell back into the hole. The abrupt release of energy threw the hydraulic jack's traveling slips out of the upper casing bowl, eliminating support for the work string. When the work string fell back down, its full weight was suddenly transferred to the crane's hoist system, shock loading the crane boom. The boom catastrophically buckled and collapsed onto the hydraulic jack work basket, folding in half with the jib end suspended approximately 15 feet above the manned Crane Operator's cab. No injuries, fire, or environmental pollution occurred. Damage exceeded $160,000.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE should consider issuing a safety alert concerning the hazard created with sudden release of stored energy when utilizing hydraulic jack systems and a crane when removing tubing from a wellbore

incident__event__top_event: Fire from overpressurization and rupture of fuel gas filter vessel
incident__event__summary: On 9 May 2015 at approximately 1728 hours, the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter vessel experienced catastrophic overpressurization during facility restart following a pressure upset. The vessel top was blown off, struck a nearby handrail creating sparks that ignited streaming fuel gas. The incident resulted in a fire that was extinguished by operators using portable fire extinguishers. Root causes included severe corrosion of vessel mounting tabs, failed pressure safety valves both upstream and downstream of the filter, unauthorized modifications to the filter vessels that removed differential gauges and altered level indicators, and a single-stage pressure reduction system that was inadequate for the operating conditions.

incident__event__summary: On 9-May-2018 at approximately 10:30 hours, a fire occurred in the temporary quarters of Platform EB 165 A. A mechanic discovered charred soot on the temporary quarters entrance door while conducting holding checks on SSVs during compressor maintenance shutdown. The alarm was sounded, muster was initiated, and personnel investigated. No visible flame was detected. incident__event__top_event: Personnel struck by swivel socket during stud bolt replacement
incident__event__summary: On 21 March 2015 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract worker was struck in the upper lip by a swivel socket attached to a Pneumatic Torque Unit (PTU) when it slipped off the nut of a stud bolt during production header maintenance at the Nansen SPAR. The worker sustained a laceration to the right side of the mouth, possible maxilla fracture, and dental trauma. The incident occurred because a Hydraulic Torque Unit (HTU) with a defective Emergency Shut Down was substituted with a PTU, which produces extreme pressure pulsations that can cause swivel sockets to release from nuts.
incident__event__recommendations: Utilize Best Available and Safest Technology for stud bolt and nut replacement operations; Initiate Stop Work Authority when primary equipment is defective and suitable alternatives are not available; Ensure torque units are equipped with operable Emergency Shut Down systems before commencing work

incident__event__top_event: Welding flash fire with personnel injury
incident__event__summary: At 13:50 hours on August 10, 2017, a welding flash fire occurred at the +12 level of EB-110A platform during hot work to prepare for temporary work platform installation. A welder using an oxygen/acetylene cutting torch to shape angle-iron on the B3 piling directed the torch toward an open out-of-service sump pile containing hydrocarbon fluids. The torch ignited vapors from the sump, causing a flash fire. One welder (Welder B) sustained burns to hands and fell approximately 12 feet into the water. He was retrieved and transferred to UTMB Hospital. The fire self-extinguished. No environmental impact or additional injuries occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America Region, East Breaks Block 602
incident__event__summary: On 21-May-2025 at approximately 1:45 p.m., a crane incident occurred on the Nansen Spar in East Breaks 602 block. Contract scaffold builders were erecting scaffolding around the crane boom rest and used multiple 1/4-ton lever hoists as bracing, attaching them to both the scaffolding and the crane boom lattice without notifying the Lifting Authority. After a lunch break, the Crane Operator resumed lifting operations without conducting a pre-use inspection or checking for changes. When the operator lifted the boom out of the rest, one of the 1/4-ton lever hoists broke and fell approximately 30 feet to the flare boom mezzanine deck. The broken lever hoist weighed 0.5 pounds and caused minor property damage ($500). No personnel injuries were reported.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Oil Release
incident__event__incident_type: Pollution event with thermal burn injury
incident__event__summary: On June 23, 2014, at approximately 10:30 A.M., the Person in Charge (PIC) at Arena Offshore East Cameron 328 B facility was replacing a faulty pneumatic temperature controller (TC) on Heater Treater #2 (NBK-2770). The TC was installed directly into a thread-o-let within the liquid medium of the vessel instead of inside a thermo-well as recommended by the manufacturer. Prior to removal, the vessel was not depressurized and hot oil was not drained to a safe level. When the TC was removed, hot pressurized oil blew out of the one inch opening, striking and burning the PIC on his left bicep. Oil continued to flow across the deck, striking the line heater approximately 20 feet away. Operations personnel manually shut down the facility and installed a nipple and ball valve to isolate the leak. A total of 12 gallons of crude oil was discharged into offshore waters.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure temperature controllers are installed inside thermo-wells as recommended by manufacturer; Implement depressurization procedures prior to removing pressurized vessel components; Drain hot oil to safe levels below component removal points; Allow sufficient cooling time before maintenance activities; Enhance Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to identify hazards specific to non-standard equipment installations

incident__event__top_event: Manway hatch structural failure and foot injury
incident__event__incident_type: Structural damage and lost time accident
incident__event__summary: On October 19, 2017 at approximately 1911 hours, a construction employee severely injured his left foot when a 24-inch manway hatch from a water skimmer (MBM-7110) dropped and struck his foot. The hatch was being opened using a davit pole assembly for cleaning operations. The piping supporting the rod assembly failed due to severe corrosion of the davit and improper placement or detachment of the washer fastener. The employee suffered lacerations and multiple fractures to the left foot. Root causes included lack of pre-operation inspection of the davit and rod assembly condition, and inadequate Job Safety & Environmental Analysis (JSEA) that failed to capture hazards associated with the manway hatch removal.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Eugene Island Block 208J
incident__event__summary: An Ensco Floorhand sustained a severe finger injury on 14 February 2014 at 14:45 hours while assisting with picking up 7-inch casing joints at the v-door area on the Ensco 90 jack-up rig. The worker installed a lift nubbin in casing joint #2 and tightened it with a valve bar. When tightening the nubbin, casing joint #2 slid down the cat walk, pinning the worker's right index finger between the valve bar and the end of casing joint #3. The finger was nearly amputated at the first knuckle joint. The worker was evacuated to LSU Medical Center where surgical amputation of the distal phalanx was required. The probable cause was attributed to the casing joint not being supported by the back stop, allowing it to slip.
incident__event__recommendations: Issue safety alert to industry regarding casing handling incidents; Refer incident to Potential Incident of Noncompliance (PINC) team to develop guidance specifically addressing pipe and casing handling operations at v-door areas on rigs

incident__event__top_event: Fire in diesel generator exhaust system
incident__event__summary: On October 21, 2020 at 0647 hours, Cox Operating operators at EI 259C observed a fire at unmanned platform EI 259A. The fire originated from the diesel generator exhaust. Investigation revealed that a governor actuator linkage failure caused engine overspeed, leading to turbo lockup and oil seal leakage. The air shut off valve failed to fully close due to oil gumming up an o-ring seal, allowing the engine to continue running and increasing exhaust temperature. Carbon buildup in the exhaust was ignited by high temperatures. A preventive maintenance inspection in January 2020 had noted suspected exhaust leak but it was not properly documented in the maintenance system.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement Mapcon auditor and trainer position to review all preventive maintenance reports for full completion; Ensure proper documentation of deficiencies discovered during preventive maintenance inspections

incident__event__summary: On 18 January 2016 at approximately 21:30 hours, a Floorman sustained a crush injury to his left thumb while assisting with laying down a pump in joint assembly using an air hoist on the H&P 105 platform rig. incident__event__summary: On April 28, 2017 at 1651 hours, the boom heel section of a Titan 5400 crane was damaged during compressor equipment transfer operations. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Eugene Island Block 355A
incident__event__summary: On 1 October 2014 at approximately 17:00 hours, the offshore supply vessel (OSV) Endurance, a dynamically-positioning (DP) capable vessel, lost station keeping while conducting well operations on Well A002 at Eugene Island Block 355A platform. The vessel moved off location, causing the Well A002 production tree to be severed at the wellhead flange and fall overboard. A lubricator connected to the wireline unit fell onto the platform deck, damaging handrails and grating. An estimated 300 ft by 50 ft area of oil was spilled. Investigation revealed that a recently installed communications tower located within 1 foot of the DP system caused electrical interference, resulting in simultaneous failure of the DP system, gyro, and throttles. No injuries were reported.
incident__event__recommendations: Add a question in e-Well to identify the type of work vessel including DP vessels and capture the vessel's DP capabilities; Develop an interim SOP to evaluate DP vessels for various offshore well operations until improved regulatory guidelines are established by the USCG and/or BSEE; Implement policy that no communications equipment can be located within 1 meter (3.28 ft) of the throttle control computer, DP computer or gyros; Utilize safety break-away hose coupling in future applications

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Eugene Island Block 360
incident__event__top_event: Mechanic hand injury from rotating fan belt contact
incident__event__summary: On 16 March 2021 at approximately 2030 hours, a mechanic suffered two fractured fingers on his left hand when he entered the compressor cooling fan housing to investigate a vibration alarm and placed his hand on a fan belt that was still rotating. The compressor had been shut down via E-stop relay, but residual energy in the fan belt was not properly isolated or dissipated. The incident was caused by human error (inattention to task) and failure to implement Stop Work Authority, Job Safety Analysis, and Lockout Tagout procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement Stop Work Authority prior to personnel entering confined spaces or equipment housings; Require Job Safety Analysis (JSA) completion before maintenance work on rotating equipment; Enforce Lockout Tagout (LOTO) procedures to isolate and dissipate all stored and residual energy; Ensure compliance with D-5 Energy Isolation LOTO Policy regarding relief and restraint of hazardous stored energy

incident__event__summary: On 12 February 2021 at approximately 0900 hours, a Quality Construction and Production crew was removing a heater tube from heat exchanger HBG-700 on Cox Operating's EI 360-E facility. During removal of a handrail, the third leg became stuck in the socket. The crew used a crane with a nylon strap to apply tension while attempting manual removal with hands and hammer. An employee positioned a 5-foot pry bar under the kick plate and stood up using his foot for leverage while another employee pushed the handrail. The employee was transported offshore and admitted to hospital for orbital socket surgery on 16 February 2021.

incident__event__summary: On 14-FEB-2019 at approximately 0930 hours, a fire was discovered in the upstairs sleeping quarters of Platform C in the Gulf of Mexico. Two Contract Compressor Mechanics detected smoke while replacing a piston. After approximately 30 minutes, the fire alarm sounded. Four personnel mustered and observed flames in the upper left corner of the sleeping quarters. Believing it to be an electrical fire from the window AC unit, they used a 30 lbs extinguisher followed by a 125 lbs extinguisher to suppress the fire. The mattress and surrounding items were removed through an emergency exit and the mattress was eventually tossed overboard. All personnel received medical treatment with no restrictions. The ignition source could not be determined due to discarded evidence including the mattress and an offshore bag.

incident__event__summary: On February 11, 2019 at 1640 hours, a helicopter mechanic fell while attempting to retrieve grease guns from the top of helicopter N438PH. The mechanic had recently purchased oversized work boots online that did not fit properly. While standing on a 2.5" x 4" helicopter fold-out step and holding a pitch change link, the mechanic's left foot slipped due to the boot folding inward. incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Mississippi Canyon Block 773
incident__event__top_event: Structural failure and detachment of external walkway platform
incident__event__summary: On 16 July 2025 at 1810 hours, an external walkway platform on the third floor of the Temporary Living Quarters (TLQ) on Devils Tower SPAR platform unexpectedly detached from the TLQ structure by 12 to 18 inches while three Nabors employees were standing on it. The walkway remained partially secured by stanchions and stairway bolts. All personnel safely evacuated. Investigation revealed the TLQ, originally designed as temporary housing in 2002, had remained on site for 23 years. The welded tabs securing the walkway lacked sufficient welded surface area and were subject to fatigue and corrosion in the marine environment. Visual-only inspections in May 2024 failed to detect the deteriorated weld condition.

incident__event__top_event: Casing lifting pin failure striking worker
incident__event__summary: On July 12, 2025, at approximately 3:30 p.m., a casing lifting pin failed during well abandonment operations on the Vermilion Block 38M platform operated by Chevron U.S.A. Inc. The failed pin, which was bent into a U-shape, struck a Supreme Services employee in the face, causing a subdural hematoma, blunt trauma, an open fracture of the right side of the base of the skull, and multiple closed fractures of facial bones. The incident resulted in a required evacuation via medevac helicopter. Root causes included: (1) installation of a spool riser with an 11.00-inch inside diameter above a seal ring with an 11.44-inch outside diameter, causing the casing to become stuck; (2) the crane operator exceeding the safe working load limit of the casing lifting pin (60,000 pounds) while attempting to lift the stuck casing with the main hoist; and (3) incorrect installation of the two-part sling with D-rings not placed directly adjacent to the casing. Independent testing demonstrated that when D-rings were positioned 3-4 inches from the casing (as they were during the incident), the pin failed at 102,500 pounds, compared to successful performance at 150,000 pounds when D-rings were properly positioned adjacent to the casing.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lake Charles District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert/Bulletin regarding this incident

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Walker Ridge Area Block 249
incident__context__operator: Murphy Exploration & Production Company / BW Offshore USA Inc.
incident__event__summary: On April 28, 2025, at approximately 10:30 a.m., a nitrogen bottle ruptured and exploded during refilling operations on the BW Pioneer FPSO in Walker Ridge Block 249. The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement formal written procedure for nitrogen bottle filling operations with detailed guidelines; Establish proper training program for personnel on cylinder markings, pressure ratings, and overpressure risks; Implement routine inspection and maintenance program for nitrogen bottles and relief devices; Ensure correct relief valves are installed matching cylinder pressure ratings; Update Job Safety Analysis to specify target fill pressure and identify overpressurization hazards; Properly identify and segregate nitrogen bottles by type (DOT vs UN-regulated) and pressure rating; Establish competency requirements for personnel interpreting cylinder markings

incident__event__summary: On March 11, 2015, the H&P 203 rig accidentally sheared a 5 7/8" drill pipe while setting a test plug. The Blind Shear Rams (BSR's) closed unexpectedly on the drill pipe after a contact switch in the rig floor panel for the close side of the shear ram function was found stuck in the closed position. A rig crew member pressed the 'Push to Operate' button intending to perform a different function, but due to the stuck contact switch, the BSR's fired automatically, shearing the pipe and leaving a test plug, pup joint, and 22.35 feet of drill pipe in the hole.

incident__event__summary: On 26-NOV-2017 at 0405 hours, during flowback operations on the Ewing Banks 834-A platform, a roustabout (R2) was struck in the right upper collarbone by a pressurized hose that ejected from an Auxiliary Mud Tank. incident__event__summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.
incident__event__recommendations: Operators should maintain proper coatings on wellhead equipment in corrosive saltwater environments; Well intervention plans should account for potential exacerbation of fluid returns and include adequate vessel and fluid handling capacity; Timely reporting of well anomalies to regulatory authorities is critical for prompt remediation; Scale and paraffin buildup should be managed proactively to enable safe well interventions; Management communication systems should ensure field observations are escalated and acted upon promptly

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - H2S Gas Release
incident__event__summary: On 29-May-2016 at 1310 hrs, a hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas release of 400 ppm occurred on Main Pass 299-FP Platform during pigging operations on the flowline from MP 299-A to FP Platform. While the crew drained fluids from the pig trap, H2S sensors detected high concentrations of sour gas. The H2S alarm sounded and the platform was shut in. All personnel donned SCBAs and mustered. After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District made no recommendations to the Office of Incident Investigations

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Time Injury from Fall
incident__event__summary: On February 7, 2024, at approximately 09:30 am, a loss time injury occurred when a Danos construction worker attempted to climb an out-of-service scaffolding ladder attached to the ExxonMobil Pipeline Company Motor Control Center/Control Building (ZZZ-006) on the Galveston A 244 JP platform. The ladder, which had been placed out of service on January 1, 2024 with the Self-Retracting Lanyard (SRL) removed and no visual identification tags, shifted when the worker reached the 3rd rung, causing him to jump to the deck and sustain injuries to both legs. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate scaffolding construction, lack of proper out-of-service marking, missing fall protection equipment, inadequate work permits that did not address working at heights, poor communication between operators and contractors, and lack of coordination between multiple contractors on the platform.
incident__event__recommendations: All operators should review or revisit Safety Alert No. 469 (BSEE Identifies Medical Evacuation and Emergency Hazards During Risk-Based Inspections)

incident__event__top_event: Explosion and Fire at Pig Launcher
incident__event__summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.

incident__event__summary: On 14 March 2018 at 16:40 hours, a Helix Energy Solutions Roughneck sustained serious injuries to his left hand during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q4000 semisubmersible vessel. While hoisting a wireline valve assembly using a coiled tubing lift frame (CTLF) attached to a multipurpose tower, the worker observed the assembly beginning to shift and potentially contact the CTLF. His hand became trapped between the main winch sheave and the static line cable, resulting in partial amputation of the left thumb and severe laceration of the left ring finger. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and required surgery.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Garden Banks Block 213
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Spill
incident__event__summary: At approximately 23:00 hours on 23 May 2014, the Diamond Ocean Onyx semi-submersible drilling rig reported a loss of synthetic base mud (SBM) during abandonment operations on Well #1. Initial report of 107 bbl was revised to 61 bbl after strapping calculations. The spill occurred when mud line valves on the Schlumberger Cement Unit were left in the open position after cleaning operations and were not checked prior to the cement job. When the Cementer began pumping cement from Batch Mixing Tank #2, the tank overflowed, causing SBM to spill into secondary containment areas and then into drains leading to the overboard discharge line, which also had open valves. The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Garden Banks Block 426A
incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to drill pipe during pipe racker lifting operations
incident__event__incident_type: Equipment failure due to human error
incident__event__summary: On 10 December 2021 at 0030 hours, a lifting incident occurred during Well A019 tripping in the hole operations on H&P Rig 406 fixed on Shell's Auger TLP. A single joint of 3.5-inch drill pipe was damaged when the Lower Pipe Racker Operator (LPRO) pulled his end ahead of the Upper Pipe Racker Operator (UPRO), causing the top of the drill pipe stand to get under the elevators as the traveling block was coming down. The pipe bowed and threads were damaged. No injuries occurred. Root causes included LPRO inexperience (second operation, 8 months on rig), inadequate Job Safety Analysis, and inadequate Job By Design Template that failed to specify waiting until block is below fingerboard before removing next stand.
incident__event__recommendations: Update Job By Design Template to require stand of drill pipe to stay in slot until block is lowered below fingerboard before bringing out next stand; Update Job Safety Analysis to identify potential hazards of impact between elevators and drill pipe when traveling down while simultaneously removing pipe from fingerboard; Include information related to inexperienced employees in JSA

incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of construction hoist block
incident__event__summary: On 28 September 2020 at approximately 0049 hours, while setting down single joint drill pipes onto the construction floor using the construction hoist block on the Noble Globetrotter I Drill Ship, the construction hoist block descended uncontrollably without input from the driller. The block continued its descent until it contacted the top of a drill pipe joint, applying approximately 50,000 pounds of pressure and causing the pipe to bow. The probable cause was determined to be failure of the mechanical coupling between the Forward Motor and Gearbox on the Construction Hoist Block.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Garden Banks Block 427
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea BOP Seal Failure
incident__event__summary: On 7 June 2017, the Noble Don Taylor drillship under contract to Shell reported an unauthorized discharge of approximately 94.2 barrels of 15.1 ppg synthetic oil base mud during exploration operations on Well DC005 (Cardamom) in Garden Banks Block 427, Gulf of Mexico. The spill was caused by failure of the upper variable bore ram (VBR) aft bonnet door cartridge seal on the subsea blowout preventer. Contributing factors included buildup of debris in the upper VBR body, failure to conduct proper hole cleaning and flushing of the BOP as recommended in NOV Product Information Bulletin released 11 August 2015, and inadequate communication of known risk of loss of seal integrity.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement proper hole cleaning and flushing procedures for subsea BOP prior to operation; Improve communication of known risks identified in equipment manufacturer bulletins; Install cartridge guard to protect cartridge and seal from debris buildup and compression

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Garden Banks Block 506
incident__event__summary: On 20 March 2024 at approximately 0705 hours, a Schlumberger Pump Operator sustained a left shoulder injury (dislocated shoulder and torn labrum) while attempting to jump across a 4-foot gap from the Coil Tubing Control Cabin to the Coil Tubing Reel Platform (approximately 12 feet above deck) during well workover operations on the Helix Q-4000 semisubmersible vessel. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Garden Banks Block 958
incident__context__materials: ['synthetic oil base mud (SOBM)', '15.3 ppg drilling fluid']
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil base mud
incident__event__summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lafayette District recommends a Safety and Environmental Management Systems (SEMS) audit be conducted concurrently during Cobalt internal audit scheduled for November 2015

incident__event__top_event: Shallow Water Flow from Low-Pressure Wellhead Housing
incident__event__summary: On March 28, 2021, at approximately 1921 hours, personnel observed a steady flow from the Lower Cement Port 1 on the 38-inch Low-Pressure Wellhead Housing during riserless drilling operations with the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship at Garden Banks Block 962. The flow occurred approximately 7 hours after 22-inch casing cement was placed. Analysis revealed the flow originated from above the top of salt in a dirty salt section, migrating through a thin channel in the cement behind the 22-inch casing. Fluid samples contained trace hydrocarbons and high chlorides indicative of seawater. The well was temporarily abandoned on April 25, 2021.
incident__event__recommendations: Setting an additional casing string, such as a 28-inch casing, just above the dirty salt section would have possibly led to much better isolation of the dirty salt section when running and conducting the cement job on the 22-inch casing

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Hand Function - Finger Amputation and Crushing Injury
incident__event__summary: While removing a gearbox coupling and mud agitator shaft for repair on the T.O. Discoverer India, the injured person (IP) and mechanic deviated from the written risk assessment (WRA) procedure. The IP installed support beams out of sequence and attempted to adjust a safety clamp without first securing it properly. While hammering an Allen wrench onto a corroded bolt head on the agitator shaft, the 750-pound shaft and coupling suddenly separated from the gearbox output shaft and fell, crushing the IP's fourth and fifth fingers between the wrench and support beams. The incident was preceded by three prior unexpected separations of the agitator shaft from the gearbox output shaft (2010, 2014, 2015), but no additional precautionary measures were implemented.
incident__event__recommendations: Update procedures and WRAs to include hazards associated with unexpected coupling failure and separation of agitator shaft from gearbox output shaft; Implement additional precautionary measures to mitigate the hazard of unexpected coupling failure; Enforce company policy requiring all participating members of a job task to individually sign the WRA; Ensure personnel wear appropriate personal protective equipment (impact gloves) during repair tasks
