incident__event__top_event: Worker injury from uncontrolled sandblasting nozzle
incident__event__summary: On February 2, 2020, at approximately 1600 hours, a sandblaster at Garden Banks 128-A sustained a laceration to the right bicep when he lost control of a sandblasting nozzle pressurized at 85 lbs while standing on an improperly secured scaffold board placed across two vessels. The board shifted during repositioning, causing the worker to lose his grip on the nozzle. The nozzle whipped and became lodged under the worker's right arm pit, blasting through his clothing before the dead-man switch could shut down the system.
incident__event__recommendations: Incorporate do's and don'ts into Cable Rigging training course to prohibit laying boards from vessel to vessel; Incorporate depressurization of hoses prior to moving or repositioning as best practice in Abrasive Blasting Procedure; Document hose depressurization requirement on Job Safety Analysis; Designate a specific employee to maintain constant visual contact with workers at heights; Construct proper scaffolds between vessels instead of using unsecured boards

incident__event__summary: On 15 November 2024 at 0837 hours, a gas release occurred in the shaker house aboard the Valaris DS-16 drillship during well abandonment operations. While displacing the kill line and landing string back to calcium bromide brine after nitrogen displacement, hydrocarbons were released into the shaker house atmosphere. The incident was triggered by a compromised liquid leg in the mud gas separator, allowing hydrocarbon gas to bypass the separator and enter the shaker house. A hydrocarbon alarm activated, prompting evacuation and crew muster. Gas levels returned to zero and operations resumed without further issues.
incident__event__recommendations: Safety meeting with all crews on importance of circulating fluids/gases from well and impact on mud gas separator liquid leg

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Garden Banks Block 959
incident__event__top_event: Drill pipe parting and loss of drill string
incident__event__summary: On 03 August 2025 at 1934 hours, during drilling of the 26-inch top-hole open water section of Well SP202 at Garden Banks Block 959, a torque spike of 71.8 kft-lbf occurred while drilling salt at 9699 feet depth. This caused a 410 kips reduction in hook load, full loss of torque, loss of hook load, and loss of pump pressure. The drill string separated below the water line. Approximately 4128 feet of 6.625-inch drill pipe fell to the seafloor, with an additional 5350 feet of bottom hole assembly and drill pipe left in the wellbore. The ROV tether was trapped in at least three locations by the fallen pipe. No injuries were reported and no personnel were in the red zone at the time of incident.

incident__event__summary: On 22-November-2019 at Garden Banks 491, during tripping in operations with an 11-7/8" Easy Drill Subsurface Valve, a loss of power to drilling equipment occurred. Personnel received an alarm indicating smoke or fire in the Port Switch Gear Room. Investigation revealed that condensation accumulated on top of the Drilling Drive DC Bar cabinet from the Air Handling Unit ducting vent and ran inside the cabinet, causing an Arc Flash fire. The fire was extinguished by mechanics using a handheld fire extinguisher. Damage to the Drilling Drive DC Bar Cabinet, AHU ductwork, vent, and insulation totaled $65,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Remind lessees about hazards of storing combustible materials near electrical transformers or batteries; Prevent possibility of condensation in Switch Gear or ECR Rooms; Clean all areas with electrical cabinets frequently to prevent accumulation of dust or debris; Learn from past experiences to avoid duplicating same outcome

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 18
incident__event__top_event: Amputation of fingers due to falling wireline tool and pack-off assembly
incident__event__summary: On August 13, 2020, a Floorhand working in a riding belt on the H&P 100 rig was hoisted to guide wireline tool string out of a lubricator. The wireline pulled free from the rope socket after the dual pack-off snagged on a shackle, causing the 65-pound pack-off assembly to fall and amputate the Floorhand's thumb and two fingers on his left hand. The incident resulted from improper hand placement in violation of H&P's Hands-Free Work Objective, lack of communication of this policy in pre-tour meetings, and failure of crew members to stop work when they observed the unsafe hand placement.
incident__event__recommendations: Update JSA to outline H&P responsibilities during rigging up/down of 3rd party wireline equipment; Include Hands-Free policy, No Go Zones, and prohibition on lifting additional tools with wireline tool in JSA; Create pre-job planning checklist for 3rd party activities; Re-issue Hands-Free training and personnel hoisting process for all Offshore Division rigs

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 237
incident__event__top_event: Loss of vessel position control and Emergency Disconnect System activation
incident__event__summary: On 16-OCT-2014 at 2250 hours, a blown fuse on the #4 generator aboard the Ensco 8502 caused loss of four of eight thrusters. Unable to maintain position, the vessel drifted toward the designated 'Red Zone' approximately 82 feet from well center. The Driller activated the EDS, which functioned as designed, shearing drill pipe and closing the Annular before unlatching the LMRP. Approximately 150 barrels of SBM were released from the riser and choke/kill hoses. No injuries occurred. The well remained secure and undamaged.
incident__event__recommendations: No recommendations issued by Houma District BSEE at this time

incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of traveling block and top drive
incident__event__summary: On June 9, 2014, while troubleshooting a brake fault alarm on the drawworks system during pipe transfer operations, the Chief E.T. performed a hard reset of drive #4 without confirming that the drawworks brakes were set. Due to miscommunication between the Toolpusher and Chief E.T., the reset interrupted motor control while the top drive was still being held up, causing the traveling block to freefall. The Assistant Driller activated the emergency hydraulic dump, which caused the brakes to slam shut but failed to prevent impact with the rig floor. Damage occurred to drill pipe, the Driller's shack, and the traveling assembly. Root cause analysis identified sticking hydraulic solenoid valves due to debris accumulation and improper hydraulic pump installation.
incident__event__recommendations: Improve communication protocols between crew members during troubleshooting operations; Establish mandatory verification procedures before performing system resets; Implement enhanced maintenance procedures for hydraulic solenoid valves; Conduct training on proper identification and investigation of maintenance alarms before attempting corrective actions

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 254
incident__event__summary: On 23-DEC-2018 at approximately 1800 hours, an explosion occurred inside Cooler HAL-4160B on the Allegheny Sea platform (OCS-G 7049, Green Canyon 254-A) operated by Eni US Operating Co. Inc. The explosion was caused by ignition of a lower explosive limit (LEL) mixture of air and natural gas within the process piping. The root cause was that instrument air (instead of instrument gas as configured by the manufacturer) was connected to the temporary rental Booster Compressor CAE-5140A. This allowed continuous air from a Kimray pressure control valve to enter the inlet suction scrubber and mix with gas from the vent system through an open Blowdown Valve (BDV). The explosion separated cooler end caps, damaging the cooler module, PSV, discharge piping, ESD station #7, main deck south guardrail, and other equipment. The ESD station shut in the facility, but the compressor engine continued running until manually shut down by the mechanic after the emergency stop button failed. All personnel mustered safely with no injuries. Contributing factors included incomplete Management of Change (MOC) process, lack of pre-startup review, unavailable operating procedures, and installation not performed as recommended by the manufacturer.
incident__event__recommendations: Contact Houma Office before startup of booster compressor

incident__event__top_event: Hand Crush Injury from Pinch Point
incident__event__summary: On 04-JUN-2021 at 0930 hours, an ROV Deckman inadvertently placed his left hand in a pinch point between the two cantilever support areas (A-Frame and lower pedestal) of the ROV lifting frame during ROV launch operations on the Seadrill West Neptune. The Deckman immediately contacted the Winch Operator by radio to stop and retract the A-Frame. The Deckman was medivaced to shore for evaluation. Investigation found that the TBRA did not identify the pinch point hazard, the red zone was unidentified, and the Deckman had become complacent during a routine operation.
incident__event__recommendations: Update TBRA form to identify pinch point hazard between upper A-Frame and lower pedestal; Identify and properly label red zones and safe observation zones for all ROV systems; Design and install physical barrier on launch and recovery system to eliminate pinch point

incident__event__top_event: Crane sheave failure with dropped objects
incident__event__summary: On September 16, 2019, at approximately 1700 hours, a sheave failure occurred during a critical lift of Mud Tank #1 module (92.5k pounds, approximately 96% of crane capacity) using the port side crane main hoist at Green Canyon Block 338 Platform A. The main line tip Nylatron idler sheave shattered, resulting in dropped objects that struck two injured persons (IPs) attending tag lines. One IP was struck on the right forearm and right boot tip; the other on left middle and ring fingers. Both received first aid and returned to full duty. Investigation revealed corrosion of the outer bearing race and mild side loading during the heavy lift as probable causes. The roller bearing had not been replaced in 17 years, had exterior corrosion, missing lubrication seals, and surface corrosion on the outer race.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 338
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - LP Pipe Rupture
incident__event__summary: On 16-OCT-2015 at 2015 hours, an 18-inch LP pipe ruptured on the Front Runner spar at GC 338A, releasing approximately 25 barrels of oil and undetermined amount of gas. The rupture occurred at the chemical injection point where the line penetrated the production deck. Root cause was severe internal corrosion (metal loss up to 50.4%) caused by acidic chemical injection without proper distribution via quills. Contributing factors included failure to use quills for chemical distribution and inadequate NDT testing that did not cover injection point areas. One employee was injured. Total estimated damage: $1.5 million.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement proper chemical injection distribution using quills at all injection points; Establish comprehensive NDT testing protocol that includes chemical injection point areas; Conduct piping inspections throughout structure at all chemical injection locations; Monitor for ongoing corrosion issues and implement preventive maintenance program

incident__event__top_event: Unauthorized discharge of Zinc Bromide due to Emergency Disconnect Sequence activation
incident__event__summary: On January 15, 2022, at approximately 16:10 hours, the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced a Dynamic Position (DP) system failure due to misalignment of Thruster-2 (180 degrees out of alignment) during completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. High winds and sudden wind direction changes caused the misaligned thruster to push the drillship in the same direction as the wind, compromising DP position into red alert status. The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.
incident__event__recommendations: Operator to send letter of explanation to Houma District Office addressing INC G-110 and detailing prevention measures for future incidents

incident__event__summary: On May 10, 2018, a fire occurred in the mud pit room of the drillship Transocean Deepwater Asgard while commissioning a Precision Fluid Injection (PFI) System. The fire was caused by incorrect wiring of temperature control modules to the wrong junction box, resulting in false temperature readings that prevented the heater safety shut-off from activating. The heater continued to heat the drilling fluid beyond safe limits, causing the discharge hose to ignite. The fire was detected by flame, heat, and smoke detectors and extinguished with portable fire extinguishers. No injuries were reported.
incident__event__recommendations: Add function test to controller before connecting power to P-Heater; Add warnings and training regarding potential for P-Heater temperatures remaining below desired setting; Require commissioning tests to be done with water; Install puck in thermocouple junction box less susceptible to errors; Change hose material to flame resistant material; Add outlet flow meter to monitor for no flow conditions; Add emergency shut down buttons in any room where heater is installed; Require two people to inspect all connections; Create installation test sheet; Require three tests of all wire segments (open, short, and signal) before commissioning tests

incident__event__summary: On April 7, 2017, a joint of 9 7/8" casing was dropped by the Pipe Deck Pipe Handler (PDPH) crane on the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana while being transferred between pipe bays. A leaking hydraulic seal on the aft pipe adapter cylinder caused the gripper to lose closing pressure, allowing the casing to slip during the lift. The crane operator had to periodically re-initialize the casing settings due to the hydraulic leak but did not identify this as a system malfunction. All personnel were in safe zones and no injuries or equipment damage resulted.
incident__event__recommendations: Replace automated pipe adapter system with manual pin style system to eliminate leaking hydraulic seals and provide visual confirmation of positive latch

incident__event__top_event: Fire in Flash Gas Compressor skid
incident__event__summary: At approximately 2350 hours on May 7, 2014, a fire occurred in the skid of the Flash Gas Compressor-B on the Marco Polo TLP. Two Platform Operators drained condensate from the 1st Stage Suction Scrubber into the compressor skid in an attempt to restart the compressor. Hydrocarbons were released and vapors reached an unknown ignition source, possibly static charge, causing a fire that spread to CLX cable trays throughout the facility. The fire was eventually controlled with AFFF and BC fire suppressants after the firewater system proved ineffective on the secondary fire.

incident__event__summary: On December 20, 2020, a 4,725-pound joint of 14-inch casing fell across the rig floor of the Transocean Deepwater Invictus drillship after the clamp type elevators (CTE) unlatched when the joint was lowered to the rig floor during troubleshooting of casing slips. The RBC panel operator prematurely functioned the CTE to 'slips up' position during troubleshooting, which allowed the elevators to open and release the casing when weight was removed. No injuries occurred, though an unauthorized Red Zone entry was made by a casing crew member who escaped unharmed.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement dual-control foot pedal (or equivalent) requiring both Driller and RBC panel operator input to open CTE slips; Review and relocate Rack Back Console system placement to area outside Red Zone; Reinforce Red Zone access control procedures and authorization requirements

incident__event__top_event: Dropped Object - Sheared Bolts and Falling Actuator Plates
incident__event__summary: On January 18, 2015, during drilling operations at 2140 hours, the PS-1000 drill pipe slips set unexpectedly while the driller was rotating at 180 RPM after making a connection. The sudden stop of the drill string created immense torque that was transferred to the MDDM (Modular Derrick Drilling Machine), causing bolts on two of the I-BOP's actuator plates to be sheared. One plate fell approximately 30 feet to the bridge-racker walkway, and the other fell approximately 130 feet to the rig floor. Each plate weighed 19 pounds. No personnel were in the impact areas and no injuries occurred. The root cause was a combination of poor weather conditions that prevented rig repositioning and equipment failure where downward force from the drill string overcame the hydraulic pressure holding the slips open.
incident__event__recommendations: Install bushing saver and guide to prevent future slips closure; Modify MHWirth/Aker I-BOP actuator plates and lever arms to prevent contact during drilling operations

incident__event__summary: On January 25, 2015 at 0100 hours, an actuator plate weighing 19 pounds fell approximately 90 feet from the top drive of the MDDM on the T.O. Deepwater Invictus rig. The bolts securing the upper actuator plate had sheared due to tight tolerances between the actuator plates and lever arms, allowing impact during drilling operations. No personnel were injured as all crew members were outside the Red Zone at the time of the incident. The equipment manufacturer identified the root cause as design tolerance issues and implemented corrective actions including machining and repositioning of components.
incident__event__recommendations: Machine grind areas at risk for impact on plates and lever arms; Reposition fork assembly for lever arm to provide additional clearance; BSEE notified all rigs with same equipment to make appropriate corrections

incident__event__summary: On April 11, 2017, while picking up riser to begin well 003 in Green Canyon Block 627, the riser gantry crane (RGC) on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet dropped a joint of riser approximately 19 feet in the riser bay. The RGC operator received a false 'Green Light' signal indicating proper capture when the hooks were not fully engaged. When the remote console was powered down for reboot, the aft crane hook lost hydraulics and released the riser joint, which fell approximately 15 feet and struck additional riser joints before falling an additional 4 feet to the bottom of the riser bay. No personnel were injured due to barricading of the work area.
incident__event__recommendations: National Oilwell Varco issued product information bulletin for inspection and correction of RGC riser capture device; Adjust position of hydraulic proximity switches to indicate when crane hook locks are fully engaged; Add 'Yoke Landed' criteria to software to ensure full capture of riser joint; Update Job Safety Analysis and work procedures to require visual confirmation that forward and aft hooks are fully engaged prior to lifting

incident__event__top_event: Arc flash in electrical cabinet DBU 7
incident__event__summary: On May 26, 2017, smoke alarms in the port 690 V switchboard room of the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet drillship triggered a required muster. An arc flash occurred in Dynamic Braking Unit (DBU) 7 due to blown fuses that had not been replaced since May 10, 2017. Carbon buildup in the fuses created a path for electrical current, enabling arcing through an IGBT switch. The arcing destroyed the fuse housing and jumped to the cabinet frame, causing an arc flash that destroyed the cabinet and activated smoke alarms. Estimated damage was $50,000.
incident__event__recommendations: If DBU fuses blow in the future, the maintenance crew should replace them immediately to prevent reoccurrence

incident__event__top_event: Employee struck by suspended casing joint
incident__event__summary: On August 7, 2016, while running casing on Chevron's PN010 well in Green Canyon 640, a Roustabout was struck in the torso by a 16-inch casing joint that shifted during repositioning of dunnage on the rig's forward conveyor system. The employee was forced over a 42-inch handrail and landed on a riser yoke spreader bar. The Banksman pulled the injured person back to safety. The incident occurred because crew failed to recognize hazards when repositioning dunnage in close proximity to a suspended load, and personnel were not removed from the area before lifting operations continued.
incident__event__recommendations: Redesign dunnage to prevent separation (four pieces of conveyer belt bolted together); Review all Work Risk Assessments involving tubular handling operations; Implement additional control measures for tubular lifting operations in Health and Safety Manual; Ensure personnel are removed from conveyor system area before lifting operations begin

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 645
incident__event__summary: On June 27, 2022, at approximately 1755 hours, a fire occurred inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Northeast side of Green Canyon Block 645 Platform A (Holstein). The fire was caused by improperly wrapped Panther felt on Zone 4 deluge wiring, which allowed slag from hot work to smolder inside the wrapping. The fire was discovered when the firewater pump activated and deluge Zone 4 was triggered by the burning wires that controlled the deluge system. Contributing factors included only one Firewatch being present during the 30-minute cool-down period instead of the required two, and fire and smoke detection systems being disabled when the hot work control panel was turned off.
incident__event__recommendations: Add a second Firewatch to PWE hot work operations to remain on station for the full 30 minutes after hot work is complete; Add the ability of the control room to monitor the status of at least one fire and one gas detection at all times; Separate power supply for fire and smoke detection so that monitoring can continue even after hot work has concluded

incident__event__summary: On February 3, 2021, at 02:00 hours, a Nabors Offshore night Crane Operator operating the West knuckle boom crane on the Holstein Spar Rig was transferring a single joint of 5.5-inch drill pipe (31.5 feet long, 810 pounds) from the pipe conveyor to the pipe rack. The Crane Operator gripped the pipe after only 2 seconds instead of the manufacturer-required 5 seconds, and the gripper feet were not in full contact with the pipe before gripping. When the operator abruptly stopped the boom swing, the pipe gripper shook and bounced erratically, causing the drill pipe to slip out and fall approximately 13 feet onto the pipe rack. The pipe then bounced and slid over the handrail into the Gulf of Mexico. No injuries were reported. Investigation determined the cause was operator error: failure to follow manufacturer's operating instructions regarding grip time and proper gripper foot contact.
incident__event__recommendations: Upgrade knuckle boom crane with visual and audible indicators to confirm proper gripping force to Crane Operator; Provide additional training to all Crane Operators with the upgrades; Ensure proper crane operation procedures are followed according to manufacturer's recommendations

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Load - Drill Pipe Fall
incident__event__summary: On February 14, 2021 at 0330 hours, a stand of 5.5-inch drill pipe fell approximately 4 feet from the Pipe Racking System (PRS) gripper to the rotary table during drilling operations on the Holstein Spar Rig. The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper was initiated to the closed position, but the pipe was not actually properly latched. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by lack of a positive latch indicator on the PRS panel and failure of the rig crew to visually verify that the drill pipe was properly gripped, despite JSA recommendation to do so.
incident__event__recommendations: Update JSA and Standard Operation Procedure to assign a required flagger while operating the PRS to ensure pipe is properly gripped prior to racking back drill pipe; Evaluate availability of upgrading the PRS with a positive latch indicator

incident__event__top_event: Thermal burn injury from ruptured sight glass
incident__event__incident_type: Other Injury - Burns to the body
incident__event__summary: On November 7, 2015, an Engine Room Operator sustained first and second degree burns on approximately 40% of his body when an inspection glass on a Marine Sanitation Device (MSD) vacuum pump ruptured, ejecting hot fluids and fractured glass. The operator was troubleshooting an alarm on the MSD when the incident occurred. The direct cause was excessive scale buildup in the discharge line causing overpressurization of the pump, as no pressure relief valve or rupture disk was installed to prevent overpressuring.
incident__event__recommendations: Install pressure relief valves or rupture disks on MSD equipment to prevent overpressuring; Install pressure and temperature gauges on equipment to assist crew during inspection and troubleshooting; Develop formal procedures for troubleshooting MSD alarms; Ensure manufacturer's Operations Manual is available to crew; Redesign equipment to prevent operator body placement in line of fire from inspection glass

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 743
incident__event__summary: On January 15, 2021, at 15:26 hours during drilling operations on the Seadrill West Vela in 6,534 feet of water, an 8-pound leveling cone and a 104-pound bell guide insert fell approximately 90 feet from Frank's International spider type casing elevators to the rig floor. The incident occurred when the casing became wedged between the VersaFlo tool and the bell guide due to slight misalignment. The elevators tilted on the Top Drive System bails, causing the leveling cone to shear off and the bell guide insert to dislodge. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by misalignment between the VersaFlo tool and bell guide, combined with inadequate secondary retention devices and excessive weight on the elevators.
incident__event__recommendations: Evaluate and adjust camera placement on rig floor during casing runs to improve visibility; Update Seadrill Step-by-Step Procedure to include equipment verification step by Assistant Driller or Driller during rigging up of casing elevators or VersaFlo tool to ensure proper alignment; Frank's International to install more robust secondary retention on leveling cones; Frank's International to explore additional options for secondary retention on bell guide inserts; Update pre-rig up check list to include additional alignment checks during casing run operation; Seadrill to share lessons learned from incident to other vessels in fleet

incident__event__top_event: High pressure fluid release striking worker in face
incident__event__incident_type: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Fluid Release
incident__event__summary: On June 9, 2015, a Subsea Engineer was struck in the face with high pressure BOP fluid while attempting to replace O-rings on a Sub Plate Mounted (SPM) valve on the BOP stack during stump testing operations. incident__event__top_event: Unintentional discharge of Synthetic Oil Based Mud
incident__event__summary: On September 13, 2017, the Mad Dog Spar Rig unintentionally discharged 71 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico due to improper valve alignment while cleaning the mud pill pit. The Derrickman and Floorhand used a valve line-up checklist to verify valve positions, but an error occurred. When the overboard discharge valve was opened and the pump activated, SBM was pumped overboard for approximately eight minutes before the pump was turned off and the valve closed. A leaking splitter valve may have been a contributing factor but was not the primary cause.
incident__event__recommendations: Improve valve line-up verification procedures to prevent recurrence; Enhance training and awareness for personnel performing valve alignment tasks

incident__event__top_event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic oil-based mud into the Gulf of Mexico
incident__event__summary: On February 27, 2021, approximately 13 barrels of synthetic oil-based mud (SOBM) containing 6.24 barrels of synthetic oil were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a wash water overboard transfer operation on the Diamond Ocean Black Hornet drilling rig. The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment at the mud mix manifold combined with inadequate communication during a tour change. The #1 mud mixing pump was left running while circulating mud in the #11 and #13 active mud pits, but this critical information was not properly communicated to the incoming crew. When the overboard discharge valve was opened, mud was discharged instead of wash water. The incident was detected when the Driller noticed volume loss in the active mud pits and immediately notified the Derrickhands, who shut down the pump and closed the valve.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement Fluid Transfer Pre-Checklist to ensure both mud mixing pumps are switched off prior to valve alignment; Improve handover documentation and communication procedures during tour changes; Ensure proper line-up checks by walking the line to confirm appropriate valves are open and others are closed prior to opening the overboard discharge valve

incident__event__top_event: Personnel struck by suspended manual tongs
incident__event__summary: On February 10, 2017 at 0223 hours, a Rowan Floorhand was struck in the back by manual tongs while assisting with pipe tripping operations on the drillship Rowan Resolute in Green Canyon Block 859. A hydraulic hose on the hydra-tong had ruptured, requiring the crew to switch to manual tongs. The manual tongs were suspended by a hoist line and secured by a half-inch tie back rope. As the driller lowered the top drive to latch onto pipe, a clamp on the kelly hose caught the hoist line, hoisting the tongs higher. When the driller stopped the top drive, the tie back rope broke and the tongs swung toward the rotary table, striking the Floorhand who was standing in the permanent Red Zone facing away from the suspended load. The Floorhand sustained a bruise but no broken bones or internal injuries.
incident__event__recommendations: Stop the job when there are changes to a planned job to determine all risks involved; Discuss the hazards of the Red Zones with crew members

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 895
incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to marine riser and LMRP during hurricane evacuation
incident__event__summary: On October 28, 2020, the Transocean Deepwater Asgard drillship was forced to evacuate location due to Hurricane Zeta. Despite initial forecasts indicating the storm would pass to the east, the hurricane's actual path brought it directly over the vessel. The decision to remain connected to the well until the last moment, combined with inaccurate weather forecasting, resulted in the drillship being pushed off location by extreme winds (peak gust 152 mph). During evacuation, the marine riser impacted the hull and the LMRP struck the seafloor twice, causing approximately $5.7 million in damage to the riser, LMRP, telescopic joint, mux cables, and subsea pods.
incident__event__recommendations: BOE to recommend well securing, riser displacement, disconnection, and relocation to safe zone for storms projected to reach Category 1 Hurricane sustained winds or greater; Modify rig contract requirements to mandate riser, BOP, and tensioner system analysis for Category 1 Hurricane conditions between June 1 and December 1; BOE to compare projected versus actual weather conditions with weather service provider to eliminate conflicting information; Transocean to revise Extreme Weather Evacuation Plan (EWEP) to ensure compliance with operational limits; Transocean to request bathymetry maps to identify hazards in safe zone transit routes; BOE to modify Hurricane Evacuation Plan to include risk assessment and barrier evaluation considering time constraints

incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of man rider during personnel lift operation
incident__event__incident_type: Personnel handling incident - man rider malfunction
incident__event__summary: On February 17, 2025, an Expro Americas Inc. employee (AP) was lifted approximately 100 feet on the forward man rider to tighten a leaking hydraulic hose on the Coil Tubing Lift Frame (CTLF) aboard the Noble Globetrotter drillship. After completing the task and signaling for descent, the man rider began descending at an accelerated rate. The hoist operator attempted to control the descent by moving the control to hoist position and attempting to activate the emergency stop, but AP reached the rig floor before intervention could be effective. Investigation revealed that the Original Equipment Manufacturer's Programming and Control Logic (PCL) had been altered from "Default" to "Warning" settings, allowing system faults to be overridden and preventing brake engagement. Contributing factors included lack of preventive maintenance, environmental degradation of equipment, inadequate pre-use inspections, and administrative deficiencies in the Control of Work permit.

incident__event__top_event: Dropped Load - Steel Plate Striking Personnel
incident__event__summary: On December 25, 2024, a 340 lbs steel plate disengaged from a magnetic heavy lifting device on the Noble Drillship Blackhornet while being lowered to the deck. The plate fell 47 inches and struck both a Noble welder (IP1) and a Noble roustabout (IP2) on the top of the left foot. Both personnel sustained swelling and contusions and were evacuated to University Hospital. Investigation determined the magnetic heavy lifting device was defective with damaged pole shoes causing air gaps that reduced lifting capacity. Contributing factors included inadequate job safety analysis, lack of timeout for safety (TOFS), failure to review hazards, and work environment constraints that created time pressure.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Houma District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a safety alert/bulletin regarding this incident

After placing the eighth pipe section on the top tier, the 2E left to retrieve a U-bolt for securing the pipe while the IP knelt to rig the next section. The unsecured eighth pipe section rolled off the rack, fell approximately 3 feet, and struck the IP's right middle finger, resulting in amputation of the fingertip at the DIP joint. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Green Canyon Block 243
incident__event__summary: On January 14, 2019, during temporary abandonment operations on well SS002 aboard the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana, the riser adapter failed and allowed the BOP stack to fall to the seafloor. The incident occurred when the Subsea Supervisor reduced riser tension to 440 KIPS instead of the agreed 650 KIPS while unlatching the wellhead connector. This reduced tension caused the BOP stack to tilt approximately 45 degrees, placing excessive bending loads on the riser adapter. The riser adapter partially ruptured immediately and fully failed 17 hours later, causing the BOP stack to fall. The well was already secured with multiple plugs and cement, preventing pollution.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Houma District recommends issuing a Safety Alert to raise awareness about utilizing proper rig procedures when disconnecting; Seadrill implemented preventative action to limit all rigs to 50 KIPS when disconnecting from wells; Operators should establish safe working parameters for setting down weight by slacking off riser tensioners; Engineering analysis and simulation should be performed to determine maximum and minimum safe set down weights

incident__event__summary: On September 11, 2016, at 0826 hours, the Senior Dynamic Positioning Officer (SDPO) aboard the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet mistakenly activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS) while the Blow Out Preventer (BOP) stack was suspended approximately 1,550 feet below the water line during a planned pull to surface. The EDS activation caused the BOP stack to fall approximately 2,330 feet to the sea floor. The incident resulted from human error triggered by confusion between similar acronyms (EDS vs ESD), similar personnel names, lack of pre-job planning, and informal EDS testing practices. The BOP stack landed safely in the designated safe zone approximately 484 feet from the wellhead with no damage to the rig, no personnel injury, and no environmental impact.
incident__event__recommendations: Include Bridge personnel (DPOs) in pre-job, face-to-face notifications before tests are conducted; Require a Subsea Engineer to be present at each EDS station during tests; Place a sign over EDS buttons when the BOP is unlatched warning that these buttons are not to be touched while unlatched; Require the Offshore Installation Manager (OIM), Rig Superintendent, or Tool Pusher to be notified prior to EDS tests; Consider placing an inhibit switch on the EDS that would only be used when the BOP is unlatched; Revise ESD test procedures so that Onward no longer supervises the weekly ESD test; responsibility transferred to Anadarko Lead Drilling Supervisor; Station Anadarko employees and the rig's Tool Pusher at the three ESD stations during ESD tests; Do not conduct ESD tests while the BOP stack is unlatched

incident__event__summary: On March 21, 2017, at approximately 10:00 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform. The Flash Gas Compressor No.1 shut in on a Level Safety High High alarm on the 1st Stage Scrubber. During restart attempts, the operator discovered a fire between the engine block and starter. The fire was caused by fuel gas escaping from a cracked starter signal return line, which ignited upon contact with a spark possibly caused by the Bendix on the starter engaging the fly-wheel. The operator shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a fire hose. No reportable injuries occurred. The damaged signal line, starter, flex piping, and associated hoses/piping were replaced.
incident__event__recommendations: The Houma District has no recommendation for the Regional Office

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Green Canyon Block 680
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Buoyancy - Well A-1 Dry Tree and Riser Descent
incident__event__summary: On December 27, 2017, the Well A-1 riser on the Constitution Spar (GC 680-A) dropped 3-4 feet onto the workover deck grating. On December 31, 2017, during air-up operations on Buoyancy Can Chamber #2, an air leak was detected. The Well A-1 dry tree subsequently descended 28 feet onto the spar deck. Root cause investigation revealed severe corrosion of the air fill-up lines in Chambers #2 and #3, allowing nitrogen to escape and seawater to enter the chambers. Loss of buoyancy in these chambers caused the weight of the dry tree and riser to exceed the buoyancy capacity of the remaining chambers, resulting in structural collapse of the workover deck grating and descent of the wellhead assembly. Estimated damage: $4,700,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Houma District issued no specific recommendations to the Regional Office; Inspections both visually and via mini ROVs in the centerwell are being performed on Constitution and three other APC truss spars (Boomvang, Nansen, Gunnison) to ensure no other corrosion problems exist

incident__event__summary: On April 29, 2017, at approximately 1:12 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform in the Gulf of Mexico. Vibration from the Flash Gas Compressor No. 2 engine caused stainless steel coolant tubing to rub against a retaining bolt head, creating a hole. Coolant sprayed onto the engine exhaust piping, which was covered with protective heat blankets. Once the blankets became saturated with coolant, the hot exhaust ignited the coolant, causing a fire. Operators responded immediately, shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a handheld portable fire extinguisher. No reportable injuries occurred.

incident__event__incident_type: Structural Damage / Crane Lifting Device Incident
incident__event__summary: On 29-OCT-2015 at 1740 hours, the Deck Coordinator sustained severe crush injuries to his left hand while attempting to relocate two joints of damaged production tubing on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet. The tubing was being set on an uneven bundle of 6 5/8 inch piping in the pipe bay area. Despite warnings from two Roustabouts that the surface was not level and the tubing would roll, the Deck Coordinator continued the operation. When the tubing began to roll, the Deck Coordinator positioned himself between the tubing and a cargo box and attempted to wedge angle iron under the tubing. The weight of the tubing caused the angle iron to flip, crushing his left hand. The tubing continued to roll, pinning him between the cargo box and tubing until the crane lifted the tubing to free him.

incident__event__summary: On May 8, 2018 at approximately 2115 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred on sales gas meter #3 at the Atlantis platform in Green Canyon Block 787-A. The release was discovered by third party personnel and lasted approximately 30 minutes. The root cause was the installation of incorrect Teflon seals instead of required Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) during a transmitter replacement 18 days prior. The seal failed at approximately 2100 psi. This was the fourth gas release incident on BP facilities since 2008 and the third on Atlantis, all due to failed Teflon seals. Contributing factors included lack of knowledge by the technician, poor oversight, inadequate training, bypassed gas detectors, insufficient detector placement, and deficient emergency response procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement process for managing third party equipment owners doing work on BP facilities; Ensure third party technicians receive sufficient training prior to performing work on facilities; Verify that third party technicians follow OEM procedures during equipment installation; Install additional gas detection sensors as recommended in 2011 and 2015 F&G assessments; Improve gas detection system placement and coverage; Enhance emergency response procedures and personnel training; Improve safety culture and communication between BP leadership and technicians

incident__event__top_event: Flash fire in waste oil incinerator tank
incident__event__summary: On December 10, 2016, at 05:40 a.m., a flash fire occurred inside the waste oil incinerator tank on the Pacific Drilling Sharav drillship operating for Chevron in Green Canyon block 807. The fire was caused by failure of the waste oil level switch to shut down power to the internal heating element when the waste oil level became too low. The exposed heating element ignited fumes inside the tank, causing a flash fire that distorted and ballooned the tank. One isolation valve was damaged and approximately 1 gallon of waste oil was released to secondary containment. No injuries or environmental threat occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Raise the suction line inside the tank above the heating element to eliminate the possibility of future flash fires

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Grand Isle Block 116
incident__event__summary: On December 3, 2017 at 2215 hours, a fire occurred at the Fieldwood Energy LLC Grand Isle 116-A platform. During normal operations, operators observed a flame coming from the #2 seawater lift pump electric motor. The fire was caused by a bearing failure in the pump motor that resulted in excessive heat buildup, igniting lube oil in the motor housing. The fire was contained using a purple K fire extinguisher and the platform fire water system. Root cause was identified as inadequate preventive maintenance and monitoring of the seawater pump motors, combined with a lube oil leak that caused the reservoir level to drop.
incident__event__recommendations: Monitor all lube oil reservoir levels as part of maintenance and monitoring program

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Oil Leak from Tubing Connection
incident__event__summary: On 16 July 2019 at 0700 hours, approximately 4.8 barrels of oil leaked from a 3/4" broken tubing connection (Swagelok fitting) on the gas lift bleed ring of the casing valve at Well R-22 on the unmanned GI 22-R platform. The leak was discovered when an operator on nearby GI 22-L observed a sheen north of the platform. The platform had been shut-in since 28 June 2019 for maintenance, but pressure remained on the gas lift line and well casing. High seas from Hurricane Barry (14-16 ft with occasional 21 ft seas) caused well movement that sheared the tubing. Contributing factors included inadequate tubing support, improperly functioning check valves in gas lift mandrels, incorrect installation of a Flow Safety Valve upstream rather than downstream of the tubing connection, and cleanup procedures that exacerbated pollution to a wildlife refuge.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE NOD recommends that OSM and OII develop a safety alert to address tubing supports. Tubing supports should be installed in such a way as to prevent stress and sheering.

incident__event__incident_type: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
incident__event__summary: On January 26, 2018, at the GI 43 AP Platform in the Gulf of Mexico, a loss of containment of hydrocarbons and gas releases occurred. Control Room Operators experienced abnormal operating conditions due to sand and sludge buildup and chemical flocking affecting level sensors. Safety devices were placed in bypass to prevent equipment shutdown while operators flushed level bridles. A hole was discovered in the Gas Vent Header Piping at 1:18 am leaking hydrocarbons. The leak was temporarily sealed with duct tape and absorbent pad. Due to communication failures and personnel falling asleep, the platform continued flowing until 6:45 am when a sheen was observed and a field-wide shut-in was initiated. The incident resulted in a 10-mile sheen and discharge from 5 locations on the platform. Root causes included sludge buildup, collapsed internal packing, failed sump pumps, safety device bypass, and failure to use Stop Work Authority.
incident__event__recommendations: Fieldwood submitted Corrective Action Plan #19115; Fieldwood announced new safety culture initiative 'Safe and Sound' emphasizing Stop Work Authority

incident__event__summary: On 18-AUG-2021 at 1300 hours, a contract crane operator employed by Danos was performing a pre-use inspection of a crane on platform AC-CMP at Grand Isle Block 43. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Grand Isle Block 76
incident__event__summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District Office referred findings to BSEE regional pipeline division for further review of Fieldwood Energy's pipeline modifications; BSEE Accident Investigator referred discrepancies on Safety Function Diagram (SFD) and Safety Analysis Function Evaluation (SAFE) chart to Production Operations Section Chief for operator correction

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Grand Isle Block 47
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Gas Release
incident__event__summary: On 25 April 2025 at approximately 1330 hours, a Louisiana Safety Systems, Inc. Valve Technician was performing maintenance on a control valve located on a 6-inch gas line at GI 47 AP platform. While disassembling the valve, a sudden pressurized gas release occurred, causing the valve bonnet flange assembly (approximately 50 pounds) to strike the technician in the left chest and abdomen area. The technician was knocked backwards onto an elevated scaffolding platform and possibly lost consciousness. The technician sustained a laceration behind the ear and an anterior rib fracture of the 8th left rib. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate communication between the operator and contractor, failure to obtain required work permits and PIC approval, failure to verify zero pressure on the gas line through proper energy isolation procedures, use of unapproved scaffolding without fall protection, and non-adherence to manufacturer recommendations for valve disassembly.
incident__event__recommendations: Management to meet with GI 47 AP leadership to review incident learnings and contributing factors; Site leadership must ensure tasks involving pressurized systems are authorized by PIC with appropriate documentation prior to work beginning; Third-party service personnel working on pressurized systems must be accompanied by operations group member to verify double-block and bleed and line bled-down verification; Scaffolding contractors to post red caution tape around unapproved scaffolding as additional visual indicator; Task JSA development to include collective discussion of all hazards and proper mitigation through clear communication; Task JSAs to include mitigation of maintaining four nuts on bolts to prevent flange dislodgement from unforeseen pressure releases; Management to develop and distribute Safety Alert describing case findings, learnings, and prevention actions; BSEE to consider issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident

incident__event__top_event: Dropped load - drill pipe and elevators falling from v-door to catwalk
incident__event__incident_type: Structural damage; required evacuation; lost time accident
incident__event__summary: During drill pipe lay-down operations on the Hercules 264 rig, a 3.5 inch drill pipe joint and single joint elevators fell from the v-door to the catwalk, striking two rig personnel (Floor Man and Roustabout) on the left shoulder/arm. The failure was caused by an improper-sized cotter pin on a 4-point shackle that came loose. Root causes included failure to properly rig up elevators, use correct cotter pin size, develop a rigging checklist, conduct pre-job rigging inspection, and incorporate crane crew in the Job Safety Analysis.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, High Island Block 154
incident__event__summary: On 22-Jan-2015 at approximately 1454 hours, the United States flagged Motor Vessel MAERSK KENTUCKY (container ship) collided with Energy XXI's High Island 154 'B' platform. The vessel had dropped anchor in the Fairway Anchorage area approximately 0.9 miles northeast of the platform. The collision occurred on the Starboard side of the container ship in the vicinity of Bay 38. The platform was shut in and unmanned at the time of the collision. The wells, surface equipment, and pipelines were in a condition which prevented a pollution event. The boat landing, caisson riser, various process vessels and associated piping, support beams, handrails, grating, and navaids sustained significant damage estimated at $4,000,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Improve anchor watch procedures and monitoring protocols for vessels in fairway anchorage areas; Enhanced weather monitoring and response procedures for vessel bridge teams; Faster engine startup procedures or alternative vessel maneuvering capabilities

incident__event__top_event: Flash fire at crane engine air intake
incident__event__summary: On 11-April-2018 at 17:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at the crane engine air intake blower during fuel system bleed-down operations at platform HI 22 A. Two Shamrock Energy Solution Mechanics were performing crane engine overhaul. After exhausting stored air pressure, facility Operators opened a valve sending natural gas to the fuel gas system to supply additional pressure for the starter. On the third cranking of the starter, a flash fire occurred at the engine air intake blower. One Mechanic sustained 1st and 2nd degree burns to hands, arms, face, and ears. The root cause was failure to vent the crane engine starter discharge to a safe area, combined with lack of communication that the starting system utilized natural gas instead of compressed air, and absence of a Management of Change procedure when the facility's air system was modified to a gas system.
incident__event__recommendations: Office of Safety Management to evaluate the incident; Safety Alert when using natural gas versus air

incident__event__top_event: Battery explosion with chemical burn injury
incident__event__summary: On 06-AUG-2014 at 0200 hours, the main platform generator at Energy XXI's High Island A368 'A' platform shut down due to severed belts. Two contract operators attempted to start the backup diesel generator. When the 'A' Operator removed the cover from the second battery box to check terminal connections, sparks from a loose battery terminal ignited accumulated hydrogen gas in the battery box, causing an explosion. Battery acid was blown into the operator's face and eyes. The operator was evacuated by helicopter and treated for chemical burns.

incident__event__summary: On 3-Jan-2014 at 05:15, a fire was detected in the exhaust piping of a rental 1000 KW generator located on the drill deck. The Contract Night Operator spotted flames, announced the fire, and pulled the engine ESD, which shut down the engine and shut in the platform. No injuries or pollution resulted.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of pipe string down wellbore
incident__event__summary: During Plug and Abandonment operations at High Island A 446 A on 4-DEC-2019 at 0704 hours, a crew member unscrewed the top joint of a 3-1/2 inch work string. When the crane operator lifted the unscrewed joint, it became caught on the last thread section, causing the bowls and slips to rise and unseat. Upon release, the remaining work string fell back down at an angle. The safety clamp broke loose during impact, allowing the bowls to open and the remaining work string (approximately 346 feet) to fall down the hole. The work string impacted the cement retainer 72 feet below mudline. Root cause was failure to verify complete separation of the joint before signaling the crane operator to lift. Contributing factors included failure to document blind lifts in the JSA, failure to secure bowls to the Pacman plate, failure to secure slip handles, crew distraction during the lift, and inadequate procedures addressing blind lifts.
incident__event__recommendations: Revise procedure for pulling and breaking out tubulars to include visual verification of complete disconnect before hoisting; Revise procedure to include blind lift situations and communication methods; Disseminate Safety Alert to all personnel detailing incident and corrective measures

incident__event__top_event: Personnel basket struck crane boom cradle and personnel fell from basket
incident__event__incident_type: Crane/Material-Handling Equipment Failure with Personnel Injury
incident__event__summary: On June 12, 2015 at 20:00 hours at platform HI A 537 B, during evacuation due to approaching tropical storm, a crane operator picked up a Billy Pugh personnel basket carrying four personnel. While swinging the basket over the helideck and lowering it toward a boat, the crane boom continued to swing northward despite the operator attempting to stop it with the swing brake. The crane was operating at 75 percent power instead of full throttle as designed. The operator raised the basket to place personnel back on deck, but the basket struck the crane boom cradle and became entangled. One person fell approximately 10-12 feet onto a Marine Portable Tank, landing on face and stomach and losing consciousness. A second person fell on the boom cradle sustaining minor injuries. Two remaining personnel stayed on basket and climbed down using boom cradle.
incident__event__recommendations: Lake Jackson District had no recommendations to Regional Office at time of report

incident__event__top_event: Worker struck by falling drill line cable
incident__event__incident_type: Struck by object / Lifting device failure
incident__event__summary: On 24-MAR-2014 at 1630 hours, during stringing down block operations on Platform D (Blake 1505 rig), a 1 3/8 inch drill line cable failed to remain in the snake grip and fell, striking a worker manning the hydraulic drill line spool winch control on the left shoulder and right hip. The drill line cable was not properly degreased before insertion into the snake grip, and improper clamping using manila rope and duct tape instead of manufacturer-specified methods contributed to the failure. An 'all stop' was called but insufficient time remained to prevent the incident.
incident__event__recommendations: Lessee will assure that manufacturer's specifications and instructions for use of a snake grip are followed completely during stringing down block operations

incident__event__summary: On 06-Jun-2014, a contract employee of Infinity Valve working for McMoRan was tasked with swapping out a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) by removing mounting bolts. A grinder with a cutting wheel was used in a small work space with limited visibility instead of a reciprocating saw. The grinder was not discussed in the Before Job Safety Analysis (BJSA). The employee was not comfortable using the grinder but did not invoke Stop Work Authority to complete the job quicker. The cutting wheel became bound in the confined space, causing the grinder to kick back and shatter the wheel. The blade contacted the back of the employee's left hand below the ring finger, causing a 4-inch laceration. The employee received 30 stitches and is expected to make a full recovery.

incident__event__top_event: Worker struck by make-up tongs during tripping operations
incident__event__summary: On May 10, 2015, during back-reaming while tripping out of the hole operations on the Hercules 300 jackup rig, a Floorhand was struck on his right arm by a set of make-up tongs which failed to remain secured to the drill pipe. The Floorhand was medically evacuated and later determined to have a radial fracture on his right arm. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety Analysis, lack of established safe standing zones, inefficient crew communication, absence of required supervision, and equipment failure of the Top Drive grabber dies.
incident__event__recommendations: All JSA's need to be reviewed and verified to insure all hazards associated with job tasks have been identified; While utilizing tongs, an area on the rig floor needs to be designated for Floorhands to safely stand after the connection has been made for all operations utilizing tongs; Discussions during safety meetings should emphasize the importance of good working communications between crew members during operations; Stop Work Authority should have been utilized to assure the injured Floorhand was clear of the tongs prior to breaking and spinning out of a connection; Supervision from experienced personnel who are not responsible for a specific task during back-reaming operations can help prevent undesired events with potential of injury incidents

incident__event__summary: On 16 March 2015 at approximately 14:48 hours, a backfire/explosion occurred at Chevron USA's High Island A582 'C' facility during platform startup after monthly Emergency Shut Down testing. Production Operators were bringing the platform back on line when the purge timer on Ambitrol Heater EAL-1510 was observed not counting down. Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - High Island Block A 379
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Consciousness in Confined Space
incident__event__summary: On September 4, 2023, at approximately 09:15 hours, an injured person (IP) entered the NBK-1070 Heater Treater vessel on platform HIA 379 B without a Supplied Air Respirator (SAR), wearing only an Air Purifying Respirator (APR). The IP was overcome by toxic fumes and passed out face down in the vessel fluids and sludge. A pump operator noticed the IP missing and alerted the cleaning crew supervisor. The supervisor entered without a respirator, was also overcome, and had to exit. A construction supervisor then entered without respiratory protection, extracted the IP, and CPR was administered for approximately 20 minutes before the IP began breathing. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated in intensive care for three days. The incident resulted from failure to follow the confined space entry plan, including no tri-gas testing, no hole watch, improper PPE, and no rescue equipment setup.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure all Subcontractors adhere to work plans; Ensure all Company and Subcontracted personnel are properly trained before task/work is started; Ensure accurate incident reporting is made, to include follow up reports

incident__event__summary: On May 26, 2022, BSEE inspectors observed a sheen approximately 30 yards by 200 yards in Gulf of Mexico waters originating from Cox Operating's High Island A 582 C facility. Investigation determined hydrocarbons were discharging from the incoming KAQ 2700 high pressure 8-inch wet gas pipeline segment number 13380 at the plus 10 deck level. The source was identified as a one-quarter inch diameter hole in the pipe due to corrosion. An estimated total of 129.145 gallons of oil was discharged during the bleed down process. The pipeline was repaired with a clamp and returned to service on June 17, 2022.

incident__event__top_event: Structural failure of Billy Pugh Derrick Basket during personnel lift
incident__event__summary: On 14-OCT-2014 at 01:05 hours, during preventive maintenance on the Aft Pipe Racker System, a Short Service Employee was hoisted in a Billy Pugh Derrick Basket to a height of approximately 110 feet. The basket hung up on an unknown part of the derrick/PRS causing it to tilt. Despite radio communication of an 'all stop' command, the basket continued to climb 3-4 inches before stopping. A loud 'pop' was heard and the rider discovered major damage to the single main support arm and two lower arm braces had separated. The rider used the anchor line for support and called for emergency descent. When the basket reached the rig floor, the hanging arm completely separated from the basket.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Keathley Canyon Block 147
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Casing Shoe Failure
incident__event__incident_type: Pollution - Unauthorized discharge of drilling mud
incident__event__summary: On 1 April 2015, the SeaDrill West Vela drill ship under contract to BP reported a dark area identified by ROV approximately 400-500 feet from the wellhead at Well 001 (Tiber #3) in Keathley Canyon Block 147. Samples from the seafloor showed gelatinous material resembling filter cake with 2% oil content, consistent with Synthetic Based Drilling Mud used during the 16.5 inch hole section drilling. The incident resulted from a 22 inch casing shoe failure at 8630 ft md/tvd set in a salt formation. From 18-22 January 2015, approximately 500 barrels of 14.4 ppg synthetic oil base mud were discharged into offshore waters during 14 inch intermediate casing running and cementing operations. Total mud losses during casing operations reached approximately 4802 barrels.
incident__event__recommendations: Management should create a Condition of Approval (COA) by the District Operations Support (DOS) Section to place on all drilling permits requiring operators to contact the district before continuing operations when extreme losses are experienced during drilling and casing running operations in any hole section

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Keathley Canyon Block 770
incident__event__top_event: Dropped drill pipe from aft pipe transfer conveyor
incident__event__summary: At approximately 08:10 hours on 6 January 2016, a joint of drill pipe fell from the aft pipe transfer conveyor onto the aft pipe deck of the Transocean Discoverer India drill ship during drilling operations for Chevron U.S.A. in Keathley Canyon Block 770. The drill pipe became trapped under a utility cart during loading arm operations, causing it to roll and fall over handrails, creating a 5-inch diameter hole in the aft deck. No personnel injuries occurred due to area barricading. The incident resulted from failure to follow work risk assessment procedures, inadequate safety time-outs, and lack of hazard assessment for utility cart positioning.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lafayette District recommends that the Office of Safety Management issue a Safety Alert identifying potential hazards concerning positioning of utility carts relative to transfer conveyor loading arms that may result in falling objects

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Spill
incident__event__summary: At 20:20 hours on 30 August 2014, the Transocean Discoverer Clear Leader drill ship under contract to Chevron was conducting drilling operations at Keathley Canyon Block 829 when a loss of synthetic base mud was observed from a telescopic slip joint packer failure. Approximately 12 barrels of 14.5 ppg SBM (6.12 gallons synthetic oil) was spilled into the Gulf of Mexico but was restricted to the moon pool area. Root cause was a leaking stainless steel JIC connection for the air hose at the bottom of the J-plate, with contributing factors including improper hose support, ocean currents causing vibration, and submersion of hose bundle below water line.

incident__event__incident_type: Loss of Containment - Trapped Pressure Release
incident__event__summary: On October 13, 2014 at approximately 1335 hours, an employee was injured while attempting to hydro test a pipeline on a new facility. As the crew began to unscrew cap screws to access a leaking check valve, trapped pressure downstream of check valve 1 (CV1) was released, striking the injured employee in the left side of face and head, rendering him unconscious. The incident resulted from failure to check the gauge downstream of CV1 and inadequate supervision of hydro-testing operations.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Keathley Canyon Block 686
incident__event__top_event: Eye injury from ejected indicator rod
incident__event__summary: On 7 August 2019 at 04:30 hours, a Seadrill subsea technician sustained an eye injury while working on the hands-free gooseneck (HFGN) during well operations. While attempting to retract the kill line HFGN locking pins, an indicator rod was ejected by trapped air and water pressure from within the operator housing, striking the technician in the right eye. The technician was diagnosed with orbital fractures and bruising. The incident resulted from operation of out-of-compliance equipment with known defects, failure to follow OEM guidelines, creation of unsafe workaround procedures, and failure to account for trapped hydrostatic pressure.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE recommends revising the bleed down procedures to accommodate for trapped pressure in choke and kill lines; BSEE Lafayette District recommends a Safety Alert be considered to address the hazards when unlocking the hands-free gooseneck choke and kill line assemblies

incident__event__summary: On April 21, 2018 at 0900 hours at platform KC 875A (Lucius), a contract employee injured his right shoulder while attempting to carry two 20 lb scaffold boards approximately 7 ft. long. The employee repositioned to avoid a cable crossing the walkway and felt his shoulder pop. Initial medical examination found no concerns and the employee was cleared to return to full duty the following day. On April 27, 2018 at 0300 hours, the employee felt his shoulder popping out of place while in his bunk and was transported to a medical facility where an MRI revealed a labrum fracture.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Keathley Canyon Block 875
incident__event__top_event: Electrical fire in drilling switchboard room
incident__event__summary: On 28 May 2019 at 21:00 hours, smoke detectors activated in the Starboard 690V Drilling Switchboard Room aboard the Diamond Offshore Ocean Blackhornet drillship. An electrical fire occurred in the Aux Drawworks B (ADWB) motor drive cabinet, causing severe damage to two Inverter Modules and two Variable Frequency Drives. The general alarm was activated and all personnel reported to muster stations. No injuries were reported. The assessed damage was approximately $180,000. The root cause could not be definitively determined, but possible causes included a faulty Inverter Module, cooling water leakage, or improperly torqued DC fuses.
incident__event__recommendations: Further failure analysis testing of ADWB equipment required to identify exact fault origin; Two Inverter Modules should be sent for thorough failure analysis

incident__event__top_event: Facial injury from pressurized hydraulic line strike
incident__event__summary: On 16 November 2018 at 1530 hours, a Diamond Offshore hydraulic mechanic sustained a facial injury while changing out a Hilman roller on the blowout preventer carrier frame aboard the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk drillship. During the operation, a 30-ton Enerpac hydraulic jack fell due to miscommunication between the hydraulic mechanic and rig mechanic operating the hydraulic pump. The falling jack sheared the hydraulic line fitting on an adjacent 25-ton Enerpac hydraulic jack, causing the pressurized hydraulic line to strike the mechanic's face. The mechanic was evacuated by helicopter and received medical treatment for facial lacerations.
incident__event__recommendations: Install a rigid support system when lifting heavy equipment

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 109
incident__event__summary: An explosion occurred on 14 February 2019 at 0115 on the Mississippi Canyon 109 A (Amberjack) Platform. The explosion originated from a 12 Volt battery located inside a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) Panel used for gas sales measurement and communication. A rechargeable battery had been overcharged due to improper replacement and installation in a non-vented enclosure, causing hydrogen gas accumulation and ignition of electrical components. The panel door was blown off and debris landed 45 feet away. No injuries or pollution occurred. The root cause was flawed equipment design and installation of non-rated electrical components in a hazardous area, compounded by inadequate personnel training on battery hydrogen venting and lack of hazard analysis during design changes.
incident__event__recommendations: The New Orleans District recommends that the Office of Incident Investigations considers drafting a Safety Alert regarding electrical components and hazardous areas

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge of Synthetic Based Mud
incident__event__summary: On 02 July 2015 at 0400 hours, the Rowan Relentless drill ship inadvertently discharged 368 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) containing 67 percent synthetic oil into Gulf of Mexico waters. The discharge occurred when the Derrick Man, without verifying valve alignment, pumped displacement seawater overboard from pits #12 and #14. A manually operated splitter valve separating reserve pit #1 from these pits was left halfway open, allowing SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line. Root causes included poor communication between parties, failure to verify valve alignment prior to pumping, and lack of awareness of company policy regarding sensitive valve lockout procedures.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge of Drilling Fluid
incident__event__summary: At approximately 0055 hrs on 1 April 2019, LLOG Exploration Offshore LLC experienced a spill of 61 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) on the Seadrill West Neptune Drillship during drilling operations at Mississippi Canyon Block 199. The spill resulted from a misalignment of trip tank valve positions. While the Derrickman was transferring mud between trip tanks per driller request, the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was found to be locked in the open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico. The pit cleaning crew had possibly locked open the master dump valve during cleaning operations and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position. Total pollution was 37.8 bbls (61 bbls at 62% synthetic make-up).

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Communication and Catastrophic Failure of Satellite Umbilical Cobra Head Termination
incident__event__summary: On 12 November 2019 at 1200 hours, a loss of communication to MC 208/209 SS001 well occurred at Murphy Exploration & Production Company's MC 254 A Delta House platform. This resulted in catastrophic failure of the satellite umbilical cobra head termination with rupture of one HP and two LP hydraulic supply lines. The root cause was heavy weight and center of gravity offset from the UTH assembly resulting in high shear and bending moment on the MQC plate beyond its reported capacity, with collapse of bolts on the MQC bracket. Contributing factors included temporary buoyancy modules left on cobra heads that were not designed for long-term subsea use, and EFL grounding to the UTH. Approximately 1,000 gallons of hydraulic fluid was released, below EPA reportable threshold. The fail-safe shutdown sequence successfully shut in associated wells.

incident__event__summary: On 9-JAN-2015 at approximately 0600 hours, during a routine lift operation on the NABORS MODS 200 crane at Platform B SIMBA in MC Block 21, the crane boom struck the boom pad causing structural damage to the boom heel. Investigation revealed that the high angle kick-out (boom hoist high limiter) safety device was adjusted all the way in, rendering it non-operational. This device is designed to prevent the crane boom from striking the boom pad. Both day and night crane operators had initialed Daily/Pre-Use Crane Inspection forms from 7-JAN-15 through 13-JAN-15 verifying the high angle boom kick-out was checked for proper operation, despite the device being inoperable.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 243
incident__event__summary: On 18 October 2020 at 0820 hours at MC 243 A (Matterhorn), a crane mechanic's finger was crushed while transporting a 55-gallon drum of hydraulic oil across the platform deck using a hand dolly. The drum and dolly were lifted onto a 12-inch high I-beam which personnel could not pull across. When an operator left to get assistance, the injured person attempted to secure the dolly on the beam. The dolly slipped from the injured person's grip and their right hand middle finger was caught between the dolly handle and the skid beam, crushing the fingertip. The injury required evacuation and amputation of the fingertip.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Mud Discharge Overboard
incident__event__summary: On 12 FEB 2014 between 1630 and 1959 hours, air pressure was lost to the primary Telescopic Joint (TJ) Packer resulting in a 128 barrel discharge of 13.5 ppg Synthetic Base Mud into offshore waters during 14" casing running operations. The air supply line to the TJ packer had ruptured. The air pressure alarm for the TJ packer was disabled with alarm set points at 0-psi for both high and low limits. No automatic redundant back-up system was in place; the back-up hydraulic system required manual engagement.

incident__event__summary: On 6 May 2019 at 0748 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. MC 437-A (Appomattox) platform experienced a partial loss of primary generator power during a fuel swap operation from diesel to natural gas. The Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS-3451) failed to provide backup power due to shorted capacitors and undersized fuses on the inverter capacitor bank. This caused shutdown of the fire and gas processor, preventing emergency generator startup. The UPS was bypassed and power restored. A second power loss occurred at 2147 hours but the emergency generator restored power within 30 seconds with the UPS in bypass. Root cause was identified as turbine generator trip combined with UPS-3451 failure. No injuries or pollution resulted. The facility had not commenced first oil production at the time of the incident.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 437
incident__event__summary: On 30 November 2021 at 1917 hours, a fire occurred in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure at the Appomattox semi-submersible production platform. Flame detectors alerted the control room, and a deck operator confirmed smoke and sparks from the generator housing. The generator ESD was activated, platform ESD initiated, and full muster sounded. Fire teams extinguished the fire with water. Investigation determined that vibration caused loss of fastener preload in the exhaust hatch bolts, allowing hot exhaust to escape and ignite Visqueen (plastic tarp) left by painters, which then melted cables. No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

incident__event__summary: On 15 July 2020 at 1411 hours, the Appomattox platform (MC 437 A) experienced an unplanned gas release while attempting to start up Bulk Gas Compressor #1. Multiple gas detectors alarmed at 2:19 pm, triggering an automatic Process Shut Down. A deck operator detected the odor of gas and the control room operator manually activated the Emergency Shut Down and Muster Alarm. Investigation revealed that level bridle drain valves on the BGC #1 Gas Scrubber had been left open. Approximately 1838 kg of gas was released over a 25-minute period. No injuries or damages occurred. The facility safety system responded in accordance with applicable regulations.
