incident__event__summary: On 29 January 2020 at 1400 hours, a Roller Ram Winch (RRW) mooring chain incident occurred at Shell Offshore Inc's Appomattox platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 437. During maintenance activities on mooring line L-11, the chain links suddenly began paying out from the chain locker. The guide roller broke free from its bearing cradle, and 22 chain links paid out outboard. The chain stopper cylinders, angle transmitter, and Slack Chain Hang-off Unit (SCHU) stopper were forced out of frame. The root cause was identified as human performance error: the maintenance crew assumed the mooring chain was in lock position without visually confirming it. Contributing factors included flawed equipment design lacking proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stopper extension. Total repair and recovery costs were $5,750,000.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE to provide training on mooring for large platforms; Update operating manual to identify how and when load is transferred to SCHU; Strengthen and clarify roles and responsibilities in maintenance procedures; Install additional proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stoppers are fully extended; Permanently reinstate gripper used during installation/commissioning as secondary barrier to prevent unplanned chain payout

incident__event__summary: On 17 April 2021 at 1415 hours, a stack operator was contacted with asphaltene inhibitor on the arms and face while attempting to open chemical injection valves to Well AC-006 on the Appomattox platform. The incident was caused by a loose tubing fitting between an isolation valve and flow element FE 4220-H1, which resulted in chemical spray from the weep hole. The operator was wearing inadequate PPE (no chemical resistant gloves or face shield). The injured person was initially released to full duty by the medic but experienced eye irritation the next day and was evacuated. An onshore physician diagnosed chemical conjunctivitis and ordered 7 days off work, resulting in an LTA > 3 days classification.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop hazardous chemical awareness program; Recognize possibility of leaks from weep holes in Job Safety Analysis (JSA); Require appropriate PPE for tasks involving potential chemical exposure, even when containment is not expected to be broken

incident__event__summary: On 27 June 2020 at 1725 hours, a subsea methanol leak was observed by an ROV operator on the Mississippi Canyon Block 503, Lease OCS-G 27277 SS002 subsea well (E1). The leak originated from the Methanol Injection Tree Valve (MITV) due to internal seal failure. Approximately 107 gallons of methanol was lost during diagnostic testing. The well was shut-in for scheduled maintenance when the leak was discovered. LLOG mobilized repair operations, and on 2 December 2020, after completing repairs, a blind flange was installed to isolate the leaking line and the well was returned to service.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure quality assurance by the manufacture of subsea trees; Isolate the MITV from all pressurized systems and produced fluids

incident__event__summary: On October 31, 2020 at approximately 17:58 hours, a spider dog safety door (grating) fell through the rotary table to the top of the Blow Out Preventers (BOP) on the SeaDrill West Neptune drillship at Mississippi Canyon 503, Well #007, injuring two floor hands. The safety door was dislodged when spider dogs were functioned to the open position while back plates remained in the down position, causing bolts securing the grating to shear. The inadequate secondary retention chain failed to prevent the grating from falling into the well center. One injured person sustained facial lacerations and the other a fractured rib.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 547
incident__event__summary: On 14 July 2019 at 21:50, a fire occurred on LLOG's MC 547 A platform when a 1/2" isolation valve on Pipeline Pump A's discharge piping developed a crack. Hydrocarbons sprayed onto the hot turbo exhaust system and ignited. The TSE activated the ESD system, all production shut in, and firewater pumps activated. A 3-man fire team extinguished the fire in 16 minutes. No injuries occurred, but pollution was detected on deck and structural supports. The root cause was improper installation of the isolation valve without adequate support, causing fatigue damage from high vibration service.

incident__event__top_event: Hand laceration and finger fracture from pinch point during pump installation
incident__event__summary: On 13 September 2019 at 1530 hours, an injured person sustained a laceration and fracture to the left index finger while assisting with installation of a flow assurance pump on Deck 7 of the Gulfstar 1 platform. The injury occurred when an air-operated chain hoist jerked upward while the IP was using an alignment tool to position piping flanges, causing the IP's hand to be pinched between the piping and alignment bar. The incident was attributed to inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis, congested workspace, poor hand placement, and the jerking motion of the air hoist.

incident__event__top_event: Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil based mud overboard
incident__event__summary: On 13 February 2015 at 1115 hours, a 260 barrel discharge of 15 ppg Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) occurred at MC 762 on the Stena Forth. The Derrickman intended to transfer salt water from active pit #2 to active pit #4 but was unaware that SOBM from equalized active pits #1 and #3 had flowed into active pit #2 due to failure of the air actuator for active pit #2 and the suction valve for active pit #3. Poor communication between the Derrickman and Mud Loggers, combined with equipment failure and lack of continuous pit monitoring, resulted in the unauthorized discharge.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement continuous monitoring of pit volumes during all fluid transfer operations; Establish robust communication protocols between Derrickman, Mud Loggers, and Driller; Conduct regular maintenance and testing of air actuators and suction valves; Require verification of pit contents before initiating transfers; Ensure personnel remain in the Mud Control Room during active pit operations

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 727
incident__event__top_event: Well Control Incident - Unintentional Shearing of Fluid Loss Control Devices
incident__event__summary: On January 9, 2021, at approximately 06:00 hours, Kosmos Energy experienced a well control incident on Seadrill West Neptune during completion operations on the SS003 well. While displacing wellbore completion fluid to packer fluid, the downhole Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) unintentionally sheared the upper and lower Fluid Loss Control Devices (FLCD), resulting in cross flow between completed intervals and fluid losses of 600 barrels per hour. The well was subsequently shut in and killed over a multi-day operation. The incident was attributed to inadequate risk recognition, poor communication, insufficient execution and supervision, and incomplete procedures.

incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to high pressure gas lift piping during mezzanine deck installation
incident__event__summary: On 6 March 2015 at 1615 hrs, during installation of the West Gas Lift Mezzanine Deck (6,500 pounds) on Murphy's MC 736 A Platform, the load shifted and jammed between high pressure (1300 psi) gas lift piping and a 4" isolation valve. The incident resulted from failure to follow engineering design specifications for lifting equipment placement, non-use of hold back hoist and tag lines, and non-installation of stabbing guides. Attempts to unjam the load caused the gas lift piping to bow and create a 5/16" gouge in the isolation valve body. No injuries or hydrocarbon releases occurred. Property damage exceeded $50,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Document lifting operations over high pressure piping as critical lifts; Ensure specified locations for chain hoist and sling placement are followed per engineering designs; Use hold back chain hoist as planned to control load; Use tag lines as required to direct and control load; Pre-install and use stabbing guides for lift deck legs; Enforce stop work authority when incidents develop; Isolate and bleed down high pressure piping prior to lifting operations over pressurized lines; Conduct adequate hazard identification for lifts over high pressure equipment

incident__event__summary: On 15 September 2015 at 0510 hours, a fire occurred inside Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) #39 at Murphy Exploration MC 736-A platform during hot work operations. Hot slag from torch welding rolled under the Velcro portion of the PWE wall, heated up and ignited. The fire was detected and extinguished by SafeZone personnel using a water fire extinguisher. Contributing factors included improper PWE installation with gaps, lack of readily available fire protection equipment, use of incorrect fire extinguisher type, failure to follow hot work procedures, and delayed communication to control room.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Discharge
incident__event__summary: On 20 June 2015, a 12 barrel discharge of Synthetic Base Mud (SBM) at 11.3 ppg occurred on the Ensco 8500 drilling rig during drill pipe extraction. The upper riser telescopic joint (TJ) seal failed, allowing SBM to discharge into offshore waters. The audible alarm for the TJ seal did not sound because air pressure remained within the normal operating range of 100 psi. The crew detected the visible leak and engaged the lower TJ seal to stop the discharge.

incident__event__incident_type: Structural damage and personnel injury from dropped load
incident__event__summary: On 7 July 2014 at 1940 hours during workover operations on the Seadrill West Vela platform in MC Block 776, a tubing hanger handling tool (THHT) separated from a tubing hanger at approximately 35 degrees angle while being transported. The hanger fell approximately 15 feet to the pipe skate, striking one employee and causing another to fall while avoiding the falling load. Two Seadrill employees were injured: one with lacerations to elbow and bruising; the second with two fractured ribs and lacerated spleen. The probable cause was either mechanical failure of the THHT or improper installation, though this remained undetermined at time of investigation.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 778
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Sea
incident__event__summary: On 13 July 2020 at 1055 hours, a pollution event occurred at BP's Thunder Horse platform (MC 778-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.
incident__event__recommendations: NOD office recommends OII coordinate with BSEE Office of Environmental Compliance/Environmental Monitoring Unit and Oil Spill Preparedness Division to determine agency response to ongoing sheen events and oil and grease limit exceedances for Thunder Horse facility

incident__event__summary: On 2 August 2020 at 1925 hours, BP production operators were preparing to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher KAH-4190 at Thunder Horse platform in the Gulf of Mexico. When operators began cracking open the launcher door, a gas cloud was observed emanating from the top of the launcher. The operators immediately shut the door. The combustible gas detector ASH 962-102, located 3 feet above the launcher, activated and reached 88% of the Low Explosive Limits (LEL). The gas cloud dissipated to 0% LEL within 2 minutes. The incident was caused by oil residue in the pig trap generating a hazardous atmosphere after the launcher had been isolated for 5 days. The launcher was not purged with nitrogen immediately before opening the door, contrary to standard operating procedures. All safety systems responded as designed and no injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure proper procedures are followed which include purging the trap with nitrogen before opening the pig launcher door

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 778
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Produced Water Discharge
incident__event__summary: A pollution event occurred on 4 May 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when approximately 5 barrels of hydrocarbons were released into the Gulf of Mexico from the produced water outfall. The incident was caused by operator error leaving the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 open, allowing 286 gallons of water clarifier to gravity drain into the vessel. This excess clarifier caused a lack of oil/water separation. The issue was prolonged because a faulty level control valve in Float Cell #1 prevented switching to the backup produced water train. The sheen was first detected via satellite imagery on 4 May 2021 and the discharge continued through 9 May 2021.

incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to Hydra-Racker and Top-Drive systems
incident__event__incident_type: Equipment failure due to human error
incident__event__summary: On August 7, 2014 at 0525 hours during pipe tripping operations, the Assistant Driller retracted the Hydra-Racker with elevators still closed and top drive extended. Without communicating with the Auxiliary Driller, the Assistant Driller lowered the top drive with drill pipe still connected to elevators and block. The drill pipe broke out of the Hydra-Racker heads, shearing mounting bolts and causing a 500 lb lower guide arm to fall 12 feet to the rig floor. The incident occurred in the last 30 minutes of the last shift prior to crew change when employees were not focused on the task.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 809
incident__event__top_event: Loss of marine riser and LMRP during hurricane evasion
incident__event__incident_type: structural damage, lost object, pollution event
incident__event__summary: The Noble Globetrotter II Drillship was conducting Temporary Abandonment operations on well P012 in MC Block 809 when Hurricane Ida approached. Despite T-time calculations indicating insufficient time to safely secure the well and evacuate, the operator and contractor proceeded with operations. A crew change delayed the temporary abandonment operation by several hours. The drillship unlatched the LMRP and began pulling riser but encountered over-torqued bolts and hydraulic torque wrench breakdowns. At approximately 7:00 pm on August 28th, the Captain initiated Stop Work with 12 riser joints and LMRP still hanging from the rotary. The drillship attempted to evade the hurricane at speeds of 1-3.5 knots but was unsuccessful against Category 2+ hurricane force winds and high seas. The riser broke below the rotary, sending approximately 11 joints and the LMRP to the seafloor. The incident resulted in approximately 3,700 gallons of miscellaneous oil spilled, one loss-time injury, over $1 million in vessel and equipment damage, and 115 personnel exposed to extreme weather conditions.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Office of Incident Investigations to work with United States Coast Guard District 8 Officer in Charge of Marine Inspection (OCMI) to draft a Safety Alert

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 809
incident__event__summary: On 9 December 2020 at approximately 1806 hours, a gas detector inside the Recycle Gas Compressor (RGC) enclosure detected a gas release from a valve flange on a 1-inch methanol injection line. A nut had backed off one of four bolts holding the valve flange, allowing gas to leak through the O-ring. The gas migrated into the RGC enclosure because the enclosure is not sealed and the flange was within 3 feet of the building. The alarm triggered an automatic shutdown of the RGC unit and platform. Emergency response crews successfully located, isolated, and secured the leak. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution occurred.

incident__event__top_event: Pressure Relief Valve Failure and Projectile Impact Injury
incident__event__summary: On 4 July 2015 at 2120 hours, an Assistant Driller was struck in the left shoulder by the end cap of a pressure relief valve from mud pump #4 on the Noble Globetrotter Drillship. The incident occurred when the Driller failed to verify proper alignment of mud pump #4 to the boost line. The pump was lined up on the #1 standpipe instead, creating a closed system. When the pump was staged to 40 strokes per minute, pressure rapidly increased to 7,919 psi, exceeding the relief valve setting of 7,100 psi. The relief valve actuator housing failed, projecting an 8.5-inch diameter, 1-inch thick, 14-pound end cap that struck the injured person. Investigation revealed no scheduled maintenance had been performed on the pressure relief valves as recommended by the manufacturer.

incident__event__summary: On 6 June 2015 at 2151 hours, a Floorhand (Injured Person) suffered a serious crush injury to both hands while attempting to free a jammed insert carrier lodged in a casing wear bushing. The casing wear bushing unexpectedly dislodged and fell approximately 1.0 to 1.5 feet onto a wooden block, trapping the IP's hands between the bushing and block. The IP's left hand was amputated just below the wrist, and the right hand lost one full finger and two partial fingers. The incident was caused by poor hand placement, failure to recognize hazards associated with changed conditions, and lack of a written Job Safety Analysis.

incident__event__top_event: Unplanned Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) Disconnect
incident__event__summary: On June 7, 2019, during BOP wet verification (stack hopping) operations on the Vito VA001 well, the drill team misinterpreted Step 21 of procedure CG1-OPS-CSP-01-59 Rev. 15. The team applied 100,000 lbs weight to the BOP connector instead of the LMRP connector, resulting in insufficient weight on the LMRP connector. When the Riser Connector was unlatched via ROV flying lead, the LMRP lifted off the BOP while pod receptacles remained energized, causing approximately $500,000 in equipment damage to pod stingers, receptacles, and related tubing/hoses. No spill was recorded.
incident__event__recommendations: Update BOP Wet Verification Procedure to include sign-offs by responsible persons; Include absolute weights at critical steps in procedure; Include formulas for calculating weights at steps with weight changes; Re-emphasize expectations to follow existing work processes such as handovers and Control of Work (COW); Consider developing a hard barrier procedure to prevent procedural control of this function; Issue a Safety Alert

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - O-Ring Failure on Aceco Compact Valve
incident__event__summary: On 16-FEB-2015 at 12:40 hours, the Titan Platform (OCS-G 16661) experienced an O-Ring failure on a 1" 15,000 psig Aceco compact valve on the Telemark (AT 63 No. 4) incoming flowline. The valve was being used during methanol flow-back operations following hydrate remediation. Approximately 438 barrels of methanol with trace amounts of crude oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico, creating a barely-visible sheen over a 1,320' by 10' area. Root causes included improper installation by a Shamrock technician: flange cap screws were not properly torqued, an incorrect outlet flange was installed, and the face seal was not properly verified. No injuries or fatalities occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District made no recommendations to the Agency

incident__event__top_event: Projectile strike injury from pipe drift (rabbit)
incident__event__summary: On 30-Nov-2015 at 0915 hours aboard the Maersk Developer semi-submersible drilling rig, an injured person was struck in the face by a Teflon pipe drift (rabbit) that became dislodged from a vertical drill pipe joint. The incident occurred while making up stands of 5-inch heavy weight drill pipe. The injured person removed the thread protector and, when the rabbit did not exit, signaled the tugger operator to raise the pipe approximately two feet. While looking up into the pipe, the rabbit came free and struck the injured person in the face, causing numerous lacerations and fractures requiring surgery and emergency evacuation.

incident__event__top_event: Firewater system piping rupture and dropped object
incident__event__summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. During recovery from the ESD, multiple procedural failures and equipment issues led to air entering the firewater system. When the Fire Water Pumps were restarted, a water hammer effect ruptured multiple sections of FiberBond firewater piping, causing extensive damage. A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.
incident__event__recommendations: Update Power Generation System Procedure to include opening SDV 2082 after ESD events; Revise Firewater System Operating Procedure to reference correct valve (PCV 6650) instead of non-existent Sea Chest Valve; Update Power Protection and Life Support Systems Procedures to reflect current JWP equipment; Implement requirement to open helideck hose reels to bleed air from firewater system before starting FWPs; Inspect and repair or replace leaking check valve FSV 6650-004; Review all operating procedures to ensure alignment with current platform design; Add equipment and replace faulty equipment as identified

incident__event__summary: On 21 October 2023 at 13:04 hours at Mississippi Canyon 127 A, Horn Mountain platform, personnel attempted a heavy lift of 8,300 pounds using the platform's West Crane. When the lift was 15 feet above the platform deck, two nylon choker slings failed due to contact with a sharp edge on the hydraulic chain jack cylinder, causing the load to fall onto two cargo baskets. No personnel injuries occurred. The root cause was the decision to use nylon choker slings wrapped around the cylinder body instead of the manufacturer-recommended lifting pad-eyes, combined with inadequate hazard analysis that failed to identify the sharp edge as a hazard.
incident__event__recommendations: OII should draft safety alert to inform operators to utilize proper lifting techniques as recommended by the manufacturer

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Dynamic Positioning and Unplanned Emergency Disconnect Sequence Activation
incident__event__summary: On 28 MAR 2025 at 1643 hours, an Able Body Seaman 1 (AB1) conducting routine accommodation fire dampener testing alone mistakenly operated the wrong switch panel in the Forward Emergency Gear Locker, inadvertently actuating emergency fuel shut off valves. This caused sequential diesel generator failures, loss of dynamic positioning capability, and drillship drift off station. The rig floor manually activated EDS, shearing the drill pipe and detaching the LMRP, resulting in discharge of 2436 barrels of brine into the Gulf of America and $2.6M in equipment damage.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement mandatory Task Risk Assessment (TRA) prior to all maintenance tasks; Establish physical barriers and warning signs on emergency fuel shut off valves; Enforce Stop Work Authority protocols when personnel express uncertainty; Require paired work assignments for critical maintenance tasks; Improve panel labeling and spatial separation of critical control systems

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 300/255
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Station-Keeping and Emergency Disconnect Sequence
incident__event__incident_type: Weather-Related Loss of Position / Structural Damage
incident__event__summary: On May 29, 2025, at approximately 10:46 a.m., the Noble Stanley Lafosse drillship experienced an unexpected and severe weather event during cement preparation operations. Forecasts had predicted winds not exceeding 10 knots; however, a thunderstorm developed producing a sudden 60-knot wind gust from the north that shifted to the west. The vessel experienced a 14-meter excursion while hanging off 5-7/8" drill pipe, triggering escalation from yellow alert to red alert. The crew initiated an Emergency Disconnect Sequence, successfully closing blind shear rams and securing the well. Approximately 417 barrels of 10.9 ppg calcium chloride were released to the sea. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by rapid and variable wind conditions that exceeded the rig's station-keeping capacity.
incident__event__recommendations: Enhance weather detection capabilities; Refine operational readiness during critical phases; Continuously improve emergency response protocols

incident__event__summary: On March 5, 2024, at 04:15 hrs, the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms while drilling the AW003 well in MC 391. Wind speeds escalated from 30 kts to 83 kts with rapid directional shifts that were not forecasted. The vessel excursion triggered an Emergency Disconnect Sequence when the vessel approached the red watch circle limit (90.3 ft). During the EDS, three joints of 6-5/8 in drill pipe and an MPD bearing assembly (13,200 lbs) fell from the derrick and struck the drill shack protective barrier. Approximately 2,875 bbls of synthetic based mud (1,667 bbls base oil) was released to the seafloor. Total estimated damages: $1,000,000.

incident__context__materials: ['crude oil', 'produced water', 'emulsion breaker chemical', 'oil and grease']
incident__event__top_event: Pollution event - discharge of oil into Gulf of America waters
incident__event__summary: On 08 February 2025, BP personnel at Na Kika platform began injecting a trial emulsion breaker chemical into the produced water treatment system. The new chemical caused frothing and foaming within production vessels, leading to faulty level readings and liquid carryover. This resulted in approximately 89 barrels of oil and grease discharging from the produced water overboard system into the Gulf of America from 08-15 February 2025. The discharge went undetected for 7 days due to the discharge point being located 80 feet below the water surface. Once BP identified the chemical trial as the cause, they discontinued the trial and returned to the original formula, successfully ending the pollution event.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Production Operations Support Section should reach out to industry to require additional safeguards to detect system upsets more quickly; Develop alternative methods to detect pollution events besides visual observation from the platform, given the discharge point location 80 feet below water surface; Improve monitoring systems to enable faster identification of unfavorable chemical trial results

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 520
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Load Control - Dropped Cleanout Assembly
incident__event__summary: On 07-JUN-2023 at 11:00 hours, a 43-foot cleanout assembly fell approximately 40 feet onto the pipe skate during drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean BlackHornet Drillship at MC 520. The incident was caused by incorrect elevator inserts being installed (5-7/8 inch inserts instead of 4-1/2 inch inserts required for Internal-External Upset pipe). The Driller and Assistant Driller failed to follow procedures and did not complete the Elevator Pre-use Checklist. A floor hand was present in the red zone during the incident but was not injured. Property damage included 2 hoses and a portable ladder valued at $500.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure Elevator Pre-use Checklist (CHKL/001) is completed before all lifting operations; Implement positive checks including actual measurements of elevator inserts to override temporary marks; Designate Assistant Driller as responsible person for elevator pre-use inspections with verification by Driller; Verify correct insert size is installed in elevators before lifting operations; Physically measure internal bore of elevators to confirm correct size for tubular in use; Restrict personnel from red zones during lifting operations

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 547
incident__event__summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The fire was caused by failure of a 90° elbow on the oil separator piping, which allowed oil to spray into the enclosure and ignite on hot surfaces. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. No injuries, environmental impact, or platform damage occurred. Damage was limited to the rental air compressor and minor charring of adjacent generator enclosure wiring. Cost of repairs/replacement: $31,969.09.

incident__event__summary: On 16 April 2025 at approximately 1500 hours, a 15-pound cable roller measuring 66 inches long and one inch in diameter broke off from the boom of an East Crane (700L-140) on the Medusa SPAR platform and fell 50 feet to the deck below. The cable roller struck a hydraulic hose on the deck, damaging it beyond use. No injuries occurred. The root causes were inadequate preventive maintenance (failure to repair corroded cable rollers identified in October 2022 inspection) and human error (failure to barricade the area beneath the crane to prevent personnel access to the drop zone).
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations should consider issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident; Install barricades or other means of preventing personnel from entering potentially hazardous areas in the immediate surroundings and drop zone areas before starting work on cranes where dropped object potential is identified; Provide more thorough description of possible dropped objects in JSAs; Require barricading of work areas prior to performing work on cranes; Remove crane boom from facility to eliminate possibility of additional components falling

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 607
incident__event__top_event: Dropped riser joint onto conveyor skate
incident__event__summary: On March 19, 2024, at 13:30 hrs, during BOP deployment/riser running operations on the Valaris DS-18 drillship in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, riser joint #31 was dropped onto the forward conveyor skate. The incident occurred when the riser cart shuttle brake valve wire was loose, causing a fuse to blow and preventing the cart from freewheeling. As the driller continued to hoist the riser joint, one end came out of the conveyor bucket and the Cameron Hydraulic Riser Running Tool (HRRT) mandrel parted, allowing the riser joint to fall approximately 28 inches onto the conveyor. The incident resulted in damage to the riser skate tailing arms, skate lift bucket, HRRT, and riser joint floatation, with estimated property damage of $418,000. No injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Job template should be more detailed on how to inspect all load path components of the HRRT

incident__context__operator: Kosmos Energy Gulf of Mexico Operations
incident__event__summary: On June 5, 2024, at approximately 15:38 hours, a nitrogen cylinder ruptured inside a twelve-cylinder bottle rack aboard the Helix Q5000 during workover operations. The rupture caused damage to other bottles in the rack and scattered them across the main deck. Three personnel sustained minor injuries from shrapnel. The root cause was corrosion of the cylinder wall due to inadequate paint coating protection in the marine environment, combined with design deficiencies including lack of protective barriers between cylinders and rack, and inadequate drainage. Visual pre-fill inspections prior to offshore deployment did not identify the corrosion because the rack was not disassembled for thorough inspection of individual cylinders.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE recommends the Office of Incident Investigations consider a Safety Alert to warn industry about the risk that these bottle racks pose; Install nitrogen generation system to eliminate reliance on external rental systems; Install physical barrier and hard piping to mitigate exposure to personnel and equipment during potential future failures; Change vendor and adhere to elevated inspection process informed by lessons learned

incident__context__materials: ['hydraulic fluid', 'methanol', 'calcium bromide completion brine', 'synthetic based oil']
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
incident__event__summary: On January 6, 2023, at 13:00 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. experienced a pollution event at Mississippi Canyon 943 where subsea well PN003-ST2-BP1 released approximately 16.61 barrels of fluid mixture (hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, and synthetic based oil) to the environment. The leak originated from loss of integrity in the Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) control lines, with fluid being released through the High Pressure vent on the subsea tree. The leak was detected and stopped by closing control valves SV1 and SV2 via ROV. The well was secured with 2 barriers and remains shut in with continuous pressure monitoring.
incident__event__recommendations: Tubing to be pulled and analyzed to identify specific causes of failure; New tubing to be re-run before bringing well back online; BSEE to engage with Shell's analysis to inform policy and procedure to prevent future reoccurrence

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 807
incident__event__summary: On 24 February 2023 at approximately 1115 hours, a Mechanic employed by Helmerich & Payne was attempting to install a hydraulic line on the Pipe Handling Machine (PHM) at MC 807A (Mars TLP). When the combination wrench being used was determined to be too long to access the fitting, the Mechanic decided to modify the wrench by cutting it shorter using a manual hack saw. The wrench was placed in a vise to stabilize it during cutting. As the final cut was made, the Mechanic's weight shifted, causing his hand to come down onto the freshly cut sharp edge of the wrench, resulting in a laceration between the ring and little finger. The Mechanic received first aid onsite and was evacuated to an onshore medical facility where sutures were applied. The Mechanic returned to the platform on 26 February 2023.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure pre-job safety and operation meetings adequately identify required tools before work begins; Implement requirement to stop job and reassess risks when new exposures are recognized not covered in JSA; Enhance supervision to ensure workers do not modify tools without approval; Ensure workers discuss tool modifications and risks with supervisors and coworkers before proceeding

incident__event__incident_type: Slip/Trip/Fall with Lost Time Injury (>3 days)
incident__event__summary: On 23 February 2025 at 0830 hours, a SafeZone worker slipped while securing felt and cleaning inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Ursa platform at MC 809. The worker lost his footing on an elevated working surface, fell 12-18 inches, and his right leg contacted a jagged edge of an I-beam, resulting in a 3-to-4-inch laceration. The worker was medically evacuated and received 11 staples and 4 stitches. Investigation found that jagged edges and uneven surfaces were not identified in the initial JSA hazard assessment, and supervision was inadequate during the pre-job hazard identification process.
incident__event__recommendations: Issue a Safety Alert regarding the incident; Conduct more thorough hazard assessments of work areas; Ensure all personnel are made aware of identified hazards before starting work; Mitigate identified hazards before work commences; Improve supervision during hazard assessment and mitigation processes; Enhance documentation of hazard analysis and job procedures on JSAs

incident__event__top_event: Pressurized hose whip causing personnel injury
incident__event__summary: On February 13, 2024, at approximately 22:35 hours, two Tetra filtration crew members were injured on the Transocean Deepwater Proteus drillship during completion operations on the SS005 well. While attempting to repair a small leak in a 6-inch camlock connection, the Tetra employee released the camlock levers. Pressurized fluid in the hose caused the hose to blow off the deck flange and strike two personnel. One worker received facial impact and a fractured tooth; the second worker was struck and fell against a pipe, sustaining bruising and chemical exposure.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure proper PPE is worn during operations involving pressurized lines and chemical exposure, including chemical-resistant clothing and gloves in addition to eye protection; Implement hazard identification procedures to identify trapped pressure risks during line flushing operations; Establish and enforce procedures to completely drain and bleed pressure from lines before disconnecting hose connections; Install whip checks around all camlock connections being disconnected to prevent hose whip injuries; Provide adequate training and supervision for short-service and inexperienced personnel working with pressurized systems

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 941
incident__event__summary: On 10 May 2023 at approximately 0930 hours, two workers employed by Grand Isle Shipyard were removing bolts from a flange on a 2-inch Well Equalization Header line during a scheduled shut-in at MC 941 Platform A (Titan). As the workers loosened the last bolt, trapped pressure within the line escaped and sprayed the two workers in the face with a small amount of hydrocarbon fluid. The release activated a nearby gas detector, sounding the platform alarm. No injuries, pollution, or equipment damage occurred. The incident was caused by oversight of residual fluids and trapped pressure in a dog-leg bend above the flange during the isolation process.
incident__event__recommendations: Review isolations to look for any possibility of hydrocarbon release from dog-legs or dead-legs due to residual fluid static pressure remaining after lockout/tagout is completed, even if zero pressure is indicated on the gauge; Implement engineering controls for residual fluids or static pressure release; Ensure personnel review isolations for potential hydrocarbon release from dog-legs or dead-legs even if gauge indicates zero pressure

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea MEG Leak
incident__event__summary: On July 11, 2018, at 1200 hours, an estimated 17,098 gallons of Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG) was discharged subsea from the Na Kika Platform (MC 474-A) following repair work on PZZ 2591/92 MEG pumps. The leak occurred through leaking Automatic Operated Valve (AOV) and Shut Down Valve (SDV) into the EA2 umbilical circuit. The leak went undetected for approximately 4 days (July 11-15, 2018) due to subsea location and pollutant dissipation before reaching the surface. The incident resulted in an unauthorized discharge violation and failure to notify BSEE.
incident__event__recommendations: Perform survey to determine whether any other out-of-service (OOS) lines should be positively isolated or documented; Positively isolate the EA-2 MEG line; Consider removing SDV from Sempcheck and safe charts; Include topsides interfaces for hydraulics, chemical services, and HMI in well P&A plans; Confirm no metallurgy issues for other subsea kit currently in service; Consider monitoring methods for earlier detection including real-time unallocated MEG calculation displayed on HMI; Survey existing services shared with multiple wells for passing AOVs and SDVs and ensure proper labeling and recording

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 474
incident__event__summary: On May 4, 2019, at 1351 hours, a partial loss of power occurred at BP Nakika Platform (MC 474-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. The root cause was rainwater ingress into the ESS-2 safety panel due to an unprotected warehouse roof under repair. The rainwater disrupted the ESS-2 processor, which handles critical safety functions including electrical power management. The emergency generator failed to start automatically due to a faulty lube oil temperature switch. Crew bypassed the switch and manually started the generator. All production safety systems functioned as designed. No evacuations, injuries, or pollution occurred. Power was restored at 1431 hours.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Drilling Fluid Release
incident__event__summary: On 1-Dec-2018 at 03:35 hours, the drillship Pacific Sharav experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, causing a forced vessel excursion. The vessel crossed the Dynamic Positioning Red Alert threshold at 03:38 hours. At 03:39 hours, the Senior Toolpusher manually activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS-3), which actuated the Blowout Preventer Casing Shear Rams and sheared the drill pipe. The LMRP was disconnected from the BOP at 03:40 hours, resulting in the release of 1,673 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud into Gulf of Mexico waters. The well was secured with no indication of flow verified by ROV at 06:18 hours.

incident__event__top_event: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
incident__event__summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.
incident__event__recommendations: Update start-up procedures and quality control checks to ensure proper sequencing of subsea well ramp-ups to account for large volumes of chemicals; Increase communication between offshore operations and onshore production leadership; Review and revise produced water handling system procedures to account for increased water production; Implement proper sequencing of well startups from unplanned shutdowns

incident__event__summary: On September 18, 2017, at 1100 hours, the Thunder Horse Platform (MC 778-A) experienced a complete loss of electrical power including emergency power. The incident was triggered by a UPS-B inverter failure on September 17, followed by a static bypass switch opening due to frequency synchronization tolerance mismatch between the Emergency Generator and Turbine Generators. The facility's Vessel Management System incorrectly maintained an abandon platform alarm state when rebooted, causing repeated power losses. A carbon monoxide gas release was detected in the UPS-B battery room. The incident resulted in the evacuation of 265 personnel and took approximately 24 hours to restore emergency power and several days to fully restore facility operations.
incident__event__recommendations: Create or revise a 'Black Start' procedure that includes complete loss of power to the VMS and/or PLC; Consult with OEM to evaluate and make appropriate recommendations for UPS protective features; Review the project engineering handover process for critical electrical systems to ensure personnel are properly trained to operate new equipment; Create or review a platform evacuation procedure specifically prompted by a facility blackout; Verify all necessary SEMS documentation is up-to-date and accessible in hard-copy format; Review 30 CFR 250.842 regarding changes to Facilities Electrical One-line drawing constituting a Facility Safety System Permit Review; Review the performance of critical ventilation systems relative to their basis of design and develop a strategy to address findings

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 778
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Hydraulic Fluid and Well Fluid Release
incident__event__summary: On 22 October 2020 at 13:59, MC 778 A (Thunder Horse) experienced an unplanned power outage when line-of-sight gas detectors near the air compressor header activated, triggering a Process Shut Down and subsequent Emergency Platform Shut Down. Upon restart and troubleshooting, loss of hydraulic fluid was discovered at Subsea Well MC 776 TG002, caused by leaking SCSSV rod piston seals. An estimated 3 to 50 barrels of Transaqua Hydraulic Control fluid and/or hydrocarbon entered the Gulf of Mexico. The SCSSV was re-opened and hydraulic control lines were re-pressurized to prevent further loss of containment. ROV inspection confirmed no fluid flowing from the SCSSV vent. A function test on 21 November 2020 confirmed the SCSSV failed to function open. The well is currently secure with subsea tree valves in the closed position.
incident__event__recommendations: Raise awareness for SCSSV rod piston seal failures in Gulf; Encourage operators to report known SCSSV rod piston seal failures to BSEE under 30 CFR 250.803; Encourage operators to train Control Room Operators to identify rod piston seal failures by monitoring irregular hydraulic pump cycling, irregular hydraulic fluid consumption, and abnormal hydraulic pressure; Before evacuating for hurricanes, operators should bleed all flowlines sub-ambient to prevent rod piston leaks from causing well fluid loss of containment; Explore opportunities for Best Available and Safest Technology (BAST) around mitigating pollution risk from sub-sea hydraulic venting

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Well Control Event
incident__event__summary: On June 2, 2019 at 14:30 hours, the Seadrill West Capricorn Semi-Submersible Drilling Rig encountered a well control event while drilling the Taggert #3 Well in Mississippi Canyon Block 816. The drill crew observed increased return flow with increased gas at the surface (max gas=1386 units). After closing the BOP upper annular and diverter bag, approximately 4 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) were diverted to the Gulf of Mexico waters. No injuries occurred. incident__event__summary: On 24-Nov-2014 at approximately 0830, an operator at Fieldwood Energy's Matagorda Island 622 'C' platform was injured when investigating a flow rate indication problem on the #4 Production Separator. While attempting to verify orifice plate installation, the operator failed to close the equalizer valve between the upper and lower chambers of the Senior Orifice Fitting prior to loosening clamp bar bolts. The operator was transported by helicopter to hospital where corneal surgeries were performed. Full recovery is expected.

incident__event__summary: On March 14, 2017 at approximately 2330 hours, a diesel-driven cement pump on the Lift Boat Jamie G Eymard experienced engine over-speed during decommissioning operations, causing fire to erupt from exhaust stacks. The right bank turbo charger had failed, allowing engine oil to be injected into the air intake. incident__event__summary: On October 2, 2015, at approximately 20:25 hours, a flash fire occurred during decommissioning activities on Matagorda Island 685, Platform A, OCS-G-04548. Tetra Offshore welding personnel were cutting slots in the B-1 conductor for installation of lifting plates when an Oxylance burning bar ignited flammable gas trapped inside the 13 3/8 inch x 9 5/8 inch casing annulus at the plus ten deck. Initial combustible gas checks at the cellar deck level and wellhead detected 0% LEL, but failed to detect trapped gas in the annulus. The flash fire burned for approximately 30 seconds before self-extinguishing. No injuries, pollution, or property damage were reported.
incident__event__recommendations: All decommissioning activities involving cutting of tubing and/or casing with hot work should require testing and verification that tubing/casing are inert and free of flammable substances; May include circulation with inert fluid, venting, and/or creating access for purging to ensure comprehensive combustible gas testing where routine monitoring cannot detect trapped combustible gas; Reference Safety Alert Notice Number 153 (February 12, 1988): cutting tubing during removal with torch does not conform to safe and workmanlike practices

incident__event__summary: On May 5, 2018, at 0745 hours, a crane incident occurred at Cox Operating LLC's MO Block 872 A platform. During the second lift, a 4000-pound tote tank containing 550 gallons of diesel was being offloaded from M/V Diamond to the platform. While lowering the tote tank into a containment skid, one of the four sling legs came loose at the swage ferrule due to a manufacturing defect. The wire rope parted, causing shock loading on the remaining three sling legs, which also failed. The tote tank fell to the deck, spilling a small amount of diesel into the containment skid. No injuries or pollution to the Gulf of Mexico occurred. Investigation revealed the sling had a manufacturing defect: a 3/8 inch ferrule was installed on 5/16 inch wire rope when 3/8 inch wire rope was specified.
incident__event__recommendations: Explore the viability of expanding BSEE's crane inspections to include slings

incident__event__summary: On 25 December 2020 at approximately 18:00, a backfire occurred while attempting to start the compressor engine at MP Block 153 B platform. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Main Pass Block 283
incident__event__top_event: Load handling injury - crate slid off dolly striking worker's foot
incident__event__summary: On 8 May 2021 at 1100 hours at MP 283 A, two personnel (mechanic and electrician) were moving a 100-pound crate 30 feet across deck grating using a hand dolly to load it into a cargo box. Due to the crate's rectangular shape and size, it could not be balanced equally on the dolly. As personnel pulled the crate across the deck and encountered a 4-inch step-up 2 feet from the cargo box opening, the crate slid off the dolly. The injured person (mechanic) lost grip on the dolly handle and it impacted his right foot just behind the steel toe, causing a contusion. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and placed on light duty for greater than 3 days.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure hand dollies are equipped with securing straps to secure loads during transport; Perform Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to identify potential hazards before task execution; Document risk assessments for manual handling tasks

incident__event__summary: On 2-FEB-2015 at 0230 hours, a flash fire occurred on Stone Energy's OCS-G 01665 Main Pass 288-A Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The Kimray Temperature Controller on the BAP-1500 Line Heater malfunctioned, causing excessive temperature rise above the normal 150 degrees setting. The Temperature Safety High (TSH) sensor failed to shut off fuel supply and inflow of combustible fluids. This caused Glycol and Antifreeze mixture to boil over and spill onto the lower deck, dripping onto the Compressor exhaust and igniting. The fire was extinguished by operators using a fire extinguisher and water hose. Contributing factors included a metal plate blocking the burner sight glass port, preventing visual inspection for abnormal conditions, and the Pressure Safety High (PSH) sensor also failing to function.

incident__event__top_event: Explosion of blasting cap during testing
incident__event__summary: On September 22, 2019, at 06:55 hours, an Electric Line Operator (ELO) at McMoRan's Main Pass 299 A facility sustained puncture wounds to his right-hand fingers, left forearm, and left torso when a Green Detonator blasting cap exploded during continuity testing. The ELO used an incorrect testing device (megohmmeter) instead of the proper galvanometer, and did not place the blasting cap inside the safety loading tube prior to testing. incident__event__summary: On 21 September 2021 at approximately 1550 hours, an Emergency Shut Down occurred at MP 42 D/L/M facility. A leak in the ESD/TSE loop caused loss of main power and rendered both primary and secondary seal oil pumps inoperable. Combined with a liquid carryover event in the first stage suction scrubber, produced gas bypassed the wet seals and entered the lube oil tank. The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Main Pass Block 64
incident__event__summary: On 9 October 2021 at 0910 hours, the reciprocating gas compressor (CBA-0900) at MP 64 A #1 experienced an explosion in the 3rd stage discharge piping between the discharge bottle and heat exchanger during startup after shutdown. The explosion was caused by oxygen intrusion into the compressor inlet suction stream through two leaking valve cover gaskets on the 1st stage compression suction cylinder. The oxygen mixed with process gas and was auto-ignited by high temperature discharge gas from the 3rd stage cylinder. The operator mistakenly interpreted an initial ignition noise as an engine backfire and restarted the compressor rather than shutting down for mechanical diagnosis. External damage was limited to a 3-inch piping section and insulation. No injuries occurred and no personnel mustered.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Load Control - Equipment Drop
incident__event__summary: On 03 May 2025 at 0830 hours, during a blind lift operation to install an Electric Submersible Fire Water Pump (ESFWP) at MP 153 B platform, a Fiberbond pipe flange contacted an I-beam beneath the top deck. This contact caused the piping to separate at the flange connection, resulting in the ESFWP and 25 feet of attached pipe falling 4 feet to the deck below. The load struck and penetrated steel grating 1-2 feet. Minor damage occurred to deck grating, Fiberbond piping, and the ESFWP. No injuries or pollution resulted from the incident.

incident__event__top_event: Thermal burn injury from heated Glycol splash
incident__event__summary: On 3 July 2023 at 1545 hours, an Island Operating Company employee was testing the Level Safety High/Low (LSHL) on the Glycol Reboiler (EBC 2151) at MP 270 Platform B when a blockage in the bridle occurred. As the IP cleared the obstruction, heated Triethylene Glycol at 350°F drained out of the bridle and splashed onto the IP, resulting in severe 2nd degree burns to approximately 27% of the body (abdomen, arms, and side). The IP was evacuated and treated at an onshore medical facility, then placed on restricted work duty for three days.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure proper PPE is worn when working with hot liquids and chemicals in accordance with MSDS requirements; Improve Job Safety Analysis to identify thermal hazards associated with hot liquids and chemicals; Include 'Other Hazards' section in JSA to address potential blocked or plugged equipment scenarios; Provide thermal-protective PPE suitable for 350°F liquids rather than ambient temperature protection only

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Main Pass Block 311
incident__event__top_event: Rupture of pressurized gas lift injection tubing with personnel injury
incident__event__summary: On 12 March 2023 at 1633 hours, two Louisiana Safety Systems technicians were injured on MP 311 B platform while performing valve maintenance on well QAY B-18ST. A 1-inch stainless steel gas lift injection tubing, rated at approximately 3100 psi, ruptured due to 4330 psi of unexpected pressure trapped in the tubing. One technician suffered a serious injury resulting in lost time greater than 3 days; the other sustained minor injuries. The incident was caused by: (1) failure to monitor casing pressure, (2) failure to perform pre-job hazard walkthrough, (3) failure to perform required casing diagnostic, and (4) discrepancies between approved drawings and actual field installation. Root cause was downhole well integrity issues that resulted in casing pressure buildup following a grease zerk repair in July 2021.
incident__event__recommendations: Generate Safety Alert emphasizing importance of accurate critical pressure data readings; Ensure operators of dry trees on gas lift verify that tubing and valves are rated per approved drawings; Implement mandatory pre-job hazard walkthroughs before commencing work; Establish routine casing pressure monitoring at required intervals; Perform casing diagnostics when trigger pressures are reached; Verify facility piping and tubing installation matches approved drawings; Implement physical pressure gauge verification instead of relying solely on SCADA system readings

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, Main Pass Block 42
incident__event__summary: On 27 April 2025 at 1520 hours, a 12-bottle nitrogen rack on the top deck of MP 42 Delta platform experienced an explosion. One nitrogen bottle with corrosion at its base ruptured, causing the 'daisy-chained' tubing and isolation valves to be ripped from the remaining bottles, launching them across the platform. Four bottles went overboard into the Gulf; seven were scattered on deck. One bottle remained severely damaged in the aluminum rack. No personnel were injured. Damage included the aluminum rack, welding machine door, and section of solid decking. The bottles were at least 1 year overdue for inspection.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement routine inspection and maintenance program for nitrogen bottles used offshore; Perform proper hazard analysis for nitrogen bottle storage and use; Inspect all nitrogen cylinders on platforms across the Gulf to identify similar deficiencies; Ensure nitrogen bottles are inspected on schedule to prevent corrosion-related failures

incident__event__top_event: Crane boom structural damage and loss of load control
incident__event__summary: On 15 October 2019 at 0755 hours, a crane boom dropped from a 45-degree angle onto the boom rest at Talos Petroleum's Main Pass 288 A platform during unscheduled maintenance. The boom was being lowered using an unapproved emergency load lowering procedure with an improper brake release tool (a 1/4" threaded bolt instead of the required fixed-head nut tool). The spiral lock ring in the brake actuator failed, causing loss of load control and the boom to fall approximately 30 feet, resulting in severe structural damage to the outermost 20-foot boom mid-section. No injuries or damage to process equipment or platform structure occurred. Root causes included inadequate communication between operator and contractor, lack of proper documentation and approval for the procedure used, and mechanical failures in the brake assembly.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Natural Gas Release
incident__event__summary: On July 4, 2014 at 1300 hours, construction workers on the main deck heard an unusual noise from the cellar deck. Natural gas was observed leaking from the upstream flange of the Flow Safety Valve (FSV) on the FA-2 flow line segment of the B-001 well. The leak was caused by multiple valve deficiencies: the Manual Master Valve, Surface Safety Valve, and Wing Valve were all leaking, allowing shut-in tubing pressure of 3200 PSIG to reach the out-of-service production header, causing gasket failure on the FSV upstream flange. These valve deficiencies had been previously identified on August 16, 2013 but were not mitigated by management. The facility was evacuated and the well was isolated.

incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to riser skate from dropped spanner joint assembly
incident__event__summary: On July 22, 2022, at 2203 hours, a spanner joint assembly being lifted by tandem lift (topdrive and crane) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced failure of the nylon sling tail line. The assembly dropped approximately 6 feet onto the riser skate deck. Investigation revealed the crane operator failed to create a new Permit to Work and Lift Plan, instead reusing an expired permit for chrome tubing. The nylon sling used was rated for only 5,100 pounds in choker arrangement but was required to lift approximately 15,000 pounds. No injuries occurred due to Red Zone Management and personnel positioning.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Main Pass Area Block 268
incident__event__summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.
incident__event__recommendations: All available historical information will be thoroughly reviewed to determine if potential for increase in stored energy exists on Third Coast pipelines under construction; Additional control measures will be implemented to mitigate substantial movement of pipeline prior to commencing construction work

incident__event__summary: At approximately 1000 hours on 8/01/2021, a fire occurred on Talos Energy South Pelto Block 23 Platform D in the Heater Treater (NBK-2900). A Production Operator noticed black smoke from the stack and investigated, discovering a small fire. The operator and PIC shut in the heater treater, turned off gas, opened the front cover hatch, and used a dry chemical extinguisher to extinguish the incipient fire. Upon inspection, a pinhole leak was found in the fire tube. Investigation determined the fire tube was operating at higher temperatures (110-120°F) than designed (maximum 105°F), and paraffin solids had accumulated on the fire tube due to insufficient heat transfer, contributing to the failure. A pattern of similar fires had occurred in 2016 and 2018. Post-incident modifications included installation of a new fire tube, burner, heat shroud, and updated operating procedures.

incident__event__top_event: Fire and overpressure in Heater Treater burner system
incident__event__summary: On September 23, 2021 at 1010 hours, a Burner Safety Low alarm occurred on the NBK-2900 Heater Treater at Talos Energy's South Pelto Platform. Two operators responded: one at the burner location and one at the master panel. After resetting the fuel shutdown valve relay and relighting the burners, a pressure release occurred when the West side burner was lit. The root cause was identified as restricted airflow in the stack arrestor from a previous fire tube fire on August 1, 2021, combined with insufficient purge time of residual gas before reignition. A second incident occurred on September 30, 2021 during restart, revealing charred wiring on the igniter and ground wires. The burner section was permanently taken out of service.

incident__event__top_event: Fire in Heater Treater Fire Tube
incident__event__summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.
incident__event__recommendations: Add anodes for cathodic protection of shell and fire tube; Construct new fire tube with heat resistant coating; Change chemical program to use emulsion breaker that will not produce calcium phosphate scale; Implement scale inhibitor program to address calcium carbonate scale deposition; Reduce fluid temperature in treater from 120°F to 105°F; Pull and visually inspect fire tube at 60 days, 180 days, and establish ongoing inspection interval based on corrosion rates; Generate and distribute lessons learned to all platforms

incident__event__summary: At approximately 1730 hours on 25-June-2018, an H2S release occurred on the South Pelto 5-B platform during abandonment operations involving flushing of eight platforms and fifteen pipelines. A worker detected an odor of H2S at an open top flow back tank. An H2S detector recorded 100 ppm of gas. All operations were stopped and personnel mustered for safety. H2S readings steadily decreased from 100 ppm to 0 ppm over nine hours. No personnel were injured. Investigation determined that water left in pipelines for over two years prior to abandonment operations allowed high concentrations of bacteria growth, which led to small pockets of H2S gas formation.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Mississippi Canyon Block 809
incident__event__summary: On 20 August 2022 at 1007 hours, a worker discovered a small flame emanating from an unattended oxygen/acetylene cutting torch hanging from a bulkhead in a Safe Welding Area on the Ursa TLP. The worker immediately extinguished the flame using a nearby portable fire extinguisher and notified the Control Room. A general alarm was sounded, full muster conducted, and Fire Team dispatched. No flame, smoke, or damage was observed by the Fire Team. The incident was caused by construction personnel failing to close torch bottle valves, depressurize lines, and fully tighten the torch head valve after switching to a plasma cutter. Sparks from the plasma cutter subsequently ignited acetylene gas leaking from the improperly secured torch. No injuries or pollution resulted.
incident__event__recommendations: Close torch bottle valves when not in use; Depressurize torch lines after bottle valves are closed; Check equipment before work to ensure connections are tight; Personnel utilizing Safe Welding Area should be mindful of spark direction and what is in the area

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Mississippi Canyon Block 940
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
incident__event__summary: On 8 May 2022 at 1130 hours, a sheen of unknown origin was observed on the water at Mississippi Canyon Block 940, OCS-G 31534. The leak originated from Subsea Well MC 940 (Vito) VA007, a completed well not yet tied back via pipeline to Shell's Vito facility. The source was identified as a tubing string parting at a coupling in the 5.5" tubing at approximately 5,122 feet below mudline. The leak was stopped within 4 days by closing the SV2 needle valve on the subsea tree. An estimated 7 barrels of oil were released into the Gulf of Mexico between 8 May and 12 May 2022. Shell's response and reporting were found to be in compliance with federal regulations.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE will engage with Shell's analysis of tubing failure to inform policy and procedure to prevent future occurrence

incident__event__summary: On February 25, 2014 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract employee fell approximately 90 feet from the rear of a crane into the Gulf of Mexico. The employee was cleaning oil from a skid when he lost his footing on a slippery beam due to humid and foggy conditions. The employee was rescued after grabbing an emergency escape rope on the north side of the facility and suffered severe injuries including a forehead laceration, broken nose, and broken tailbone.

incident__event__top_event: High-pressure fluid release causing facial injury
incident__event__summary: On May 20, 2019 at approximately 1400 hours, an employee sustained injuries to his nose and face while attempting to close a 1/2" ball valve on the Gas Lift Fluid Slug Catcher (MBD-150) operating at 1200 psi. incident__event__top_event: Structural damage to crane boom heel section
incident__event__summary: On 7 September 2022, damage to the boom heel section of the SM 192A platform crane was discovered during a monthly facility inspection. The damage consisted of bent vertical lacings and uniform bends in both upper chords of the boom heel section. Investigation revealed that the high-angle boom kick-out safety device had failed to function, allowing the boom to contact the boom stops and sustain damage. The root causes were identified as: (1) inoperable safety device due to incorrect mounted position, and (2) human performance error where crane operators failed to function test the high-angle kick-out despite checking it off on pre-use inspection forms on multiple occasions. The crane was de-rated and placed out of service.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, South Marsh Island Block 27
incident__event__summary: On December 3, 2014, a sheen was reported in the vicinity of South Marsh Island Area Block 27. Pipeline Segment Number 3334 operated by Sea Robin Pipeline Company was identified as the source. A hole measuring 1/2" by 3/4" was located at the 6 o'clock position at the bottom of the line at 93 feet water depth. The spill volume was initially reported as 15 barrels but later revised to 0.7 barrels of condensate. The leak was secured on December 5, 2014, and repairs were completed by December 12, 2014. Investigation findings indicated no corrosion, no third-party damage, and normal cathodic protection readings, suggesting the probable cause was a manufacturing defect or isolated pit in the pipe.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - South Marsh Island Block 130
incident__event__summary: On 25-FEB-2024 at 0930 hours, a contract construction crew attempted to raise the elevation of an I-beam support by approximately 1 inch on the Talos OCS-G02280 SM 130 B production facility. After unsuccessful attempts with come-along winches, the crew attached the crane's main line to the fixed I-beam and applied tension. The crane operator exceeded the maximum load capacity of 21,500 lbs at the 55-degree boom angle by applying approximately 30,000 lbs of tension, resulting in damage to the crane boom heel section and lattice. Estimated repair cost: $400,000. No injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure crane operators monitor weight indicators while having knowledge of maximum capacity at boom angle; Do not connect or use cranes to lift fixed objects per API RP 2D standards; Conduct comprehensive Job Safety & Environmental Analysis (JSEA) discussing all hazard mitigation methods; Implement stop work procedures when initial lifting attempts fail; Adhere to API RP 2D appendix B C.3.2.3 requirements before starting lifts

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - South Marsh Island Block 130
incident__event__summary: On May 12, 2023, at approximately 1605 hours, a crane boom collapse occurred on the Talos ERT LLC OCS-G02280 South Marsh Island 130 B Facility. While attempting to lower an 11,000 lb grating rack to the top deck, the boom cable broke causing the boom and load to collapse onto the deck. The boom cable failed due to fatigue from numerous cycles through the bridle sheaves, exacerbated by frequent operation at 60-to-75-degree boom angles and inadequate lubrication penetration. Total damage was $730,000 ($330,000 to deck and production equipment, $370,000 to crane). No injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Replace wire rope when nominal size is reached, not reduction size, per API RP 2D; Include additional wire rope micrometer evaluations during quarterly and annual inspections, focusing on high rotation and wear areas; Conduct boom cable replacement at maximum 100 hours or within one year on SMI-130B crane; Identify alternative lubrication that provides adequate penetration to cable core

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - South Marsh Island Block 128
incident__event__summary: On January 16, 2024, at 0252 hours, the C1 compressor at Arena Offshore's SM-128B facility experienced a piston failure. The piston rod nut loosened due to component fault or wear, causing the two bottom connecting rod bolts to break. This allowed the piston rod nut to contact the head end of the cylinder and separate from the frame. As the housing broke, gas ignited. The vibration switch detected the abnormal vibration and tripped, but failed to initiate shutdown at the panel due to moisture in the output line that froze in the 17°F ambient temperature. The night operator discovered the fire, activated the fire alarm and ESD, alerted personnel, and the fire was extinguished without injuries.
incident__event__recommendations: Install in-line desiccant dryer to pneumatic supply inlets of compressor control panel to prevent condensation formation; Improve compressor maintenance procedures to prevent piston scarring and component wear; Ensure vibration switch signal integrity in cold weather conditions

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, South Marsh Island Block 130
incident__event__summary: On December 15, 2023, at 0520 hours, an 8-inch bi-directional gas pipeline (Segment 5114) containing 1220 psi ruptured on the Talos ERT LLC SM 130 B facility in the Gulf of Mexico. The rupture resulted in a fire that traveled approximately 80 feet from below the +10 level to the production deck. A 3-foot section of pipeline from the +4 to +7 elevation was missing. The fire lasted 5-7 minutes before gas pressure bled down. Life saving equipment, wiring, and minor structural damage occurred near the +10 level. No injuries were reported. Favorable wind conditions (28 mph blowing away from platform) prevented more severe damage. Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.
incident__event__recommendations: Complete mechanical integrity inspection of Segment 5114 with rope access near waterline; Implement preventative maintenance program for pipeline coatings; Ensure mechanical integrity tests are performed on required 5-year schedule

incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled descent of topdrive and traveling block
incident__event__summary: On 11 September 2019 at approximately 13:00 hours, while making up for a casing run on the Enterprise 263 jack-up rig, the driller's-side drawworks brake became non-responsive. The topdrive and traveling block began to descend uncontrollably. The driller used the off-side drawworks brake to slow the descent. The topdrive struck the top of the 7.625-inch casing in the hole and the traveling block fell against the casing stabbing board. Investigation revealed a non-OEM stainless steel retention roll pin had broken, causing brake band separation. Post-incident investigation found incorrect retaining roll pins installed in both drawworks brake band assemblies instead of required OEM cotter pins. No injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Safety Alert No. 369 issued on 25 October 2019 informing operators of hazards of using non-OEM parts

incident__event__top_event: Personnel basket struck vessel railing and platform structure during transfer
incident__event__summary: During personnel transfer from M/V Patience Lab to SP 93 B Platform using a 4-Man Billy Pugh basket, a sudden wind gust caused the crane boom to swing. The crane operator attempted to lock the swing brake but it failed to hold. The hydraulic swing gear motor (slew drive system) had experienced structural failure due to mounting bolts backing out, resulting in complete loss of boom swing control. incident__event__summary: On 09-OCT-2015 at 0015 hours, a fire ignited inside the galley/diner on Platform SP 93 B in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire burned for over 8 hours, with at least 4 hours of contained burning with both doors closed. Two operators on the platform detected the fire via alarm and attempted initial containment using the platform's Fire Water Cooling System. The Person In Charge shut in gas and oil pipelines and made emergency distress calls. A nearby vessel (M/V Canyon Runner) responded and assisted in extinguishing the fire using its water cannon. The fire caused extensive structural damage to the galley/diner. Root cause investigation identified improper installation of an electric stove/oven with insufficient clearance from combustible materials in the walls, allowing heat transfer to ignite wood paneling, insulation, and wiring. No personnel injuries occurred.
incident__event__recommendations: Provide root cause and analysis for fire; Request a Safety Alert concerning proper stove installation for offshore living quarters; USCG request Gulf Land provide letters to their customer base concerning similar installation setups; USCG request that other similar galley buildings be tested for heat transference with a heat gun

incident__event__summary: On October 20, 2014 at 1000 hours, a fire occurred on the Energy XXI Facility, Lease OCS-G 1619, South Pass 93-A platform. The Lead Operator was loading the #4 Arial Compressors after a shutdown. After restarting the compressor and switching the governor from manual to automatic mode, a loud popping sound was heard from the turbine area followed by a small flash fire approximately 6 inches long. The fire was extinguished using a handheld fire extinguisher with no injuries reported. Investigation revealed that minor metal fragments from previous repairs of the right bank #6 cylinder went unnoticed during installation, causing coolant to enter the cylinder and leading to a cascade failure involving lube oil sealing system damage and combustion.
