incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled release of mechanical energy resulting in hand entrapment and amputation
incident__event__summary: On 30 July 2025 at 13:10 CDT, a Supreme Services crew contracted by W&T Offshore was laying down a 12-inch mechanical casing cutter from Well C005 into a shipping basket on South Pass 49 C platform. incident__event__top_event: Laceration injury from falling PIG launcher cap
incident__event__summary: On 21 September 2023 at 1505 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered a laceration to the right hand while assisting two other operators in reinstalling a PIG launcher cap onto gas pipeline KZZ-602 on the cellar deck of unmanned Platform A, SP Block 83. The three-person crew removed the 12-inch cap for pipeline flushing operations. During removal, the lever arm assembly became damaged, forcing manual reinstallation. The crew mistakenly believed the cap was securely threaded after rotating it, and when they lessened their hold, the cap fell. The sharp edge of the lever arm shaft penetrated the IP's cut-resistant gloves, causing a laceration. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated at an onshore medical facility with eleven sutures.

incident__event__summary: On August 2, 2017, a fire occurred on Ship Shoal Block 177 Platform A during decommissioning operations of well A4. A cutting crew was removing a casing valve using a cutting torch when a fire ignited at a weep hole on well A5's Surface Safety Valve (SSV). The fire was extinguished in approximately 3 seconds by the Fire Watch using a portable fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed that well A5 had a leaking diaphragm in its SSV which allowed gas to escape and be ignited by slag from the cutting torch. The primary cause was the failure to shut-in well A5 prior to commencing hot work, compounded by lack of communication between the cutting crew and the Production Operator.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure clear communication protocols between all personnel involved in hot work operations, particularly between cutting crews and production operators responsible for well shut-in; Reconcile conflicting procedures between Job Safety Environmental Analysis (JSEA) and Hot Work Permits to ensure consistency in hazard identification and control requirements; Implement verification procedures to confirm well shut-in status prior to hot work commencement, with documented sign-off from the responsible Production Operator

incident__event__summary: On July 16, 2017, at approximately 4:30 pm, a Waukesha Reciprocating Compressor on the Ship Shoal 198-J platform experienced an internal explosion during start-up operations. The compressor had undergone maintenance on July 12-15, 2017, during which internal components were exposed to atmosphere. During restart, the compressor was not purged or loaded before running idle for approximately ten minutes with the blowdown valve open. An LEL mixture of air and natural gas ignited within the process piping, causing the explosion and damaging the 3rd stage cooler and associated piping. The compressor mechanic experienced migraines and dizziness five days later. Probable causes were identified as failure to purge and inadequate job safety analysis.
incident__event__recommendations: Retrain all personnel on recognition of hazard analysis and proper system purging requirements following any repairs that expose combustible equipment to atmospheric conditions; Develop and implement a general purging procedure; Improve employee competency testing on purging techniques; Enhance hazard analysis processes to identify purging as a key step in job safety analysis

incident__event__top_event: Crane boom structural failure during load offloading
incident__event__summary: On June 18, 2015 at approximately 1300 hours, the platform crane boom on Ship Shoal 229 A suffered major structural failure while offloading equipment onto an offshore vessel. The crane operator was lowering a load approximately 10-20 feet above the vessel deck when boom swing control became inactive. The operator heard what sounded like failure of boom hydraulic pistons and the boom and load began lowering uncontrollably. When the operator attempted to raise the boom, it collapsed in the middle, dropping the load onto the starboard side and deck of the vessel. The root cause was that the crane operator used an assumed load weight of 12,000-14,000 pounds rather than verifying the actual weight of 20,300 pounds, which exceeded the maximum rated capacity of 15,000 pounds at a 70-degree boom angle.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure cargo manifests show actual weights per individual lift, not total weights divided by number of lifts; Verify actual load weights prior to lifting using available weight indicators or load charts; Follow company policy requiring confirmation of actual load weight before crane operation; Consider installation of weight indicators in crane cabs to aid prevention of overload incidents; Communicate actual cargo weights from shore base to offshore facility and crane operator prior to lift preparation

incident__event__summary: On January 5, 2017, at approximately 2:00 a.m., a fire occurred on Ship Shoal 266-A platform when oil discharged from a failed interface sight glass on the Heater Treater (NBK-1700) saturated protective insulation and contacted the hot exhaust manifold of Pipeline Pump #1 (PAX-4100), located 14 feet away. The sight glass failure was exacerbated by missing ball checks in the gauge cock valves, which allowed continuous oil discharge. All four crew members were evacuated and rescued. The fire was extinguished at approximately 5:50 a.m. Total property damage was estimated at $2.7 million.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE should issue a Safety Alert to industry identifying possible issues pertaining to Sight Glass Assemblies, Gauge Cock Valves, and Ball Check Assemblies; Operators should refer to API RP 14J for Design and Hazards Analysis for Offshore Production Facilities

incident__event__top_event: Gas migration during cementing with well control incident
incident__event__summary: On October 23, 2014, while performing drilling operations on the C-22 well, the Ensco 87 drilling rig experienced a well control incident during cementing operations. A gas bubble from the formation entered the well during the cement job and migrated up the well before the cement had time to cure. At approximately 10:15am, fluid began flowing from the casing valve at 15 barrels per hour. The crew cleared the well and closed the Diverter Annular at 11:00am, after which fluid discharged to a height of approximately 20 feet above the rig floor. For approximately 30 minutes, the well intermittently discharged a mixture of gas, mud, and cement from the Diverter line. The Emergency Shut Down was initiated and 62 personnel were evacuated. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution were sustained.

incident__event__top_event: Fire in Heater Treater Fire Tube
incident__event__summary: On December 23, 2016, at approximately 1550 hours, personnel on the E-Auxiliary platform at Ship Shoal Block 154 noticed smoke coming from the exhaust stack of the Heater Treater (NBK-1800). After shutting off fuel gas, a small fire was observed inside the fire tube. The fire was extinguished with a handheld fire extinguisher. incident__event__top_event: Uncontrolled release of energy and tool ejection from wellbore
incident__event__summary: On January 31, 2023, during temporary abandonment of the E-4 well at Ship Shoal 224 E platform, a wireline casing cutter operation resulted in uncontrolled release of trapped pressure in the annulus below a subsea wellhead tieback system. The tool string and fluids were ejected from the wellbore, damaging grating on the cellar and wellbay decks. No injuries were reported. The root cause was that personnel were unaware of trapped pressure in the 10-3/8" and 7-5/8" annulus due to lack of communication above and below the subsea wellhead system and absence of pressure control equipment.
incident__event__recommendations: Install slip-on surface wellhead on all wells with subsea wellhead systems at the facility; Update standard operating procedures to include utilizing pressure control equipment before perforating untested annuli

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Load - Crane Wire Rope Failure
incident__event__summary: On December 13, 2021 at 11:15 hours, the auxiliary wire rope of the SS 72 OF crane failed during offloading operations at Sanare Energy Partners' Ship Shoal Block 72 Platform. A 4,000 lbs rental air compressor being offloaded from motor vessel Leader fell approximately 20-30 feet into offshore waters when the aux cable parted above the hook. The compressor was recovered five days later. Root cause was determined to be failure to lubricate the wire rope, allowing corrosion and disintegration. Contributing factors included inadequate maintenance, failure to follow manufacturer recommendations, and failure to take the crane out of service despite identified deficiencies.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure cranes with identified deficiencies are taken out of service or operation is restricted in accordance with API RP 2D paragraph 3.1.5c; Implement proper lubrication program for wire rope to prevent corrosion; Maintain mechanical integrity program in accordance with manufacturer recommendations; Ensure crane service providers follow established procedures and API standards; Post proper cautionary notices and communicate limits of continued service when deficiencies are identified

incident__event__top_event: Flash fire during pipeline pump startup
incident__event__summary: On March 7, 2025, at 08:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at W&T Offshore's production facility at Ship Shoal Block 80, Platform A while attempting to bring Pipeline Pump #2 (PAX-360) online. A W&T Mechanic and Contractor Electrician sustained burn injuries to head and hands. The fire resulted from a loose fitting on the fuel gas starter exhaust piping that allowed gas to accumulate near the pump. When the engine backfired during startup, the accumulated gas ignited. Both injured personnel were evacuated and treated; the Electrician was released to return to work on April 9, 2025, while the Mechanic continued treatment. The incident was exacerbated by personnel not wearing required PPE (gloves) and wind direction that pushed flames toward one worker.
incident__event__recommendations: When using fuel gas to start prime movers, always ensure the starter exhaust gas is vented to a safe location; Review gas engine startup procedures to include equipment checks verifying proper installation of components; Utilize gas detectors when performing startup procedures on engines with gas as a fuel source and/or as a pneumatic supply for starters; Conduct mandatory checks on engines with gas as a fuel source and/or as a pneumatic supply for starters to ensure proper installation of starter exhaust piping; Replace all flexible hoses with hard piping for fuel gas systems; Conduct routine checks on all fittings associated with starter gas; Stress the importance of wearing all required PPE during equipment startup operations

incident__event__summary: On August 25, 2018 at 0345 hours, a Floorhand employed by Helmerich & Payne was severely injured while being hoisted in the derrick of rig H&P 107 to inspect the top drive assembly following a jarring operation. The Floorhand's secondary safety static line became snagged on a monkey board finger while he was being hoisted upward. The opposing forces created by the moving hoist line and the snagged safety line caused the Floorhand to be pulled in opposite directions, resulting in severe injury. The Toolpusher operating the man rider winch had obstructed line of sight due to racked drill pipe and was not using a flagger as required by the JSA and Pre-Job Checklist. The Floorhand was evacuated via helicopter and transported to hospital for emergency medical treatment.
incident__event__recommendations: When JSA meetings take place, all personnel should speak up to address any issues with lines of sight; If lines of sight are an issue due to an obstacle, a second person should be given the responsibility of constantly monitoring the individual who is out of sight; Any personnel lift using air hoists must have at least one spotter depending on the need of the operation, and spotter(s) must be in direct communication with the hoist operator at all times while personnel are elevated

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, South Timbalier Block 26
incident__event__summary: On May 2, 2021, at approximately 10:11 hours, a fire occurred on the unmanned, out-of-service ST-26 A/A-QTR fixed platform in South Timbalier Block 26, Lease OCS-G 01361, operated by Cox Operating, L.L.C. The fire was caused by lightning striking the Communications Tower and Communications Building during severe thunderstorms. The fire was intensified by hydrocarbons released from a nearby Dry Oil Tank that was damaged by intense heat, causing process piping to separate at welds. incident__event__top_event: Flash fire from ignited gas during welding operations
incident__event__summary: On May 17, 2014, at 0830 hours, while performing abandonment operations on Walter Oil & Gas's #001 well on the Rowan Gorilla IV, three employees were burned by a flash fire. The crew was welding pad eyes to the inside of a 36-inch casing to enable removal. Burning slag fell inside the wellbore, igniting gas that had migrated from the reservoir after the casing was cut. Investigation revealed gas was present at approximately 40 feet depth inside the casing but not at the surface where employees were working.

incident__event__summary: At approximately 1200 hours on January 23, 2020, at South Timbalier Block 311 Platform A, an operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of an in-service LP Gas Compressor 3rd stage scrubber LSH with his foot/shin while exiting the area. This released approximately 1100 psi through a 3/8" stainless steel tubing drain line approximately 7 feet in length. The pressure release caused a whipping action in the drain tubing striking the operator on the side of the face/hardhat. Hydrocarbons from the 3rd stage suction scrubber vented toward the in-service LP gas compressor engine and ignited. Personnel activated the manual ESD station, signaled the fire alarm, and all personnel mustered. An attempt to use a nearby 125 lb. wheeled fire extinguisher unit failed to discharge. Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, South Timbalier Area
incident__event__top_event: Load Block Failure and Dropped Object
incident__event__summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. While lowering a Job Safety Analysis document in a trash bag attached to the load block hook, the crane cable lost tension and fell approximately 65 feet onto the aft deck of a motor vessel. The load block sheave was ejected upward and fell onto the vessel deck, striking a grocery box. A deckhand sustained a lumbar muscle strain while evading the falling components. Root cause analysis revealed inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings due to internal debris obstruction in the lubrication passage, leading to bearing failure, sheared dowel pin, and sheave pin dislodgement. Contributing factors included operator inattention to distress indicators, inadequate training on early mechanical failure signs, and unsafe work practices involving maintaining the suspended load at excessive height above the vessel.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Houma District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert; Implement revised Block and Hook Inspection Forms requiring explicit verification of lubrication points serviced; Enhance Crane Pre-Use Inspections to include visual checks of all load block pins, retainers, and hardware; Require flushing and verification of all lubrication pathways during annual, quarterly, and monthly servicing; Implement photographic documentation during annual inspections; Strengthen operator training on early mechanical distress indicators including block rocking, grinding/popping, and reduced wire-rope tension; Establish protocol to remove crane from service immediately upon detection of abnormalities; Apply safe work practice of lowering loads to lowest and safest height above water before swinging over vessel; Conduct hazard analysis to identify and mitigate consequences of suspended loads at height

incident__event__top_event: Fire on Glycol Reboiler vent line
incident__event__summary: On January 29, 2024 at approximately 1745 hours, a fire occurred on the Glycol Reboiler vent line at Cantium LLC facility in South Timbalier Block 23. The facility had shut in due to a Level Safety Low alarm on the Glycol Surge Tank. While operators were adding glycol to the system, a flame was observed coming from the Pressure Safety Valve vent line when the valve relieved pressure. The fire was extinguished using potable water. The probable cause was determined to be human error during reassembly of the still column following October 2023 maintenance, where the packing section was not reinstalled properly, removing the ability to preheat glycol and allow entrained gases to be released.
incident__event__recommendations: Send still column back for thorough cleaning; Replace perforated metal plate with expanded metal in packing section to improve flow

incident__event__top_event: Crane boom structural failure and collapse
incident__event__summary: On September 15, 2017, during a boom rest operation on the South Timbalier 34B Platform, the crane boom experienced structural failure. The two bottom bolts connecting the midsection and heel section failed, causing excessive stress on the top bolts. The top section of the boom collapsed (jackknifed) to the right and dropped, eventually hanging by the main and auxiliary cables on the west side of the platform. The probable cause was a recent boom/boom stop contact that damaged the lower bolts. Heavy corrosion may have contributed to weakening the bolts. No reportable injuries occurred.

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Viosca Knoll Block 900
incident__event__summary: On 22 July 2023 at approximately 1240 hours, a fire occurred on VK 900 A Platform when diesel from a tote tank spilled onto the intake housing of the #1 turbine compressor. The fire was initiated by diesel contacting the hot turbine exhaust and/or an exposed extension cord. Personnel attempted firefighting but the fire spread to the 8-man sleeper and became uncontrollable. All 6 personnel were safely evacuated. The fire was extinguished by responding vessels using water cannons. Property damage was estimated at $1,900,000.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement comprehensive hazard analysis and written job procedures for diesel transfer operations; Establish protocols for securing hoses and extension cords during work breaks; Improve inspection and maintenance of electrical equipment to prevent exposure and degradation; Ensure Job Safety Analysis (JSA) documents are retained and accessible for all maintenance and transfer operations

incident__context__operator: Occidental Petroleum (owned by Anadarko Petroleum Corporation)
incident__event__summary: On 24 November 2024 at 1710 hours, a 3/4 inch pipe nipple on the sight glass level bridle of Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber (MBF P630A-1) cracked and broke, releasing gas from the platform. The gas alarm activated, alerting the Control Room Operator who witnessed the release and initiated platform shutdown via the Emergency Shutdown System. A platform muster was conducted with no injuries reported. The broken nipple was replaced and the facility returned to production within 30 minutes. The incident was caused by fatigue cracking of the nipple due to extended compressor vibrations, over-tightening during original installation, possible loosening causing excessive vibration, and excessive weight from the sight glass level bridle support.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE recommends issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident; Anadarko to submit Management of Change (MOC) to install additional support to Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber sight glass level bridle to reduce weight and vibration on the nipple

incident__event__incident_type: Casing Failure with Oil and Gas Release
incident__event__summary: On 19-June-2015 at 0605 hours, a production operator on platform VR-279 discovered gas and oil leaking from well A3/A3D through a hole in the 20 inch drive pipe at the +10 bell guide and from the 20 inch x 10 3/4 inch annulus below the wellhead base plate. Approximately 0.67 gallons of oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico. Root cause analysis determined that a gas compressor failure on 16-June-2015 caused production shutdown, allowing reservoir shut-in pressure to build. This pressure caused pump through plugs to fail, allowing gas and oil to enter deteriorated short string tubing, then the 7 5/8 inch production casing, and finally the 10 3/4 inch surface casing, which had a leak approximately 10 feet below the wellhead. The surface casing leak allowed pressurized fluids to enter the non-pressure-containing 20 inch drive pipe, creating open leak paths to the atmosphere.

incident__event__summary: On 13-Nov-2014, the Hercules 300 was completing well A5 when approximately 66 barrels of 16.6 ppg Zinc Bromide completion fluid was discharged overboard. The Derrickman failed to confirm that valves isolating the cement unit were closed prior to beginning the fluid transfer to the filtration unit.

incident__event__top_event: Crane wire rope guard assembly fell from height striking deck
incident__event__summary: On June 24, 2022, at approximately 12:30 pm during completion operations at VR-369 platform A, the wire rope guard assembly for the main load line idler sheave carrier fell 103 feet to the platform deck. The incident occurred when the auxiliary load line jumped over the main load line idler sheave carrier plate and came to rest on the wire rope guard, causing abrasion damage. The root cause was identified as flawed equipment design of the American Aero OM 450 platform crane, where the auxiliary load line and boom line run in close proximity to the main load line idler sheave carrier, allowing contact during high-speed operation.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lake Charles District recommends issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident; Replace main load line idler sheave carrier with a double-sheave idler to allow independent operation of main and auxiliary load lines

incident__context__operator: Black Elk Energy Offshore Operating LLC
incident__event__summary: On April 18, 2014 at approximately 1900 hours, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater (NBK-2100) on the VR369A platform operated by Black Elk Energy Offshore Operating LLC in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire was caused by a pin hole that developed in the fire tube due to pit corrosion from frequent high-dosage batch treatments with acid-based de-emulsifier (Gyptron TA-21). Produced oil leaked through the pin hole into the fire tube and sprayed near the main burner, providing a steady fuel source. The operator responded to a Burner Safety Low alarm, isolated gas supply manually, activated the ESD station, and alerted personnel. The fire was successfully extinguished using handheld fire extinguishers. The incident resulted in $15,000 in property damage to the fire tube.
incident__event__recommendations: Inspect the fire tube when possible hazards are first identified; Implement formal documentation of daily vessel monitoring (pressures, levels, temperatures); Establish preventive maintenance and inspection schedule for fire tube given acid exposure; Review chemical program and heat management to prevent emulsion pad formation; Ensure adequate records of all maintenance and inspection activities

incident__event__top_event: Crane shock loading due to packer shear ring failure
incident__event__summary: On February 10, 2020, at 5:45 am, a shock loading incident occurred with the platform crane at Vermilion Block 371 while setting a tension packer during plug and abandonment operations. The packer's shear ring failed at 26,000 pounds of pull (below its 40,000 pound rating) due to the ring not being changed after previous settings per manufacturer instructions. The sudden load reduction from 26,000 to 9,000 pounds shock loaded the crane, damaging the 9/16 inch diameter Dyform 6 boom cable. Root causes included failure to follow API RP 2D guidelines, use of incorrect Job Safety Analysis, crew misunderstanding of shear ring rating (believed 50,000 pounds instead of 40,000 pounds), and non-compliance with manufacturer instructions to change shear ring after each setting.
incident__event__recommendations: Draft safety alert to not use platform crane to set tension packers; Draft safety alert to follow manufacturer instruction to change shear ring on MAD-1 tension packer after each setting; Install load cell data recording device for lifts utilizing platform cranes for well work to reduce events beyond SOP guidelines and provide critical data records

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Vermilion Block 170 A
incident__event__summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lake Charles District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert/Bulletin regarding this incident

incident__event__summary: On 16-DEC-2015 at approximately 1845 hours, a crane incident occurred at Fieldwood Energy's Vermillion 261-A-Aux facility during boom positioning activities. The boom hoist limit device failed to function, allowing the boom to exceed its maximum angle height and contact the boom stops, resulting in bent boom chords and bent boom stops. Contributing factors included failure of pre-use inspection to identify a loose and bent extension shaft on the boom hoist limit device, a boom angle indicator obscured by paint overspray and grime, and failure of the job safety analysis to identify hazards associated with close proximity of load to crane's minimum boom radius and low lighting conditions.

incident__event__top_event: Hydraulic boom cylinder rod separation from cylinder rod eye
incident__event__summary: A hydraulic box boom crane at Castex Offshore's Vermilion 252-A facility experienced two consecutive hydraulic boom cylinder (HBC) failures where the cylinder rod separated from the cylinder rod eye. The first failure occurred two days after installation of a refurbished unit, and the second failure occurred twenty-four days after replacement. Investigation revealed that both failed cylinders had inferior quality welds with voids and lack of penetration, and more critically, the refurbished cylinders had a working stroke roughly one inch different from the originals. This mismatch created applied tension instead of compression, causing rod eye failure. Both failures occurred with little or no hook load and resulted in no personnel injury or additional crane damage.
incident__event__recommendations: Issue a Safety Alert to oil and gas operators and crane maintenance and repair companies to heighten awareness and prevent reoccurrence; Operators should consider changing both hydraulic boom cylinders when repairs to one are necessary to ensure a matched pair; Alternatively, verify a matched set by confirming the same pin-to-pin centerline (both while collapsed and while extended) and equal working stroke measurements of each HBC before installation

incident__event__top_event: Contact Dermatitis from SOBM splash exposure
incident__event__summary: On July 1, 2019, at 2:00 pm, during pulling of workstring (POOH) operations on the Ensco 102 rig at Vermilion Block 369, a floor hand was splashed with Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) on his legs. The injured person was not wearing required personal protective equipment (barrier cream, tyvek suit, nitrile gloves, face shield, and goggles). The SOBM soaked through his clothing, causing contact dermatitis with lesions, scabbed blisters, and signs of infection. The injured person continued working and did not report the injury until July 7, when he sought medical attention. He was evacuated by helicopter for onshore medical treatment. Investigation revealed inconsistent completion of Job Safety Analysis (JSA) forms, inadequate hazard identification in pre-tour safety meetings, and failure of supervision to enforce PPE requirements.

incident__event__summary: On February 21, 2024, at approximately 1349 hours, a fire occurred at the HI A 379 B facility during construction operations. A construction fitter was using an electric grinder to smooth welds on deck plating when sparks migrated through two layers of protective fire blanket barriers into a chemical/methanol storage area located approximately six feet from the grinding area, igniting flammable materials. The fire was extinguished and the facility was shut down. Investigation revealed that written procedures required removal of flammable chemicals from the work area, but the construction crew elected to use fire blanket barriers as an alternative control measure, which proved inadequate.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure all approved welding and burning plans state that proper Code of Federal Regulations must be followed; Implement consistent requirements across all hot work safety documentation; Strengthen pre-job safety meetings to ensure discussion of all written control requirements; Ensure site leadership enforces compliance with Job Safety Analysis requirements

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Cameron Block 544
incident__event__top_event: Projectile Impact Injury from Sand Hopper Lid Failure
incident__event__summary: On 24-OCT-2014 at approximately 1455 hours during routine sandblast and paint operations on Arena Offshore's West Cameron 544-A facility, the top hatch lid of a sand hopper failed catastrophically due to poor condition of fastening components. The lid dislodged, was deflected by the upper rack assembly, and struck a blast and paint crew member along a nearby walkway, causing significant injuries including head lacerations, finger damage, mandible fracture, broken teeth, and pelvic fracture. Investigation revealed worn bolt threads with inconsistent washers and mismatched nuts, two of which were completely missing. Pre-job inspection procedures and daily maintenance checklists were not properly followed.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE Lake Charles District recommends the Office of Safety Management issue a safety alert identifying the hazards associated with sandblasting equipment when not being properly maintained

incident__event__summary: On November 27, 2021, at approximately 09:30 am, a flash fire occurred while removing skid pads with a torch on the top deck at West Cameron Block 96 platform B. incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Cameron Block 33
incident__event__top_event: Wire rope sling failure and struck-by injury
incident__event__summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during crane operations to remove a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson at WC-33 #1 platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling in the lifting arrangement parted and struck an Offshore Technical Services (OTS) employee on the head and neck. The incident occurred when the abrasive cutter hung up in the caisson simultaneously with a wave surge, introducing dynamic loading that exceeded the safe working limit of the sling. The injured person sustained a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA) that did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions (5-7 foot sea state), failure to call an all-stop after the initial hang-up, and personnel positioned near the suspended load contrary to JSA requirements.
incident__event__recommendations: Address possible hazards with adverse sea conditions and dynamic loading in JSAs for crane operations; Include maximum lift weight and sling limitations in JSAs; Call all-stop to address new threats when suspended loads hang up; Enforce requirement for personnel to stay well clear of suspended loads; Install handrails around open caissons during operations; Ensure personnel wear required safety harnesses during crane operations

incident__event__top_event: Object strike injury from falling pump connection
incident__event__summary: On 13-APR-2019 at 0615 hours, a hammer union pump connection (Type 1502, 40.6 lbs) fell 36.8 feet from a tubing joint being lifted by liftboat crane during workover operations on the A1 well at WC-61A platform. The pump connection struck a DSS worker on the hard hat, upper right arm, right thigh, and right foot, fracturing the humerus and lacerating the big toe. The root cause was improper securing of the pump connection to the tubing joint; during the second attempt to make the connection with the work string, the crane sling unscrewed the pump connection as workers turned the tubing joint clockwise. Contributing factors included poor communication regarding shift progress, inadequate supervision, missing work permits, complacency, inattention to task detail, and failure to follow JSA procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Issue safety alert on findings of how the pump connection fell 36.8 feet striking a worker

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - West Delta Block 105
incident__event__summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.
incident__event__recommendations: Utilize gas detector to confirm area is gas-free prior to troubleshooting suspected ignition-related issues where equipment is still running or online; Utilize hot work permit program when troubleshooting suspected ignition issues with fire watch present; Improve pre-job planning and discussion between mechanic and Person In Charge (PIC) signing the JSA; Educate maintenance personnel on risks of disconnecting ignition wires while equipment is running; Ensure JSA is task-specific and identifies all potential hazards including gas leaks and ignition sources

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, West Delta Block 152
incident__event__summary: On January 17, 2019, at 0930 hours, a fire occurred in the glycol dehydration unit on Renaissance Offshore's WD 152-A platform. During platform startup following an extended shutin, the Field Foreman started the glycol reboiler and increased temperature from 200°F to 250°F without proper circulation time or adherence to industry guidelines. The #1 compressor shut down due to high pressure, and the Field Foreman left the glycol unit unattended. Rapid temperature rise caused overpressure in the reboiler due to high water content (21% vs. typical 5-10%), forcing Triethylene Glycol (TEG) and liquid contaminates out of the still column onto the hot stack (operating temperature >660°F). The TEG ignited upon contact with the stack, creating a large flame. Personnel responded by activating ESD, fire alarms, and manually fighting the fire with fire reels. No injuries or pollution were reported. Damage included fire-damaged aluminum shell, broken sight glass, melted pressure gauges, burned insulation, heat-damaged motor/belt, damaged electrical wiring, and damaged asbestos panels. Root causes were inadequate startup procedures and lack of operator training on glycol phase vaporization characteristics.
incident__event__recommendations: Insulate the glycol stack to eliminate hot surfaces as ignition sources; Enhance training for personnel on chemical and effluent characteristics during liquid-to-gas phase vaporization and handling of contaminated or saturated glycol; Improve glycol startup and shutdown procedures with specific operational definitions (temperature set points vs. vague instructions like 'bringing up slowly'); Ensure operators are trained and familiar with specific glycol reboiler startup and shutdown procedures; Utilize third-party expertise to conduct onsite operator competency assessments; Conduct Hazard Analysis on all glycol reboilers at Renaissance facilities to ensure safe working condition; Create Safety Alert or Safety Bulletin addressing advanced training on glycol phase vaporization, requirement for manned glycol startup operations, specific startup procedures with circulation time requirements, and consideration of reboiler stack insulation

incident__event__summary: On 24 November 2019 at 0700 hours, a fire occurred on West Delta 27 A Platform involving the cooler fan tower package (HAL 1161/62/63) for gas compressor unit CBA-1101. The fire was caused by ignition of escaping gas from a ruptured cooling tube in the third stage cooler (HAL-1163) when it contacted the compressor's hot exhaust system operating above 900°F. Personnel extinguished the fire within six minutes with no injuries. Equipment damage was limited to $2,040 in charred metal on the cooling tower. The facility's TSE and ESD systems functioned as designed, safely shutting down production and mitigating consequences.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Gulf Waters
incident__event__summary: On 21 September 2016 at 0828 hours, BSEE Inspectors discovered an oil sheen 7 miles long and 1 mile wide originating from WD 32 E Platform. Investigation revealed that the Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) failed due to a stuck internal pump shifting/slider valve, preventing oil transfer from the Floatation Cell (ABM-410). Oil level in the oil chamber rose to the level of the produced water side and flowed back into the overboard discharge chamber. The Fisher 2500 Level Controller maintained preset water discharge level, causing oil to discharge through the overboard water outlet. The Level Safety High (LSH 1) shut down device was not installed in the oil chamber as required, preventing automatic platform shut-in. Approximately 8.05 barrels of crude oil were discharged into Gulf waters. The unmanned facility was in-service and producing at the time of discovery.
incident__event__recommendations: LSH 1 (Fisher 2100) shut down device has been installed and function tested in the oil chamber of the floatation cell to prevent carryover of produced oil back into the water chamber and activate system shut-in; BSEE rescinded the 3-times-per-week pollution inspection frequency and now requires daily pollution inspection for WD 32 DAE facility

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 152
incident__event__summary: On 27 August 2025 at 1045 hours, the WD-152 A platform crane failed during a dynamic lift of an 8,000-pound Power Pack from a Motor Vessel. The crane's boom cable separated, causing the crane boom to fall uncontrollably and strike platform handrails and the vessel below. The root cause was a failed bearing in the boom cable bridle sheave assembly that caused excessive wear to the sheave pin, allowing the cable to cut through the sheave and ultimately fail under load. No injuries occurred, but damage was sustained to platform handrails, the crane assembly, and the Motor Vessel. A small amount of hydraulic fluid was released into the Gulf of America.
incident__event__recommendations: BSEE New Orleans District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations consider issuing a Safety Alert regarding the incident; Renaissance to update crane safe work practices to require annual removal of sheaves and sheave pins for inspection; Third-party crane service to re-write procedures to remove pins for inspections per customer requests

incident__event__summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759), approximately 5-7 feet above the water line on the WD 73AT platform. The platform's ESD was activated, shutting down the facility and sounding the alarm. All 26 personnel on board were accounted for and sheltered in place in the quarters building upwind from the leak. The pipeline was bled down over several hours. A light sheen was observed during overflight, later determined to originate from the slop oil tank rather than the pipeline. Investigation revealed inadequate preventative maintenance, failed repairs documented in a 2015 survey, and insufficient pipeline inspection methods.

incident__context__region: Gulf of America, West Delta Block 90
incident__event__summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.
incident__event__recommendations: More rigorous pre-job planning; Inclusion of complete and accurate wellbore history in procedures; Implementation of structured hazard assessment process; Earlier implementation of engineering controls such as rubberized sealant at spooling head; Systematic contingency planning and information sharing

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Discharge of Synthetic Based Mud Overboard
incident__event__summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, a saltwater kick occurred during drilling of the 12-1/4 inch hole section at approximately 25,000 feet on the Diamond Offshore West Vela drillship. The kick caused Surface Back Pressure in the MPD system to exceed the 1,300 psi PRV setting. Approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud were discharged overboard via the MPD system Pressure Relief Valves. The well was successfully shut in and the kick was circulated out. The incident was attributed to human performance error in the decision to divert drilling fluid overboard rather than to the flowline as specified in the New Technology Application.
incident__event__recommendations: MPD procedures and hole section influx management envelope matrix should include a differential flow threshold to inform the Driller how to respond when using MPD; When using PCVs for MPD, ensure modeling is performed to determine optimal PCV setpoints with down hole pumps on based on maximum kick intensity and maximum PCV setpoint based on required SBP for connections with down hole pumps off; Set maximum PCV pressure to protect against PRVs from opening based on a maximum flow rate for the selected setpoint; Review FMCA for plugging downstream of the MPD choke to confirm the correct PRV set point should limit system to pressure rating of equipment upstream of the choke; Review the latest version of the flow control software that responds to influxes in step changes and switches the MPD from Flow Control to SBP mode when the high-pressure limit is reached

incident__event__top_event: Employee struck by falling coring barrel
incident__event__summary: On October 22, 2015, while performing drilling operations on Statoil's #002 well, an employee working on the drill floor of the Discoverer Americas was struck on the foot by a coring barrel while retrieving a sample from the wellbore. The coring barrel became hung up on a hang off pin on the transport rack, forming slack in the suspension line. incident__event__top_event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic oil based mud
incident__event__summary: On December 11, 2014, the Noble Jim Day unintentionally discharged 142 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico during mud pit cleaning operations. The discharge resulted from valve misalignment during the transition between crew changes. Keys to the dump valves were passed between personnel without proper Lock-out/Tag-out documentation. The Derrickman, lacking knowledge of proper valve alignment and receiving poor communication and labeling, misaligned the valves and created a pathway for hydrocarbons to discharge overboard. The Pit Volume Totalizer alarm detected the loss after approximately 5 minutes, at which point the crew secured the pits and corrected the valve alignment.
incident__event__recommendations: Improve valve labeling and identification on piping systems; Enhance Lock-out/Tag-out procedures and documentation requirements; Update system drawings to accurately reflect valve installation; Conduct pre-tour safety meetings for all crew transitions; Improve training on proper valve alignment procedures; Enhance communication protocols during critical operations; Ensure Job Safety Analysis (JSA) identifies environmental hazards

incident__event__top_event: Employee struck by chain hoist handle during riser skate repositioning
incident__event__incident_type: Struck-by injury from defective lifting equipment
incident__event__summary: On July 27, 2016, during riser running operations on the Maersk Viking, a riser skate trolley derailed from its track. While attempting to reposition the skate using a 3.2 ton Vital Lever Hoist, the chain hoist malfunctioned when tension was applied. The hoist handle spun and struck an employee on the wrist, causing a bone fracture. The root cause was a defective chain hoist missing a vital retaining pawl latch. incident__event__top_event: Unintentional discharge of Synthetic Oil Based Mud into Gulf of Mexico
incident__event__summary: On June 27, 2017 at 2120 hours, the Pacific Sharav discharged 29 barrels of 14.0 PPG Synthetic Oil Based Mud into the Gulf of Mexico. The discharge occurred due to misalignment of two valves on the low pressure header while the Mud System Operator was pumping deck drain water from the muddy water tank overboard. The valves were incorrectly opened, allowing mud from the active mud system to enter the overboard line. The incident was caused by human error and failure to follow approved procedures, specifically the absence of the Mud Engineer who is responsible for verifying valve alignment during the semi-weekly discharge procedure.
incident__event__recommendations: Modify semi-weekly discharge procedure to explicitly show proper valve alignment; Require verification of valve alignment by a competent individual prior to opening overboard valves; Require competent individual to verify proper valve alignment when more than one type of fluid is in the active mud system; Conduct crew training on incident findings

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico, Walker Ridge Block 29
incident__event__summary: At approximately 0130 hours on July 26, 2018, a fire ignited in the skid of a rental oil filtration unit on the North side of the production deck at the Big Foot facility (OCS-G 16942), located 120 miles from shore in 5185 feet of water. The fire was approximately 10 feet high by 10 feet wide with heavy black smoke. The root cause was that an operator restarted the filtration unit but failed to reopen the inlet and outlet valves after a supper break. The operator then left the unit unattended for approximately 14 minutes. With the valves closed and safety devices disabled, the temperature in the unit rose high enough to degrade the integrity of the hose, causing it to burst and release hot lube oil. The lube oil ignited, causing the fire. The fire was extinguished within a few minutes using AFFF and firewater. No injuries were reported. Property damage totaled $3,275,000.

incident__event__summary: On March 26, 2015, while performing drilling operations on the Noble Jim Day in Walker Ridge Block 508, approximately 3,107 feet of 22" casing was dropped to the seafloor. The rig was pre-running the 22" casing in an attempt to minimize downtime. Unforeseen loop currents increased from the forecasted 2-3 knots to 4.9 knots, causing the rig to lose dynamic positioning capability. A controlled drift was initiated to reduce loads on engines, thrusters, and the suspended casing string. At 09:00 on March 26th, uncommon rig movement indicated mechanical failure. Investigation revealed the Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Mandrel had snapped in two within an O-Ring groove due to excessive force from the casing weight combined with high loop currents. No subsea infrastructure or rig equipment was damaged. Metallurgical testing showed no equipment anomalies.

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Based Mud Discharge
incident__event__summary: While performing drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk for Anadarko Petroleum Corporation, two incidents occurred in which Synthetic Based Mud was inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico. The first incident on July 4, 2015 released 2.5 barrels of SBM, and the second on August 8, 2015 released 3.3 barrels of SBM. Both incidents were caused by a malfunction in the rig's software for the Slip Joint Packer management system, combined with inadequate crew monitoring of packer pressures. The software logic automatically adjusted low alarm set points based on instrument air pressure fluctuations without crew knowledge, causing the packer system to vent mud when pressure dropped below the adjusted set point.
incident__event__recommendations: Manufacturer to develop and install upgrade to packer management system logic to prevent automatic adjustment of low alarm set points without crew knowledge

incident__context__region: Gulf of Mexico - Walker Ridge Block 551
incident__event__summary: On 26-NOV-2016 at approximately 1406 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred around the gas export metering skid on the FPSO Turritella at Walker Ridge Block 551-A. During an attempt to collect gas samples, an engineer mistakenly pulled a half-inch plug from a double block and bleed valve without first bleeding down the line, despite system pressure of up to 2500 psi. The escaping gas was detected by the gas detection system, triggering an automatic facility shutdown. The engineer sustained a minor injury and received onboard medical treatment. No evacuation was necessary.

incident__context__materials: ['XL 10 (methylhydrogensiloxane dimethylsiloxane copolymer)', 'TD 6/12 Blend (ethoxylated tridecyl alcohol surfactant)', '10% KOH (potassium hydroxide solution)', 'EM 652 (emulsion product)']
incident__event__incident_type: Chemical reaction, hydrogen release, explosion, and fire
incident__event__summary: On May 3, 2019, at AB Specialty Silicones in Waukegan, Illinois, an operator pumped an incorrect chemical (10% KOH) into a batch tank containing XL 10 and TD 6/12 Blend. The incompatible chemicals were stored in nearly identical 55-gallon blue drums, differing only in small labels. The reaction between XL 10 (containing silicon hydride bonds) and aqueous KOH produced hydrogen gas, which released into the production building. The hydrogen gas ignited, causing a massive explosion and fire that fatally injured four employees (Byron Biehn, Jeffrey Cummings, Daniel Nicklas, Allen Stevens), destroyed the production building, and forced operational disruptions.
incident__event__recommendations: Amend OSHA PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, including broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals; Revise EPA Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals; Ensure hydrogen gas detection and alarm systems are properly installed, maintained, and configured based on facility application, manufacturer specifications, codes, standards, and industry guidance; Establish a safety management system addressing process safety at AB Specialty Waukegan facility, including elements from CCPS publications; Incorporate CCPS Essential Practices for Managing Chemical Reactivity Hazards into operations

incident__event__summary: On October 26, 2019, Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at Aghorn's Foster D waterflood station. While attempting to isolate Pump #1 without performing Lockout/Tagout, the pump automatically energized, causing a broken plunger to release water containing hydrogen sulfide gas. Pumper A was fatally injured from H2S exposure. Subsequently, Pumper A's spouse gained access to the unlocked facility, was also exposed to H2S, and was fatally injured. The incident was attributed to multiple safety failures including nonuse of personal H2S detectors, lack of Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate ventilation, nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, and deficient site security.
incident__event__recommendations: Mandate use of personal H2S detection devices as integral part of PPE kit for all employees and visitors prior to entering facilities with H2S potential; Develop site-specific, formalized, comprehensive Lockout/Tagout program meeting 29 CFR 1910.147 requirements; Commission independent analysis of facility design regarding ventilation and mitigation systems for H2S release scenarios; Develop and demonstrate comprehensive safety management program addressing H2S hazards including risk identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring; Ensure H2S detection and alarm systems are properly maintained, configured, and include site-specific programs based on manufacturer specifications; Employ multiple layers of alerts (audible and visual) in H2S detection and alarm system design; Develop and implement formal, written, site-specific security program requiring employees to lock access gates upon entering and departing facility

incident__event__summary: On August 28, 2016, a nitrous oxide trailer truck exploded at the Airgas manufacturing facility in Cantonment, Florida, fatally injuring one Airgas employee and heavily damaging the facility. The most probable cause was that during initial loading of a trailer truck, a pump heated nitrous oxide above safe operating limits, likely starting a nitrous oxide decomposition reaction that propagated from the pump into the trailer truck, causing the explosion. The CSB determined that Airgas lacked an effective safety management system to identify, evaluate, and control process safety-related hazards. Following the incident, Airgas rapidly implemented comprehensive process safety management components for its nitrous oxide operations.
incident__event__recommendations: Complete in-progress post-incident actions; Continue safety initiatives for nitrous oxide operations

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Organic Peroxide Decomposition and Fire
incident__event__summary: On August 31, 2017, organic peroxides stored in refrigerated trailers at the Arkema Crosby facility decomposed and ignited following catastrophic flooding from Hurricane Harvey. The facility lost electrical power, backup generators, liquid nitrogen cooling systems, and refrigerated trailer cooling due to a common-mode failure caused by unprecedented flooding. Over 350,000 pounds of organic peroxide decomposed across three separate fires over 12 days. More than 200 residents evacuated and 21 emergency responders sought medical attention due to exposure to decomposition vapors.
incident__event__recommendations: Facilities should perform analysis to determine susceptibility to extreme weather events; Evaluate flood maps to determine if facility is within 100 or 500-year floodplain; Evaluate seismic risk maps for potential earthquake risk; Consider risk of other extreme weather phenomena; Ensure critical protections and equipment are not susceptible to common-mode failures during high flood levels; Implement independent layers of protection available in case of high inundation levels; Develop systems to retain key summarized information from incidents documenting historical safety risks; Apply conservative risk management approach when evaluating impacts of extreme climate scenarios; Do not reopen evacuation zone roads until combustion is confirmed absent despite telemetry data

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Toxic Chlorine Gas Release
incident__event__incident_type: Chemical decomposition and fire resulting in toxic gas release
incident__event__summary: On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to Bio-Lab's Lake Charles facility in Westlake, Louisiana. Extreme winds tore off portions of the roof of Plant 4, which stored approximately 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation. Rainwater from the hurricane contacted the stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The decomposition and fire destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. A massive plume of hazardous gases crossed the facility boundary and was visible over a large portion of the nearby community. Interstate 10 was closed for over 28 hours, and a shelter-in-place order was issued. No injuries were reported. Approximately $250 million was invested in redesigning and reconstructing the facility, which resumed operations around March 2023.
incident__event__recommendations: Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement processes and safeguards for protection, including constructing buildings to withstand hurricane winds and flooding, implementing safeguards to ensure hazardous chemicals are not compromised during extreme weather, and following CCPS guidance on natural hazards assessment and planning; Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Develop and implement improved PHA action item management system with assignment, documentation, and tracking of all action items until resolution, and conduct periodic audits; Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Perform PHAs on all buildings and units processing or storing TCCA, revalidate at least every five years, and include building design basis as process safety information; Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Revise emergency response plan to require proper maintenance and function-testing of fire protection system, checking emergency equipment one month before hurricane season, and training personnel on emergency generators and equipment; Louisiana Governor and State Legislature: Require existing chemical manufacturing and storage facilities in hurricane-prone regions to evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement protective processes and safeguards; OSHA: Amend PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, broaden application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, and augment PHA element to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards; EPA: Revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals and process-specific conditions; EPA: Implement five open recommendations from 2022 GAO Report on chemical accident prevention related to climate change and natural hazards risks

incident__context__operator: BP Products North America Inc. (now Ohio Refining Company LLC)
incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Naphtha Release and Fire
incident__event__incident_type: Flash Fire from Vapor Cloud Ignition
incident__event__summary: On September 20, 2022, at approximately 6:46 p.m., a vapor cloud ignited causing a flash fire at the BP-Husky Toledo Refinery in Oregon, Ohio. Two BP employees (brothers Ben and Max Morrissey) were fatally injured when they released approximately 23,000 pounds of flammable liquid naphtha from a pressurized Fuel Gas Mix Drum to the ground. The drum, which normally contained only vapor (fuel gas), had filled with liquid naphtha when an upstream Crude 1 Tower overflowed naphtha into a vapor bypass line. The incident resulted in approximately $597 million in property damage and is the largest fatal incident at a BP-operated petroleum refinery since the 2005 Texas City incident.
incident__event__recommendations: Revise safeguards in process hazard analyses to establish effective preventive safeguards using engineered controls that do not rely solely on human intervention; Revise Abnormal Situation Management policy to incorporate CCPS guidance and include procedures for unplanned crude slate changes, continued operation with units shut down, and emergency pressure-relief valve opening; Develop and implement Stop Work Authority policy with clear procedures and regular training for employees to stop unsafe work during abnormal situations; Revise Toledo Alarm Philosophy to incorporate EEMUA guidance for alarm rate following upset and improve alarm performance to reduce alarm flood duration and peak rate; API should develop guidance on Fuel Gas Mix Drum design, sizing, instrumentation, and preventive safeguards; API should develop publication addressing prevention of pressure vessel overflow with guidance on instrumentation, process hazard analysis, safeguards, and training; ISA should revise ANSI/ISA 18.2-2016 to include performance targets for short-term alarm flood analysis

incident__event__top_event: Tank overfill and vapor cloud explosion
incident__event__summary: On October 23, 2009, at approximately 12:23 a.m., a 5-million gallon aboveground storage tank (Tank 409) at the CAPECO facility in Bayamón, Puerto Rico overflowed during offloading of gasoline from the tanker ship Cape Bruny. Approximately 194,000 gallons of gasoline escaped, forming a large vapor cloud that encompassed 107 acres. The vapor cloud migrated through open dike drain valves to low-lying areas and the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited. The resulting explosion registered 2.9 on the Richter scale, damaged approximately 300 homes and businesses up to 1.25 miles away, caused over $5 million in damages to Fort Buchanan military facility, and resulted in fires that burned for nearly 60 hours, destroying or damaging 17 of 48 petroleum storage tanks. Environmental contamination included petroleum products leaking into soil, nearby wetlands, and navigable waterways.
incident__event__recommendations: EPA should require risk assessments for bulk petroleum storage tank terminals considering proximity to communities and potential for multiple tank releases; OSHA should extend Process Safety Management (PSM) standard to tank terminal facilities; EPA should require high-level alarm systems and automatic overfill prevention systems under SPCC requirements; API should revise ANSI/API 2350 to require automatic overfill prevention systems for all facilities, not just remotely operated ones; NFPA should revise NFPA 30 to require independent or redundant level alarms and automatic overfill prevention systems; ICC should require independent audible or visual alarms to indicate rising liquid levels; Facilities should conduct comprehensive risk assessments considering site complexity, flammable liquid types, and community proximity; Emergency responders should conduct pre-incident planning with facilities for vapor cloud explosions and multiple tank fires; National incident database for tank terminal incidents should be established to assess frequency and develop tailored requirements

incident__event__summary: On August 6, 2012, a catastrophic rupture occurred in the 4-sidecut piping of the #4 Crude Unit at Chevron Richmond Refinery. The 52-inch component of the 8-inch line ruptured due to sulfidation corrosion, releasing approximately 10,800 barrels per day of light gas oil. The release vaporized into a large vapor cloud that engulfed 19 employees. Approximately two minutes after release, the vapor ignited, resulting in a large fire. One firefighter was caught in the fireball but escaped due to protective equipment. Six employees suffered minor injuries. Approximately 15,000 people from surrounding communities sought medical treatment. The incident resulted in a Community Warning System Level 3 alert and shelter-in-place advisory for Richmond, San Pablo, and North Richmond.
incident__event__recommendations: Chevron to engage diverse team for documented damage mechanism hazard review as part of Process Hazard Analysis cycle; Include applicable industry best practices and inherently safer systems in hazard reviews; Report leading and lagging process safety indicators at all California Chevron refineries; Require documented use of inherently safer systems analysis and hierarchy of controls in establishing safeguards; California refineries to perform documented damage mechanism hazard reviews; Identify and require reporting of leading and lagging process safety indicators; Establish multi-agency process safety regulatory program for California oil refineries; EPA to jointly plan and conduct inspections with Cal/OSHA and state agencies; Enhance and restructure California's process safety management regulations for petroleum refineries; Implement compensation system to ensure regulator capability in process safety oversight

incident__event__summary: On July 31, 2024, at approximately 8:20 p.m., an emergency pressure relief valve on Heat Exchanger 5 Surge Drum in Tank Farm 5 opened due to overpressure, discharging approximately 275 pounds of anhydrous ammonia to the atmosphere. The discharged ammonia contained a high level of liquid, causing it to rapidly drop to ground level near the employee parking lot and building. During evacuation, 33 workers were injured by ammonia inhalation, with 4 admitted to hospitals. The incident was caused by overpressure in the vessel, failure to discharge to a safe location, and insufficient emergency preparedness including lack of effective alarms and emergency shutdown procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: IIAR to update ANSI/IIAR 2 to include guidance for preventing or mitigating liquid or two-phase atmospheric discharges from emergency pressure relief systems; IIAR to update ANSI/IIAR 2 to require assessment of whether emergency pressure relief devices discharge to a safe location; Cuisine Solutions to reduce likelihood or mitigate consequences of liquid or two-phase atmospheric discharges through engineering controls and emergency pressure control systems; Cuisine Solutions to implement electronic process data historian and management system; Cuisine Solutions to update Emergency Action Plan with separate indoor/outdoor release procedures, evacuation routes, windsock guidance, and annual drills; Cuisine Solutions to add ammonia-specific alarm system with documented response procedures

incident__event__top_event: Combustible dust explosions and building collapse
incident__event__summary: On May 31, 2017, at approximately 11:00 p.m., multiple combustible dust explosions occurred at the Didion Milling dry corn milling facility in Cambria, Wisconsin. The incident fatally injured five employees and injured 14 others. During the day of the incident, Didion connected the discharge of two rotary gap mills to increase production. On the night of the incident, employees smelled smoke and investigated the source. A fire spread through process equipment via interconnected dust collection systems, resulting in explosions in dust collection equipment. Secondary explosions occurred throughout the facility, causing the collapse of multiple buildings including the Multipurpose Building, F Mill, and the Boiler House. The estimated property damage was $15,375,959 with no off-site consequences.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop comprehensive combustible dust process safety management system including management of change, process safety information, audits and inspections, fugitive dust management, incident investigation, dust hazard analyses, engineering controls, PPE, and emergency preparedness; Modify pneumatic conveying and dust collector ductwork systems to ensure minimum transport velocity and implement periodic inspection and testing programs; Perform dust hazard analyses on all buildings and units processing combustible dust with revalidation every five years; Assess and implement engineering controls for structural design and venting of reconstructed facility; Incorporate paper-based process safety information into electronic records management system; Perform personal protective equipment hazard analyses and require flame-resistant garments for combustible dust operations; Update facility emergency response plan with signal/alarm system, emergency shutdown procedures, evacuation instructions, and response to fire scenarios; Assess and update pre-deflagration detection and suppression engineering controls; Develop and implement process safety leadership and culture program; Issue comprehensive OSHA combustible dust standard based on NFPA standards

incident__event__incident_type: Equipment failure leading to fires and explosions
incident__event__summary: On June 27, 2016, at approximately 11:22 p.m., a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) on the A-Train process line failed due to thermal fatigue, resulting in a major loss of containment of flammable hydrocarbons. The released hydrocarbons ignited, initiating a series of fires and explosions that caused 13 different ruptures of piping and equipment over approximately 35 minutes. Two workers on the night shift were uninjured. The facility experienced extensive damage and was shut down for almost six months. Enterprise incurred $10.4 million in fire response expenses and a non-cash loss of $7.1 million in damaged assets. A total of 104,000 pounds of emissions were released, with most burned off by fires and flares.
incident__event__recommendations: 2016-02-I-MS-R1: American Petroleum Institute recommendation on BAHX thermal fatigue management; 2016-02-I-MS-R2: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX integrity management; 2016-02-I-MS-R3: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on industry data sharing; 2016-02-I-MS-R4: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX operating guidance; 2016-02-I-MS-R5: Jackson County Local Emergency Planning Committee recommendation on community notification and social media use

incident__event__summary: On November 22, 2016, during removal of an inoperable gearbox on a plug valve in the sulfuric acid alkylation unit, an operator removed four vertical bolts that secured both the gearbox support bracket and the pressure-retaining top-cap. When the operator attempted to open the valve with a pipe wrench, the valve came apart and released approximately 2,000 pounds of pressurized isobutane, forming a flammable vapor cloud. The vapor reached an ignition source (an energized welding machine approximately 70 feet away) within 30 seconds, causing a fire that severely burned four workers (one ExxonMobil employee and three contractors) with third-degree burns.
incident__event__recommendations: Evaluate human factors associated with equipment design and apply the hierarchy of controls to mitigate identified hazards; Establish detailed and accurate written procedures and provide training to ensure workers can perform all anticipated job tasks safely; Upgrade older model plug valves (pre-1984 design) to improved design that attaches gearbox support bracket to valve body flanges rather than pressure-retaining components; Develop written procedures detailing steps for safely removing various types of plug valve gearboxes; Provide formal training to operators on how to remove gearboxes from plug valves and associated hazards; Conduct human factors evaluation during Process Hazard Analysis to identify equipment design and reliability issues; Review existing process safety management practices and equipment design for potential improvements

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Liquid Nitrogen Release
incident__event__incident_type: Cryogenic liquid release resulting in asphyxiation fatalities
incident__event__summary: On January 28, 2021, liquid nitrogen overflowed from an immersion freezer at Foundation Food Group's Plant 4 in Gainesville, Georgia. The overflow occurred while maintenance workers were troubleshooting operational issues with the freezer, which was designed and owned by Messer LLC and rented to FFG. Once released, the liquid nitrogen vaporized, expanded, and rapidly accumulated within a partially enclosed cold room lacking mechanical ventilation. Two maintenance workers died from asphyxiation due to vaporized nitrogen. The uncontrolled nitrogen leak continued undetected for 30-60 minutes. When discovered, at least 14 FFG employees responded by entering the cold room or attempting rescue, resulting in four additional fatalities and three employees plus one firefighter suffering severe asphyxiation symptoms. The incident was caused by failure of the liquid level control system due to deformation of the bubble-type level gauge component, combined with inadequate process safety management, emergency preparedness, atmospheric monitoring, and product stewardship practices.
incident__event__recommendations: Gold Creek Foods (current facility owner) should proactively engage local emergency responders with information about hazardous substances, critical equipment locations, and emergency systems; Messer LLC should update product stewardship policy to include participation in customer PHAs, verify proper signage for oxygen-deficient atmospheres, and review emergency shutdown switch locations; Messer should create informational materials for customers on safety issues and effective safety management systems for inert gas asphyxiation hazards; OSHA should update Regional Emphasis Programs for poultry processing facilities (Regions 4 and 6) and food manufacturing (Region 5) to explicitly cover liquid nitrogen freezing processes; OSHA should promulgate a specific standard for cryogenic asphyxiant agents addressing equipment design, atmospheric monitoring, emergency shutdown systems, employee training, emergency action plans, and process safety management elements; OSHA should develop guidance document for process safety management practices applicable to compressed gases and cryogenic asphyxiant agents; Compressed Gas Association (CGA) should develop comprehensive standard for safe storage, handling, and use of liquid nitrogen in fixed facilities; CGA should update P-76 standard on oxygen-deficient atmospheres to require atmospheric monitoring systems, distinct alarm indications, remote-operated emergency isolation valves (ROEIV), and guidance on emergency stop device locations; National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) should update NFPA 55 to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of manual isolation valves and emergency activation devices; International Code Council (ICC) should update International Fire Code to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of isolation valves and emergency devices

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment from Aboveground Storage Tank
incident__event__incident_type: Chemical spill to environment and drinking water contamination
incident__event__summary: On January 9, 2014, approximately 11,000 gallons of a mixture containing Crude MCHM and PPH, stripped leaked from tank 396 at Freedom Industries facility in Charleston, West Virginia. The leak resulted from pitting corrosion that created two holes (0.75 and 0.4 inches diameter) in the tank floor. The chemical mixture escaped through failed secondary containment and an underground culvert, flowing into the Elk River approximately 1.5 miles upstream of the West Virginia American Water treatment facility intake. The contamination resulted in a Do Not Use order affecting approximately 300,000 residents across nine counties. Over 300 people sought medical treatment for symptoms including nausea, rashes, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. The incident exposed significant gaps in tank inspection, secondary containment maintenance, leak detection systems, source water protection, and public health communication.
incident__event__recommendations: AST owners and operators should establish regular inspection programs and routinely monitor tanks and secondary containment to verify tank integrity; AST owners and operators should coordinate with nearby water utilities and emergency response organizations to ensure chemical information is immediately available; AST owners and operators should ensure spill prevention and protection plans are updated and implemented; States should take immediate action to protect source waters through increased inspections and enforcement at chemical storage facilities near water sources; States should establish Source Water Assessment Programs that mandate source water protection planning by water utilities; Water utilities should engage with Local Emergency Planning Committees to obtain Tier II information about stored chemicals; Water utilities should assess treatment system capabilities to remove potential hazardous chemicals; Public health agencies should establish communication frameworks to ensure information is coordinated through one entity; American Water Works Association should disseminate lessons learned from chemical contamination incidents; American Water should establish requirements for subsidiary surface water treatment plants to develop source water protection plans
