incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Hydrogen Fluoride Release
incident__event__incident_type: Multiple incidents: gasket failure with fatality (Oct 2021), reboiler explosion (Jan 2023), HF release with serious injury (Jun 2024)
incident__event__summary: Three separate hydrogen fluoride incidents occurred at Honeywell Geismar's HFC-245fa unit between October 2021 and June 2024. The first incident (October 21, 2021) involved catastrophic failure of a spiral wound gasket in a flanged piping connection, releasing 39 pounds of anhydrous HF and fatally injuring Field Operator 2 who was exposed to the toxic vapor. The second incident (January 23, 2023) involved catastrophic failure of a reboiler that had thinned due to HF corrosion, releasing over 800 pounds of anhydrous HF and over 1,600 pounds of chlorine gas; no personnel were injured but a complex-wide shelter-in-place was initiated. The third incident (June 7, 2024) involved partial removal of a blind flange from a valve containing pressurized HF during maintenance, releasing less than one pound of HF and seriously injuring a contract worker. All three incidents were preventable through effective implementation of existing safety management systems.
incident__event__recommendations: Perform comprehensive third-party audit of Geismar facility's process safety and allied management systems evaluating compliance with federal standards and internal management systems (2023-02-I-LA-R1); Require periodic reporting updates from Geismar site regarding closure of audit findings (2023-02-I-LA-R2); Perform Safer Technologies and Alternatives Analysis (STAA) for HFC-245fa unit (2023-02-I-LA-R3); EPA to initiate prioritization of hydrogen fluoride as High-Priority Substance under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation (2023-02-I-LA-R4); OSHA to amend PSM standard to require MOC review for organizational changes impacting process safety (reiterated 2005-04-I-TX-R9)

incident__event__summary: On 22 July 2010 at 14:20-16:19, a fatal explosion and fire occurred on Column B, a cadmium distillation column at the Horsehead zinc smelter in Monaca, Pennsylvania. The incident resulted from a partial blockage of the column sump that allowed liquid zinc to back-fill up the column, creating a pressure cooker effect. As the column filled with liquid zinc, internal pressure increased, raising the boiling point of zinc from 907°C to 980°C and creating superheated conditions. The column operated outside design parameters with pressure surges as vapour attempted to escape. Eventually, tray walls failed under the increased internal pressure, releasing a surge of superheated zinc vapour and liquid that ignited explosively, destroying the combustion chamber wall and creating a major conflagration in the workplace. The root causes included sump design with restricted clearance (25.625 inches or 65mm), poorly executed column commissioning that allowed formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion, high and increasing zinc throughput, inadequate sump temperatures during heat-up, and human factors including normalisation of hazardous conditions due to history of column blockages.
incident__event__recommendations: Increase sump underflow clearance from 1 brick (65mm) to 2 bricks (130-180mm) minimum to allow passage of zinc, dross and debris; Implement best practice standards of management and training; Improve operator awareness and plant information systems; Take all possible steps to prevent air ingress to the column at feed point, condenser and sump; Improve dross separation at feedbox; Implement constant checking that column sump is running normally to prevent accumulation of liquid zinc; Avoid too low a run-off operating regime to prevent formation and accumulation of hard zinc compounds; Ensure adequate sump temperatures during column heat-up (target 650°C minimum); Implement procedures to detect sump blockages early through nostril box inspections for dark trays; Change feed arrangement from top-down to bottom-up needle valve configuration to reduce dross and air entrainment; Maintain steady feed and firing conditions to minimize internal pressure disturbances; Implement monitoring of liquation pot temperatures to prevent excessive iron recycling (target maximum 450°C); Improve commissioning procedures to prevent formation of sticky zinc oxide dross/liquid zinc emulsion; Establish clear emergency procedures that address sump blockage risks on all column types, not just zinc oxide columns

incident__event__summary: On March 17, 2019, a mechanical seal failure on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump resulted in release of approximately 177+ barrels of butane-enriched naphtha product over approximately 30 minutes. The released flammable vapors accumulated undetected in the absence of gas detection systems and ignited at 10:00:46 a.m. The resulting fire spread to 14 adjacent tanks in the First & Second 80's tank farm due to tank spacing, design, and lack of remotely operated isolation valves. The fire burned for three days, destroying 15 tanks and their contents. A containment wall breach released an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon products, foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou, Buffalo Bayou, and the Houston Ship Channel. The incident caused no fatalities or injuries but resulted in significant environmental contamination, community disruption, and over $150 million in property damage.
incident__event__recommendations: Develop and implement a process safety management system for all atmospheric storage tanks and associated equipment in highly hazardous chemical service at ITC Deer Park terminal; Develop and implement a condition monitoring program for all pumps in highly hazardous chemical service with vibration alarms per ANSI/HI 9.6.9-2018; Install flammable gas detection systems with alarm functions in product storage and transfer areas where flammable substance releases could occur; Install remotely operated emergency isolation valves configured to Fail-Closed for all atmospheric storage tanks containing highly hazardous chemicals or liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating; Conduct evaluation of tank farm design against API STD 2610 and NFPA 30 standards, addressing tank spacing, subdivisions, process equipment placement, and drainage systems; Update API STD 2610 to include flammable gas detection systems in leak detection section; Eliminate the atmospheric storage tank exemption from OSHA PSM standard; Modify EPA RMP rule to expand coverage to include all flammable liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating

incident__event__top_event: Equipment rupture and flammable vapor cloud explosion
incident__event__summary: On April 2, 2019, at 10:51 a.m., a 3-inch cast iron y-strainer in the isobutylene supply piping ruptured due to brittle overload fracture driven by internal pressure from liquid thermal expansion. The rupture released more than 10,000 pounds of flammable isobutylene, creating a vapor cloud. KMCO operators manually closed valves and activated firewater systems to mitigate the release. However, the isobutylene vapor cloud ignited while two operators were still in or near the vapor cloud, fatally injuring one operator (James Mangum) and seriously injuring two others. At least 28 workers total were injured. The explosion and subsequent fires caused substantial facility damage. KMCO filed for bankruptcy in May 2020.
incident__event__recommendations: Establish clear emergency response policies distinguishing which incidents operators should handle versus those requiring emergency response team response; Provide remote isolation equipment (remotely operated valves) to allow workers to stop chemical releases from safe locations such as blast-resistant control rooms; Implement comprehensive hazard evaluation programs that identify brittle materials in hazardous applications and potential high-pressure conditions; Install pressure-relief devices on piping sections subject to potential high-pressure conditions from thermal expansion; Ensure emergency response procedures and training prioritize life safety and do not rely on operators putting themselves in danger to stop releases; Activate facility alarm systems during chemical emergencies to ensure all workers receive notification

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Acetic Acid and Methyl Iodide Release
incident__event__summary: On July 27, 2021, three contract workers employed by Turn2 Specialty Companies were removing an actuator from a pneumatically actuated plug valve in the acetic acid unit at LyondellBasell La Porte Complex. The workers inadvertently removed pressure-retaining nuts while attempting to remove the actuator. When they attempted to remove the coupler using a pry bar, the combination of forces and internal pressure caused the valve cover and plug to eject. Approximately 164,000 pounds of acetic acid mixture at 238°F and 130 psi was released. Two workers were fatally injured from chemical burns and toxic inhalation of acetic acid and methyl iodide vapors. A third Turn2 worker and a LyondellBasell responder were seriously injured. Twenty-nine additional personnel were transported for medical evaluation. Property damage was estimated at $40 million.
incident__event__recommendations: LyondellBasell: Develop procedures for removing actuating equipment from plug valves, clearly identifying pressure-retaining vs. non-pressure-retaining components; ensure hazardous energy control per 29 CFR 1910.147; conduct risk assessments before authorization; ensure sufficient procedures and safeguards to prevent worker exposure; LyondellBasell: Require competent employees to verify contractor competence, training, and qualifications; ensure contractors are informed of process hazards and provided equipment-specific procedures; Turn2: Develop written, detailed procedures for safely conducting work on process equipment and train employees before work authorization; ASME: Revise Standard B16.34 to require clear marking of pressure-retaining components on existing plug valves and design new plug valves to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components when removing actuators or gearboxes; API: Revise Standard 599 to document multiple incidents, recommend written procedures for actuator/gearbox removal, require clear marking of pressure-retaining components on existing valves, and require new plug valves be designed to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components; VMA Technical Committee: Develop white paper addressing plug valve design with recommendations for written procedures, marking of pressure-retaining components, and design of new plug valves to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components

incident__event__top_event: Fired heater tube rupture and fire
incident__event__summary: On November 19, 2023, at 12:21 a.m., a metal tube ruptured within a fired heater during initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit at Marathon Martinez Renewables facility in Martinez, California. The rupture released over 200,000 pounds of renewable diesel and approximately 2,200 pounds of hydrogen, resulting in a fire that seriously injured one field operator with third-degree burns over 80 percent of his body. The incident was caused by overheating of fired heater tubes due to: (1) a misaligned (open) valve that diverted significant process flow away from the heater, and (2) two of four burners operating with cover plates blocking air inlets, leading to afterburning in the convection section. The field operator was positioned next to the heater during troubleshooting because adequate safe operating limits and alarms were not in place to alert personnel of unsafe conditions, and personnel chose to troubleshoot rather than remotely shut down the heater.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement engineering safeguards to detect and prevent afterburning in the fired heater (combustibles measurements, flame detectors, thermocouples); Implement tube metal temperature alarming consistent with corporate guidance with predetermined response actions including remote shutdown and personnel removal; Improve Walk the Line performance through operator field walkdowns, shift communications, and training on valve alignment; Complete comprehensive gap assessment of Martinez facility against Marathon corporate policies; Update corporate Process Heater Not-to-Exceed Limits and Alarms standard with tube metal temperature alarming and predetermined response actions; Update corporate Heater Application Standard with requirements for protecting fired heaters from low process flow and afterburning detection; Conduct Operations Excellence full assessment on Martinez facility; Revise API RP 556 with requirements for high tube metal temperature response, low process flow protection, and afterburning detection

incident__event__top_event: Inadvertent mixing of sulfuric acid and sodium hypochlorite resulting in chlorine gas release
incident__event__incident_type: Chemical reaction and toxic gas release
incident__event__summary: On October 21, 2016, at approximately 7:35 AM, a Harcros Chemicals cargo tank motor vehicle arrived at MGPI Processing facility in Atchison, Kansas to deliver 30% sulfuric acid. During unloading operations, the driver inadvertently connected the sulfuric acid discharge hose to the unsecured sodium hypochlorite fill line instead of the sulfuric acid fill line. Approximately 4,000 gallons of sulfuric acid mixed with 5,850 gallons of sodium hypochlorite in the bulk tank, initiating a highly exothermic reaction that produced a greenish-yellow toxic gas cloud containing chlorine and other compounds. The cloud migrated northeast from the facility, impacting workers onsite and community members. Over 140 individuals sought medical attention; one MGPI employee and five community members were hospitalized. The incident was mitigated by emergency responders closing the cargo tank discharge valve and activating the facility's deluge system.
incident__event__recommendations: Design chemical transfer equipment with physical separation between incompatible fill lines; Implement uniquely shaped and color-coded hose couplings for each chemical or class of chemicals; Install automated or remote emergency shut-off systems for chemical unloading operations; Improve pipe markings and labeling to clearly identify chemical lines; Enhance chemical unloading procedures to include verification steps and secondary confirmation; Ensure emergency escape respirator face pieces are stored in readily accessible locations; Conduct comprehensive process hazard analysis (PHA) considering human factors; Implement management of change procedures for equipment modifications; Provide training to drivers and operators on chemical incompatibilities and safe unloading practices; Establish communication protocols between facility operators and delivery drivers

incident__event__top_event: Explosion of pressurized vessel during chemical treatment and draining operations
incident__event__summary: Two explosions occurred at Midland Resource Recovery facility in Philippi, West Virginia on May 24, 2017 and June 20, 2017. The May 24 incident killed two workers (including company founder/president) and severely injured another. The June 20 incident fatally injured a contractor from Specialized Professional Services, Inc. Both explosions occurred during draining of Peerless MP odorizers that had been chemically treated with sodium hypochlorite. The probable cause was reactive, unstable chemicals generated during treatment of uncharacterized liquid residues in the vessels. MRR lacked effective process safety management systems to identify and control hazards from reactive chemicals.
incident__event__recommendations: OSHA should establish or revise standards to require companies dealing with reactive chemistry to conduct formal hazard analyses and implement robust safety management systems; EPA should strengthen Risk Management Plan (RMP) regulations to address reactive chemical hazards; Companies should avoid treating uncharacterized waste materials with sodium hypochlorite due to explosive hazard potential; Companies should conduct thorough evaluation of reactive chemistry under design and abnormal conditions; Implement formal hazard identification processes before decommissioning equipment; Install pressure relief protection on vessels containing reactive chemicals; Establish complete chemical characterization protocols before treatment operations

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Anhydrous Ammonia Release
incident__event__incident_type: Catastrophic piping failure due to hydraulic shock
incident__event__summary: On August 23, 2010, a hydraulic shock event caused catastrophic failure of a 12-inch roof-mounted suction pipe at Millard Refrigerated Services, releasing 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia. The incident was triggered by a 7-hour power outage followed by manual interruption of a defrost cycle, which bypassed the programmed bleed sequence. When the system restarted, high-pressure hot gas mixed with low-temperature liquid in the suction line, causing condensation-induced shock and vapor-propelled liquid phenomena that ruptured both the roof piping and an evaporator coil. The ammonia cloud traveled 0.25 miles across Theodore Industrial Canal, exposing 153 people including 1 Millard employee, 9 ship crew members, and 143 offsite contractors. Of those exposed, 32 were hospitalized and 4 placed in intensive care.
incident__event__recommendations: Avoid grouping multiple large-capacity evaporators to a single set of control valves; use individual valve control groups for each evaporator; Program defrost control systems to automatically depressurize or bleed coils upon restart after power outage or interruption prior to opening suction stop valve; Avoid manual interruption of evaporators during defrost; equip control systems with password-protected controls to restrict access to trained and authorized personnel only; Ensure pump-out times are long enough to remove residual liquid refrigerant from evaporator coils prior to introducing hot gas, especially after low-load periods or power outages; In event of ammonia release that cannot be promptly isolated, activate emergency shut-down switch to de-energize pumps, compressors and valves instead of attempting to isolate leaking equipment while system is running

incident__context__materials: ['sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®)', 'sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC, CDB-63®)', 'chlorine gas', 'nitrogen trichloride', 'methanol']
incident__event__summary: On December 8, 2020, at approximately 10:00 p.m., a pressure-rated rotary double cone dryer containing sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (NaDCC dihydrate, CDB-56®) exploded at Optima Belle LLC in Belle, West Virginia. The explosion occurred during a toll manufacturing operation in which Optima Belle was dehydrating CDB-56® to produce anhydrous sodium dichloroisocyanurate (CDB-63®) on behalf of Clearon Corporation through a contractual agreement with tolling outsourcing partner Richman Chemical Inc. (RCI). The chlorinated isocyanurate compound underwent an unexpected self-accelerating decomposition reaction, releasing gases that increased the dryer internal pressure above its design pressure, causing the dryer to explode. Metal debris and dryer fragments propelled off-site and within the facility, striking a methanol pipe that subsequently caught fire. One Optima Belle employee (John Mark Gillenwater II) was fatally injured, two others were evaluated for respiratory irritation, and one Kanawha County resident reported a minor leg injury. Debris was found almost a half mile from the site. Estimated property damage was $33.1 million.
incident__event__recommendations: Optima Belle: Develop and implement a written thermal and reactive hazards evaluation and management program adhering to CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R1); Optima Belle: Develop and implement a written program for tolling process design and equipment selection using CCPS guidance to ensure equipment design basis is adequate, safeguards are properly designed, and new processes are evaluated at laboratory and pilot scales before production scale (2021-02-I-WV-R2); Optima Belle: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources to identify roles and responsibilities, evaluate equipment requirements, and ensure participation by all parties (2021-02-I-WV-R3); Optima Belle: Develop and implement a process safety management system consistent with CCPS guidance addressing hazard identification, risk analysis, and management of risk (2021-02-I-WV-R4); Clearon: Develop and implement a comprehensive process knowledge management program ensuring robust process technology and safety information packages, awareness of critical reactive chemistry information, and procedures for transmittal to toll manufacturers (2021-02-I-WV-R5); Clearon: Update the sodium dichloroisocyanurate dihydrate (CDB-56®) safety data sheet to provide reasoning for storage and decomposition temperatures, explain differences between decomposition temperature and lowest temperature for self-accelerating decomposition, and provide exothermic decomposition energy (2021-02-I-WV-R6); Clearon: Develop and implement a written program for tolling process design and equipment selection using CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R7); Clearon: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources (2021-02-I-WV-R8); Clearon: Develop and implement a process safety management system consistent with CCPS guidance (2021-02-I-WV-R9); RCI: Develop and implement a formalized program for toll manufacturing agreements using CCPS resources to identify roles and responsibilities and ensure participation by all parties (2021-02-I-WV-R10); OSHA: Update the Chemical Reactivity Hazards website to include various reactivity assessment tools developed since 2002 (2021-02-I-WV-R11); OSHA: Develop and implement a comprehensive outreach plan to ensure chemical industry awareness of the Chemical Reactivity Hazards website (2021-02-I-WV-R12); OSHA: Amend the Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard, 29 CFR 1910.119, to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards by broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, requiring consultation of multiple information sources, and augmenting process hazard analysis to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards (2021-02-I-WV-R13); NCBI: Update safety information in PubChem for sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC) dihydrate to include reactivity and decomposition information including SADT, explosion hazard, and DSC and ARC results (2021-02-I-WV-R14); CCPS: Update Guidelines for Process Safety in Outsourced Manufacturing Operations or develop new tolling guidance document to include current best practices, guidance specific to tolling brokers, and alignment with comprehensive management systems framework (2021-02-I-WV-R15)

incident__event__summary: On February 8, 2017, at approximately 11:05 am CDT, a foul condensate tank at the PCA DeRidder containerboard mill exploded during annual maintenance outage. The tank, part of a non-condensable gas system, contained water with a layer of flammable turpentine and an explosive vapor space. Air entered the tank through a pressure vacuum breaker during normal pressure swings and cooling, mixing with turpentine vapor to create an explosive atmosphere. Hot work (welding) on water piping above the tank likely ignited the explosive mixture. The explosion killed three contractors and injured seven others, launching the 100,000-gallon tank approximately 375 feet over a six-story building. The incident resulted from inadequate process hazard analysis, ineffective safeguards, and failure to apply process safety management systems to the non-condensable gas system.
incident__event__recommendations: Evaluate and replace foul condensate tank with vessel designed for full-vacuum conditions; Install explosion prevention systems on non-condensable gas equipment; Design foul condensate tank with weak roof-to-shell attachment (frangible roof); Implement robust administrative controls for non-condensable gas system operations; Conduct process hazard analysis for non-condensable gas system; Expand process safety management program boundaries beyond regulatory requirements; Apply hierarchy of controls to non-condensable gas system safeguards; Evaluate inherently safer design options to eliminate air ingress pathways; Establish clear ownership and responsibility for foul condensate tank operations; Apply TAPPI TIP 0416-09, NFPA 69, NFPA 68, and ISA 84 standards to non-condensable gas systems; Reiterate OSHA Process Safety Management standard coverage to include atmospheric storage tanks

incident__event__top_event: Rupture of steel pipe elbow with release of hydrofluoric acid and hydrocarbons
incident__event__incident_type: Loss of Containment with Fire and Explosions
incident__event__summary: On June 21, 2019 at 4:00:16 a.m., a steel pipe elbow in the HF alkylation unit ruptured after the control room operator increased feed to the T-7 propane stripper. The elbow, containing high concentrations of nickel and copper, had corroded from HF exposure and thinned to 0.011 inch (less than 7% of retirement thickness). The rupture released approximately 95% propane, 2.5% HF, and other hydrocarbons, forming a large vapor cloud. The cloud ignited two minutes later at 4:02:06 a.m. The RAD system was successfully activated, draining 339,000 pounds of HF. However, the water mitigation system control communication failed at ignition, and backup power failed nine seconds later, preventing remote activation of water spray cannons. Three secondary explosions occurred, with the largest at 4:22 a.m. when vessel V-1 ruptured, ejecting a 38,000-pound fragment across the Schuylkill River. Manual activation of the water pump at 4:39 a.m. allowed HF mitigation spraying. The fire was extinguished on June 22. Total HF release was estimated at 5,239 pounds, with 3,271 pounds released to atmosphere. Approximately 676,000 pounds of hydrocarbons released, 608,000 pounds combusted. Property damage estimated at $750 million, ranking as the third-largest refinery loss worldwide since 1974. Five workers and one firefighter sustained minor injuries. No off-site fatalities reported.
incident__event__recommendations: EPA: Develop program prioritizing inspections of refinery HF alkylation units under National Compliance Initiative; verify compliance with API RP 751 special emphasis inspection program for accelerated corrosion; protect safety-critical safeguards from fire and explosion hazards; install remotely-operated emergency isolation valves on HF and hydrocarbon vessels; EPA: Revise 40 C.F.R. Part 68 to require petroleum refineries with HF alkylation units to conduct safer technology and alternatives analysis (STAA) every 5 years; EPA: Initiate prioritization of hydrofluoric acid as High-Priority Substance under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation; API: Update API RP 751 to require protection of critical safeguards and control system components from fire and explosion hazards; require remotely-operated emergency isolation valves on HF and hydrocarbon vessels; ASTM International: Revise ASTM A234 to incorporate supplementary requirements for piping in HF service as defined in ASTM A106 version 19a

incident__event__top_event: Gas well blowout and rig fire
incident__event__summary: On January 22, 2018, a blowout and rig fire occurred at Pryor Trust Well 1H-9 in Pittsburg County, Oklahoma. The fire killed five workers inside the driller's cabin from thermal burn injuries and smoke/soot inhalation. The blowout occurred approximately 3.5 hours after tripping drill pipe out of the well. Root causes included failure of the primary barrier (hydrostatic pressure from drilling mud) and secondary barrier (human detection of influx and BOP activation). Contributing factors included underbalanced operations performed without proper planning or equipment, inadequate training on electronic trip sheets, equipment misalignment causing data interpretation errors, failure to identify surface pressure, miscalculated weighted pill, deactivated alarm system, lack of flow checks, inadequate driller training in influx detection, and ineffective safety management system. The BOP failed to close after the fire because hydraulic control hoses burned and leaked, depleting accumulator pressure.
incident__event__recommendations: OSHA should establish drilling-specific regulatory standards for onshore drilling safety; API should develop industry standards for alarm management in drilling operations; API should develop industry guidance on tripping procedures for underbalanced operations; Patterson-UTI should implement effective monitoring of policy implementation including flow checks; Patterson-UTI should test drillers' influx detection skills through simulated pit gains; Patterson-UTI should improve driller training on electronic trip sheets; Red Mountain Operating should specify required barriers during operations and response procedures; IADC should develop tripping guidance for underbalanced operations; Pason Systems should improve alarm system design for different operating states; NOV should improve BOP design and hydraulic line protection; State of Oklahoma should establish drilling safety regulations

incident__event__summary: On November 4, 2009, a sudden and complete rupture occurred in the 10-inch diameter discharge line at the exit of the South Reactor (R30101) in the Mobil Distillate Dewaxing Unit (MDDW) during hot hydrogen catalyst regeneration. The rupture released approximately 175.3 kg of hydrogen-rich gas mixture at 642 psig and 785°F. The released gas ignited approximately 0.66 seconds after rupture, causing an explosion that damaged residential homes in the adjacent community. Metallurgical analysis revealed wall thinning from the inside diameter through the weld metal and base metal of the 10-inch straight segment, consistent with sulfidation corrosion. The piping had been in service since 1993 with no documented wall thickness monitoring between installation and the incident.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement corrosion monitoring program for low-alloy carbon steel piping in sulfidation-prone service; Establish baseline wall thickness measurements and periodic ultrasonic inspection intervals; Consider material upgrades to molybdenum-containing alloys or stainless steel in high-temperature sulfur-containing service; Review process stream chemistry and operating conditions for sulfidation corrosion risk; Document original pipe specifications and maintain inspection records

incident__event__incident_type: Hot work incident involving explosive atmosphere in piping
incident__event__summary: On August 12, 2016, at approximately 8:15 PM CDT, a flash fire and explosion occurred while L-Con contractor was welding a piping segment at the Sunoco Nederland crude oil terminal. The 30-inch diameter piping section contained residual crude oil and was isolated using CARBER isolation tools. During internal welding of flange A, vapor inside the piping ignited, causing a pressure spike that ejected the isolation tools from both ends of the pipe. The explosion and flash fire injured seven contractors (four welders, one fire watch, two others), with four requiring hospitalization for burns, bone fractures, and blast-related injuries. The incident was caused by hot work being conducted on equipment containing an explosive atmosphere without proper clearing or inerting of flammable material.
incident__event__recommendations: Equipment containing flammable materials must be purged, made safe, or decontaminated prior to hot work; Hot work activities must not proceed if flammable vapors are present above 10% LEL; Continuous atmospheric monitoring for flammable gases is preferred during hot work; Alternative precautions such as inert gas purging must be established when piping cannot be fully decontaminated; Isolation devices must be continuously monitored until servicing work is completed; Formal JHA must be performed each time a piping mechanical seal device is applied; Mechanical seal devices should only be used after available means of vapor removal have been employed

incident__event__top_event: Catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E
incident__event__incident_type: Loss of Containment - Explosion and Fire
incident__event__summary: On April 2, 2010, the Tesoro Anacortes Refinery experienced a catastrophic rupture of heat exchanger E-6600E in the Naphtha Hydrotreater (NHT) unit. The carbon steel heat exchanger failed due to High Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA), releasing highly flammable hydrogen and naphtha at temperatures exceeding 500°F. The release ignited, causing an explosion and fire that burned for over three hours. Seven Tesoro employees (one shift supervisor and six operators) working in the immediate vicinity were fatally injured. This was the largest fatal incident at a US petroleum refinery since the BP Texas City accident in March 2005.
incident__event__recommendations: EPA should enforce inherently safer design principles in petroleum refineries; Washington State Legislature should strengthen PSM regulations and regulatory oversight; Washington State DOSH should enhance inspection programs and competency; API should revise RP 941 to include minimum requirements and accurate HTHA guidance; API should revise RP 581 to require verification of actual operating conditions; Tesoro should implement inherently safer design using high chromium steels; Tesoro should improve Process Hazard Analysis procedures and effectiveness; Tesoro should enhance control of nonroutine hazardous work; United Steelworkers should participate in process safety oversight

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Sulfuric Acid Release
incident__event__summary: On February 12, 2014, two Tesoro Martinez refinery employees suffered first and second degree chemical burns while working to return a sulfuric acid sampling system to service following nonroutine maintenance. Concentrated sulfuric acid sprayed the workers following a mechanical integrity failure of a ¾-inch tubing connector adjacent to the acid sampling station. The tubing came apart at the connector when operators opened a valve to return the acid sampling system to service. Acid continued to spray from a nearly 100,000-gallon acid settler for over two hours, resulting in the release of approximately 84,000 pounds of sulfuric acid. Both workers were transported to a burn center and neither returned to work for over 150 days. The incident was caused by insufficient tightening of the compression joint. The CSB investigation found that weak process safety culture at the refinery, including failure to implement inherently safer design, inadequate PPE availability, poor maintenance practices, and failure to learn from previous incidents, were causal to the incident.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement inherently safer sulfuric acid sampling system design with closed-loop systems; Ensure all required PPE is available and compatible for workers; Strengthen process hazard analysis to adequately evaluate sampling system hazards; Implement robust leak-testing procedures for tubing following repairs; Improve maintenance program planning, documentation, and communication; Strengthen process safety culture through management commitment and worker engagement; Implement effective corrective actions from safety culture assessments; Improve communication and implementation of lessons learned from previous incidents; Enhance regulatory oversight through proactive and preventive inspections; Use leading and lagging process safety indicators to identify impending serious incidents

incident__event__summary: A dangerous substance known as popcorn polymer accumulated in a temporary dead leg created when a process pump was taken out of service for maintenance at the Huntsman Chemical Plant in Port Neches, Texas. The popcorn polymer expanded and ruptured the piping, releasing butadiene. The butadiene release resulted in fires that burned for more than a month, causing $40 million in on-site property damage and $9 million in off-site property damage. Two CSB employees and a contractor reported minor injuries, and at least five local residents reported injuries. The incident led to a mandatory four-mile radius evacuation order affecting Port Neches, Groves, Nederland, and portions of Port Arthur.
incident__event__recommendations: Ensure that piping associated with out-of-service equipment in high-purity butadiene service be flushed monthly; Identify, control, or prevent all potential temporary dead legs within the unit; Implement effective safety management systems to identify when safety-critical procedures cannot be conducted as intended

incident__event__top_event: Molten salt eruption and steam explosion
incident__event__summary: On May 30, 2024, at 8:58 a.m., an explosion occurred at the TS USA liquid nitriding facility in Chattanooga, Tennessee, while employees were processing metal rollers with cavities. Water became trapped in a roller cavity due to a solidified salt plug blocking the drain hole. When the roller was reintroduced into the 800°F oxidizing salt bath, the trapped water boiled and rapidly expanded as steam, creating overpressure that ejected the roller's press fit end. The contact of water with the molten salt caused a steam explosion that erupted approximately 4,500 pounds of molten sodium hydroxide and sodium nitrate from the bath, engulfing a line operator and causing second- and third-degree chemical and thermal burns over 95 percent of his body. The operator died later that day at the hospital. Three other employees suffered minor burns. The eruption ignited multiple fires throughout the facility, resulting in approximately $1.3 million in property damage.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement physical protective barriers around molten salt baths to isolate employees from hazardous releases; Develop comprehensive safety management system including hazard analysis program, written operating procedures, training program, and incident investigation program; Establish position with specific expertise in safety management systems at each TS USA facility; Include physical protective barriers as standard design for liquid nitriding processes; Develop and implement effective knowledge management program for sharing information across HEF Groupe organization; Develop and implement comprehensive corporate governance program with regular audits and accountability tracking

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Hydrogen Chloride Release
incident__event__summary: On November 13, 2020, at 10:04 a.m., a graphite heat exchanger (AW234) fractured and released hydrogen chloride (HCl) gas at Wacker Polysilicon North America in Charleston, Tennessee. The fracture occurred when a pipe fitter apprentice inadvertently over-torqued bolts on a flange connection using a torque wrench set to 40 foot-pounds, when the manufacturer-recommended torque for the graphite-to-PTFE connection was 15 foot-pounds. Seven workers were present on the fifth-floor platform at the time of the release. Three workers from Pen Gulf, lacking full chemical-resistant suits and unable to access the single stairway due to the white HCl gas cloud, attempted to descend via piping on the side of the structure approximately 70 feet above ground. All three fell to the ground; one worker died from injuries sustained in the fall, and two others suffered serious injuries. The incident resulted in one fatality, two serious injuries, and $214,000 in property damage.
incident__event__recommendations: OSHA: Promulgate standard requiring coordination of simultaneous operations (SIMOP) involving multiple work groups including contractors; Wacker: Develop detailed maintenance procedures for torquing activities with clear specifications and visual differentiation; Wacker: Develop policy requirements for torquing activities on equipment containing hazardous energy; Wacker: Develop and implement formalized SIMOP program; Wacker: Install additional means of egress for elevated equipment platforms; TOSHA: Promulgate standard requiring SIMOP coordination; CCPS: Develop safety product on safe work practices including SIMOP guidelines; ICC: Modify International Building Code to address multiple means of egress from elevated equipment platforms; NFPA: Review Life Safety Code, Compressed Gases and Cryogenic Fluids Code, and Hazardous Materials Code to address egress from elevated structures containing hazardous materials

incident__event__summary: On January 24, 2020, a leak of propylene from a high velocity oxygen fuel (HVOF) thermal spray process resulted in an explosion at Watson Grinding and Manufacturing Co. facility in Houston, Texas. The leak was caused by a disconnected hose inside one of the facility's coating booths. The explosion fatally injured two employees and a member of the public, with two additional employees injured. The explosion damaged hundreds of nearby structures including homes and businesses.
incident__event__recommendations: Urge member companies that handle hazardous chemicals to share information with customers about safety issues and the need to develop and implement effective process safety management systems; Inform customers about CGA P-86, Guidelines for Process Safety Management, CCPS Guidelines for Risk Based Process Safety, or equivalent approaches

incident__event__summary: On January 29, 2020, at approximately 3:00 p.m., the Daniel H. Wendland 1-H well in Burleson County, Texas experienced a loss of well control during a workover operation to replace the tubing head. The well blowout released oil and gas which ignited within seconds, resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three contract workers and seriously injured a fourth. The incident resulted in an estimated $1 million in property damage. Root causes included inadequate well planning, ineffective well control barriers for underpressured reservoirs, and poor ignition source management.
incident__event__recommendations: Chesapeake Operating, L.L.C. should develop or revise policies incorporating API RP 59 recommendations regarding well planning, specifically including well history review in conjunction with workover well control planning; American Petroleum Institute should publish focused guidance on workover operations including completed wells in underpressured reservoirs, clarify that stabbing valves should not be considered as well control barriers, and emphasize that every well should have two well control barriers with at least one preventative barrier; OSHA should remove the exemption for oil and gas drilling and well servicing from the Control of Hazardous Energy standard (29 CFR 1910.147); OSHA should promulgate a new standard with prescriptive requirements for primary and secondary barriers, detailed written procedures, management of change requirements, risk assessments, RAGAGEP compliance, Well Construction Interface Documents, flow checks, employee participation, critical well information maintenance, and hazard analysis for onshore oil and gas drilling, production, and servicing/workover activities

incident__event__top_event: Fire and Detonation of Ammonium Nitrate
incident__event__summary: On April 17, 2013, at approximately 7:51 pm CDT, a fire and explosion occurred at the West Fertilizer Company facility in West, Texas. The facility stored between 40 and 60 tons of fertilizer grade ammonium nitrate (FGAN). The explosion fatally injured 12 emergency responders and 3 members of the public, with more than 260 injured. The blast completely destroyed the WFC facility and caused widespread damage to over 150 offsite buildings, including schools, a nursing home, and residential structures. The total insurance-related losses were estimated at approximately $230 million.
incident__event__recommendations: Implement inherently safer technology and alternative FGAN formulations; Establish automatic sprinkler systems in FGAN storage facilities; Improve federal and state regulations for FGAN storage and handling; Enhance emergency response training and pre-incident planning for hazardous materials; Implement land use planning and zoning regulations to separate hazardous chemical storage from populated areas; Improve insurance coverage requirements and loss control inspections; Enhance firefighter training on FGAN hazards and response procedures

incident__event__incident_type: Catastrophic equipment failure with explosion and fire
incident__event__summary: On June 13, 2013, propylene fractionator Reboiler B at the Williams Geismar Olefins Plant catastrophically ruptured and exploded, resulting in a boiling liquid expanding vapor explosion (BLEVE) and fire. The incident killed two Williams employees and injured 167 workers (3 Williams employees and 164 contractors). The reboiler, which had been offline for 16 months, contained liquid propane that had accumulated while isolated from its pressure relief device. When quench water valves were opened to troubleshoot a fouling problem in the operating reboiler, heat was introduced to the blocked-in standby reboiler, causing liquid thermal expansion and rapid overpressurization. The vessel failed at an estimated pressure of 674-1,212 psig, releasing over 30,000 pounds of flammable hydrocarbons. Root causes included deficiencies in Management of Change (MOC), Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR), and Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) programs, as well as inadequate overpressure protection and lack of equipment-specific operating procedures.
incident__event__recommendations: Strengthen existing safety management systems and adopt additional safety programs; Implement robust Management of Change practices to analyze hazards in the entire process affected by changes; Conduct thorough Pre-Startup Safety Reviews to verify effective implementation of design intent and proper field equipment installation; Develop equipment-specific operating procedures that address hazards of operation; Ensure adequate overpressure protection through pressure relief valves or administrative controls such as car seals; Install instrumentation to detect process fluid within standby equipment; Use more robust isolation methods such as blinds rather than relying solely on block valves; Establish strong organizational process safety culture with management commitment; Implement robust process safety management programs with continual vigilance; Apply hierarchy of controls when evaluating and selecting safeguards

incident__event__top_event: Loss of Containment - Vapor Cloud Explosion and Fire
incident__event__incident_type: Vessel Leak, Vapor Cloud Explosion, and Fire
incident__event__summary: On April 8, 2021, at approximately 12:02 a.m., a mixture of flammable naphtha solvent vapors and resin liquid became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway on Kettle 3 at the Yenkin-Majestic OPC Polymers resin plant in Columbus, Ohio. The release occurred after the kettle operator turned on the agitator, which caused rapid vaporization of solvent that had been added to the hot resin while the agitator was unexpectedly off. The flammable vapor cloud spread throughout the enclosed building and formed both inside and outside. Approximately two minutes later, at 12:04 a.m., the vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion and large fire. One employee was fatally injured from thermal injuries and inhalation of combustion products. Eight other employees were transported to hospitals with injuries including third-degree burns and limb fractures, with one requiring leg amputation. The incident caused over $90 million in property damage.
incident__event__recommendations: Update mechanical integrity procedures for all process vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, including pressure vessels not exceeding 15 psig, to adopt alteration guidance in API 510 or National Board Inspection Code Part 3; Assess and document applicable design, construction, and alteration standards for all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, adopting PIP VESLP001 Low-Pressure Welded Vessel Specification at minimum; Implement program to assess pressure vessels against updated RAGAGEP at least once every five years; Develop and implement written procedures to document and maintain records of all inspections, alterations, and maintenance/repairs on all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service; Demonstrate use of prevention through design using hierarchy of controls in future resin plant designs, prioritizing inherently safer design and engineering controls; Identify and document all equipment that could release flammable materials and install LEL detectors in accordance with CCPS guidelines; Ensure detection of hazardous conditions automatically triggers both visual and audible alarms to alert plant personnel; Develop and implement employee training on actions to take when alarms are activated, such as prompt evacuation; Develop and implement requirements for personnel to wear flame-resistant uniforms in all operating areas that process flammable chemicals; Update employee training material to include requirement for and purpose of PPE use; API to develop specific design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure process vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig; ASME to assist API in developing design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig
